topics.title: Biomolecules: Structure, Chemistry and Reactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT302 - BIOCHEMISTRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Biomolecules: Structure, Chemistry and Reactions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧬 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Biomolecules?

**Biomolecules** are organic compounds synthesized by living organisms that form the structural and functional foundation of all life. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCBBT302 – Biochemistry, Module 1)**, biomolecules are classified into four major macromolecular families:
**carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCBBT302, Module 1):** Students must be able to describe the **classification, structure (primary, secondary, tertiary, quaternary where applicable), chemical properties, and characteristic biochemical reactions** of each biomolecule family.

### 1.2 The "Building Block" Analogy

Think of a **biomolecule** like a **Lego structure**:

- The **individual Lego bricks** are the *monomers* (e.g., glucose, amino acids, fatty acids, nucleotides).
- The **completed Lego model** is the *macromolecule / polymer* (e.g., starch, protein, triglyceride, DNA).
- The **instruction manual** determining how the bricks are joined is the **covalent bond pattern** (glycosidic, peptide, ester, phosphodiester).

Just as a Lego model is constructed piece by piece according to a specific blueprint, cells assemble biomolecules **monomer by monomer** using enzyme-catalyzed reactions, creating polymers with precisely defined three-dimensional shapes that determine their biological role.

### 1.3 The Four Major Classes at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physiological Reference Values (Human, 37 °C, pH 7.4):**
> - **Plasma glucose:** ~**90–110 mg/dL** (fasting).
> - **Average protein size:** **10–1000 amino acid residues**.
> - **DNA double-helix diameter:** **2.0 nm** (20 Å).
> - **Cell membrane lipid bilayer thickness:** **~5 nm**.

| Class | Monomer | Biological Polymer | Key Functional Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbohydrates | Monosaccharides (e.g., glucose) | Polysaccharides (e.g., starch, glycogen) | Energy storage, structural support |
| Proteins | Amino acids | Polypeptides | Catalysis, transport, signaling |
| Lipids | Fatty acids + glycerol | Triglycerides, phospholipids | Energy reserve, membrane structure |
| Nucleic Acids | Nucleotides | DNA, RNA | Genetic information storage/transfer |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Carbohydrates — Chemistry, Structure, and Reactions

### 2.1.1 Definition and Classification
Carbohydrates are polyhydroxy aldehydes or polyhydroxy ketones, or compounds that yield these on hydrolysis. General empirical formula:

$$(CH_2O)_n$$

where *n* ≥ **3** for the smallest carbohydrate.

**Classification hierarchy:**
1. **Monosaccharides** (e.g., glucose, fructose, galactose) — single units.
2. **Disaccharides** (e.g., sucrose, maltose, lactose) — two units joined by a glycosidic bond.
3. **Oligosaccharides** — 3–10 units.
4. **Polysaccharides** — >10 units (e.g., starch, glycogen, cellulose, chitin).

### 2.1.2 Stereochemistry and the D/L System
- The reference sugar is **D-glyceraldehyde**.
- Sugars are designated **D** if the —OH on the **highest-numbered chiral carbon** (the carbon farthest from the carbonyl) is on the **right** in a Fischer projection.

### 2.1.3 Cyclization: Haworth Projection
- Aldoses (e.g., glucose) and ketoses (e.g., fructose) cyclize in solution.
- **Glucose** forms a 6-membered **pyranose** ring; **fructose** forms a 5-membered **furanose** ring.
- The cyclization creates a new chiral center at **C1 (anomeric carbon)**, producing **α** (—OH down) and **β** (—OH up) anomers in Haworth representation.
- This interconversion in solution is called **mutarotation**.

### 2.1.4 Key Reactions of Carbohydrates

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these reactions matter for KTU exams:** Carbohydrate identification tests in practical biochemistry (Module 1 lab) are routinely asked as 3-mark questions in ESE.

| Reaction / Test | Reagent | Positive Result | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Molisch test | α-naphthol + conc. H₂SO₄ | Purple ring at junction | General carbohydrate test |
| Benedict's / Fehling's | Cu²⁺ (alkaline tartrate) | Brick-red Cu₂O precipitate | Detects **reducing sugars** |
| Barfoed's test | Cu²⁺ (acidic) | Red ppt. (monosaccharides < 1 min) | Differentiates mono- from disaccharides |
| Iodine test | I₂/KI | Blue-black (starch), red-brown (glycogen) | Detects polysaccharides |
| Osazone formation | Phenylhydrazine | Yellow needle-shaped crystals | Identifies sugars by crystal shape |
| Seliwanoff's test | Resorcinol + HCl | Cherry-red (ketose), faint pink (aldose) | Distinguishes keto- from aldoses |

**Glycosidic Bond Formation** (e.g., in maltose):

$$\text{Glucose-OH} + \text{HO-Glucose} \xrightarrow{\text{condensation}} \text{Glucose-O-Glucose} + H_2O$$

The C1 —OH of one glucose condenses with the C4 —OH of another, releasing a water molecule. The bond (C1–O–C4) is the **α-1,4-glycosidic bond** in maltose.

---

## 2.2 Amino Acids and Proteins

### 2.2.1 General Structure of an α-Amino Acid

At physiological pH (~7.4), amino acids exist predominantly as **zwitterions**:

$$\text{H}_3\text{N}^+ - \overset{\overset{\displaystyle R}{|}}{C} - COO^-$$

**Standard α-amino acid backbone:**

$$\text{NH}_2 - \underset{\underset{\displaystyle H}{|}}{C_{\alpha}} - \text{COOH} \quad (+ \text{ side chain } R)$$

- **20 standard amino acids** are encoded by the genetic code.
- They are differentiated solely by the **R (side-chain) group**.

### 2.2.2 Classification by Side Chain Polarity
- **Nonpolar (hydrophobic):** Gly, Ala, Val, Leu, Ile, Pro, Phe, Trp, Met.
- **Polar uncharged:** Ser, Thr, Cys, Tyr, Asn, Gln.
- **Positively charged (basic):** Lys, Arg, His.
- **Negatively charged (acidic):** Asp, Glu.

### 2.2.3 Peptide Bond Formation
A **peptide bond** is an amide linkage formed by condensation between the **α-carboxyl group of one amino acid** and the **α-amino group of the next**, releasing water:

$$\text{H}_2\text{N-CHR}_1\text{-COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{N-CHR}_2\text{-COOH} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{N-CHR}_1\text{-CO-NH-CHR}_2\text{-COOH} + H_2O$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The peptide bond has **partial double-bond character** (≈40% double bond) due to resonance, making it **planar and rigid**. This is a frequent KTU 14-mark question.

### 2.2.4 Hierarchical Levels of Protein Structure

| Level | Description | Stabilizing Interactions | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary (1°)** | Linear sequence of amino acids joined by peptide bonds | Covalent peptide bonds | Insulin A-chain (21 residues) |
| **Secondary (2°)** | Local regular folding: α-helix or β-pleated sheet | Hydrogen bonds (backbone N–H···O=C) | Myoglobin α-helices |
| **Tertiary (3°)** | Overall 3D fold of a single polypeptide chain | Hydrophobic interactions, H-bonds, ionic (salt bridges), disulfide bonds (S–S) | Lysozyme |
| **Quaternary (4°)** | Association of multiple polypeptide subunits | Same as 3° + subunit–subunit interfaces | Hemoglobin (2α + 2β) |

### 2.2.5 Key Color Reactions of Proteins

| Test | Reagent | Detects | Positive Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| Biuret | CuSO₄ + NaOH | Peptide bonds (≥2) | Violet/purple color |
| Ninhydrin | Ninhydrin reagent | Free α-amino groups | Ruhemann's purple (R-ninhydrin complex) |
| Xanthoproteic | Conc. HNO₃ | Aromatic AAs (Tyr, Trp, Phe) | Yellow → orange (on adding alkali) |
| Millon's | Hg²⁺ in HNO₃ | Phenolic –OH (Tyrosine) | Red precipitate |
| Hopkins–Cole | Glyoxylic acid | Tryptophan | Violet ring |

---

## 2.3 Lipids — Chemistry and Reactions

### 2.3.1 Definition
Lipids are a **heterogeneous group of hydrophobic / amphipathic** biological molecules insoluble in water but soluble in **nonpolar organic solvents** (chloroform, ether, benzene).

### 2.3.2 Fatty Acid — The Core Building Block
A fatty acid is a **carboxylic acid with a long hydrocarbon chain (typically 4–28 carbons)**.

**Saponification reaction** (alkaline hydrolysis of a triglyceride):

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,NaOH \longrightarrow \text{Glycerol} + 3\,RCOO^-Na^+ \text{ (soap)}$$

The sodium/potassium salts of long-chain fatty acids are **soaps**; this reaction is the basis of soap manufacture.

**Iodine number (I.N.)** — measures degree of unsaturation:

$$\text{Iodine Number} = \frac{\text{mass of I}_2 \text{ (g) absorbed}}{100 \text{ g of fat}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **High Iodine Number = high unsaturation = liquid (oily) fat at room temperature** (e.g., linseed oil I.N. ≈ 170). **Low Iodine Number = saturated = solid fat** (e.g., butter I.N. ≈ 30).

**Acid number (A.N.)** — measures free fatty acid content:

$$\text{Acid Number} = \frac{\text{mass of KOH (mg) needed to neutralize 1 g of fat}}$$

### 2.3.3 Major Lipid Classes

| Class | Backbone | Key Feature | Biological Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simple lipids: Triglycerides | Glycerol + 3 fatty acids (ester bonds) | Energy storage (9 kcal/g) | Adipose tissue fat |
| Waxes | Long-chain alcohol + fatty acid | Waterproofing | Beeswax, cutin |
| Phospholipids | Glycerol + 2 FA + phosphate + alcohol | Amphipathic; membrane bilayers | Phosphatidylcholine (lecithin) |
| Sphingolipids | Sphingosine backbone | Myelin sheath, nerve signaling | Sphingomyelin, cerebrosides |
| Steroids | Four fused rings (cyclopentanoperhydrophenanthrene) | Hormones, membrane fluidity | Cholesterol, testosterone |
| Terpenes | Isoprene (C₅) units | Pigments, vitamins, scents | β-carotene, menthol |

### 2.3.4 Phospholipid Bilayer Architecture
Phospholipids are **amphipathic**: a **polar/hydrophilic head** (phosphate + alcohol) and **two nonpolar/hydrophobic tails** (fatty acids). In aqueous medium, they self-assemble into a **bilayer** — the structural basis of all biological membranes.

---

## 2.4 Nucleic Acids — Chemistry and Structure

### 2.4.1 Building Block: The Nucleotide
A **nucleotide** = **nitrogenous base + pentose sugar + phosphate group(s)**.

| Component | DNA | RNA |
|---|---|---|
| Sugar | 2′-deoxyribose | Ribose |
| Bases — purines | Adenine (A), Guanine (G) | Adenine (A), Guanine (G) |
| Bases — pyrimidines | Cytosine (C), Thymine (T) | Cytosine (C), Uracil (U) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU memory hook:** **"DNA uses T; RNA uses U."** This is a near-universal 1-mark question.

### 2.4.2 Phosphodiester Bond
Nucleotides are joined by a **3′→5′ phosphodiester bond**, formed between the **3′-OH of one sugar** and the **5′-phosphate of the next**, releasing water (condensation).

### 2.4.3 Chargaff's Rules
In double-stranded DNA:

$$[A] = [T] \quad \text{and} \quad [G] = [C]$$

which is the chemical basis of the **A=T (2 H-bonds)** and **G≡C (3 H-bonds)** Watson–Crick base pairing.

### 2.4.4 Watson–Crick Base Pairing Geometry

| Base pair | Hydrogen bonds | Purine | Pyrimidine |
|---|---|---|---|
| A = T | **2** | Adenine | Thymine |
| G ≡ C | **3** | Guanine | Cytosine |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why GC pairs are stronger:** The extra H-bond makes G–C pairs thermodynamically more stable. DNA regions rich in GC require higher temperatures to denature ("melt"). For every 1% increase in GC content, **Tm increases by ~0.4 °C** (an approximation used in molecular biology primers design).

### 2.4.5 DNA Double-Helix (B-form) Key Parameters
- **Helix diameter:** 2.0 nm = **20 Å**.
- **Rise per base pair:** **3.4 Å (0.34 nm)**.
- **Pitch per turn:** **34 Å (3.4 nm) = 10 base pairs per turn**.
- **Major and minor grooves** provide docking sites for regulatory proteins.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formulas — Module 1, PCBBT302.** Memorize these for Part A 3-mark questions.

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(CH_2O)_n$ | Empirical formula of carbohydrates | — |
| 2 | $\text{Iodine Number} = \frac{m_{I_2}}{100\,g \text{ fat}}$ | Degree of unsaturation of lipid | g I₂ / 100 g |
| 3 | $\text{Acid Number} = \frac{m_{KOH}}{1\,g \text{ fat}}$ | Free fatty acid content | mg KOH / g |
| 4 | $\text{Saponification Number} = \frac{m_{KOH}}{1\,g \text{ fat}}$ | Average FA chain length | mg KOH / g |
| 5 | $[\alpha]_D^{20} = \frac{\alpha_{obs}}{l \cdot c}$ | Specific rotation (optical activity) | deg·mL·g⁻¹·dm⁻¹ |
| 6 | $\text{pI} = \frac{pK_{a1} + pK_{a2}}{2}$ | Isoelectric point (neutral AA) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Beer–Lambert: } A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ | UV absorbance of nucleic acids / proteins | absorbance (AU) |
| 8 | $T_m = 69.3 + 0.41 \cdot (\%GC)$ | DNA melting temperature (Wallace rule) | °C |
| 9 | $\%GC = \frac{G+C}{A+T+G+C} \times 100$ | GC content of DNA | % |
| 10 | $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_{eq}$ | Spontaneity of biochemical reaction | kJ·mol⁻¹ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔢 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Isoelectric Point (pI) of a Neutral Amino Acid

> **Problem:** Amino acid glycine has two dissociable protons: the α-COOH (pKₐ₁ = 2.34) and the α-NH₃⁺ (pKₐ₂ = 9.60). Derive an expression for the **isoelectric point (pI)** and calculate it.

### Step 1 — Identify the relevant equilibrium

Glycine in solution at low pH exists as the fully protonated cation H₃N⁺–CH₂–COOH. As pH rises, two sequential deprotonations occur:

$$H_3N^+-CH_2-COOH \xrightleftharpoons{pK_{a1}=2.34} H_3N^+-CH_2-COO^- \xrightleftharpoons{pK_{a2}=9.60} H_2N-CH_2-COO^-$$

| Species | Form | Net charge |
|---|---|---|
| Cation (fully protonated) | H₃N⁺–CH₂–COOH | +1 |
| Zwitterion | H₃N⁺–CH₂–COO⁻ | 0 |
| Anion (fully deprotonated) | H₂N–CH₂–COO⁻ | −1 |

### Step 2 — Apply the Henderson–Hasselbalch Equation

For the first dissociation (COOH → COO⁻):

$$pH = pK_{a1} + \log \frac{[H_3N^+-CH_2-COO^-]}{[H_3N^+-CH_2-COOH]}$$

For the second dissociation (NH₃⁺ → NH₂):

$$pH = pK_{a2} + \log \frac{[H_2N-CH_2-COO^-]}{[H_3N^+-CH_2-COO^-]}$$

### Step 3 — Define the Isoelectric Point
At the **isoelectric point (pI)**, the net charge on the molecule is **zero**, i.e., the concentrations of the cation and the anion are equal:

$$[H_3N^+-CH_2-COOH] = [H_2N-CH_2-COO^-]$$

### Step 4 — Algebraic Derivation of pI
Add the two Henderson–Hasselbalch equations:

$$2\,pH = pK_{a1} + pK_{a2} + \log \frac{[Zwitterion]^2}{[Cation][Anion]}$$

At pI, the logarithmic term vanishes (since [Cation] = [Anion]), giving:

$$2\,pI = pK_{a1} + pK_{a2}$$

$$\boxed{\,pI = \dfrac{pK_{a1} + pK_{a2}}{2}\,}$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Evaluation
For glycine (pKₐ₁ = 2.34, pKₐ₂ = 9.60):

$$pI = \dfrac{2.34 + 9.60}{2} = \dfrac{11.94}{2} = 5.97$$

**Conclusion:** Glycine's pI ≈ **5.97** — at this pH, glycine carries zero net charge and will not migrate in an electric field (the basis of **electrophoresis** and **ion-exchange chromatography**).

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Melting Temperature (Tₘ) of Double-Stranded DNA

> **Problem:** A short DNA primer has the following nucleotide composition: A = 25, T = 25, G = 30, C = 30. Estimate Tₘ using the **Wallace rule for short oligos** and the **Marmur–Schildkraut equation for long DNA**, and compare.

### Step 1 — Compute %GC

$$\%GC = \frac{G + C}{A + T + G + C} \times 100 = \frac{30 + 30}{25 + 25 + 30 + 30} \times 100 = \frac{60}{110} \times 100 \approx 54.55\%$$

### Step 2 — Apply Wallace Rule (valid for oligos < 25 bp)

$$T_m = 2\,^\circ C \cdot (A + T) + 4\,^\circ C \cdot (G + C)$$

$$T_m = 2(50) + 4(60) = 100 + 240 = 340\,^\circ C$$

This is unreasonably high for a real oligonucleotide — Wallace's rule was originally designed for very short probes (≤ 14 bp); for typical primers (18–25 bp), a more appropriate formula is used.

### Step 3 — Apply a More Realistic Primer Tₘ Formula (≤ 25 bp)

For primers of length N with %GC:

$$T_m = 64.9 + 41 \cdot \frac{G + C - 16.4}{N}$$

With N = 110 nt, %GC = 54.55:

$$T_m = 64.9 + 41 \cdot \frac{60 - 16.4}{110} = 64.9 + 41 \cdot \frac{43.6}{110} = 64.9 + 41 \cdot 0.3964 \approx 64.9 + 16.25 \approx 81.1\,^\circ C$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Marmur–Schildkraut Equation (long DNA, > 1000 bp)

$$T_m = 69.3 + 0.41 \cdot (\%GC) = 69.3 + 0.41 \cdot (54.55) = 69.3 + 22.37 = 91.67\,^\circ C$$

**Conclusion:** The simple linear formula $T_m = 69.3 + 0.41\,(\%GC)$ is appropriate for genomic DNA; for primers, a length-adjusted equation is essential. **Wallace's rule $2(A+T) + 4(G+C)$ should only be used for probes ≤ 14 bp.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Acid Value Calculation

> **Problem:** 5.0 g of an edible oil is titrated against standardized KOH. 12.5 mL of 0.1 N KOH is required to reach the phenolphthalein endpoint. Calculate the **Acid Value**.

### Step 1 — Standard Formula

$$\text{Acid Value} = \frac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W}$$

where V = volume of KOH (mL), N = normality of KOH (eq/L), W = mass of oil (g), 56.1 = equivalent weight of KOH (g/eq).

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$\text{Acid Value} = \frac{12.5 \times 0.1 \times 56.1}{5.0} = \frac{70.125}{5.0} = 14.025 \text{ mg KOH / g}$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation
A typical refined edible oil has an acid value of **< 4 mg KOH/g**. A value of 14 indicates the oil is **significantly hydrolyzed / rancid** and is unfit for human consumption. This is a standard test under **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards) / FSSAI** for edible oil quality control.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study of the Four Biomolecule Families

| Property | Carbohydrates | Proteins | Lipids | Nucleic Acids |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Monomer** | Monosaccharide | α-Amino acid | Fatty acid + glycerol | Nucleotide |
| **Polymer** | Polysaccharide | Polypeptide | Triglyceride / phospholipid | Polynucleotide |
| **Linkage** | Glycosidic (C–O–C) | Peptide (C–N) | Ester (C–O–C=O) | Phosphodiester (C–O–P–O–C) |
| **Elemental composition** | C, H, O | C, H, O, N (+S in Cys/Met) | C, H, O (+P in phospholipids) | C, H, O, N, P |
| **Solubility in water** | High (mono-, di-) | Variable | Insoluble | Highly soluble (Na salt) |
| **Main test** | Molisch, Benedict's | Biuret, Ninhydrin | Saponification, I₂ number | Dische, Orcinol |
| **Energy yield** | 4 kcal/g | 4 kcal/g | 9 kcal/g | None (catalytic) |
| **Biological role** | Energy + structure | Catalysis, transport, signaling | Energy + membrane | Genetic information |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Iodine Number from Hydrogenation Stoichiometry

> **Problem:** 1.0 g of linseed oil is hydrogenated completely using H₂ in the presence of Pt catalyst. The H₂ consumed is **2.55 × 10⁻³ mol**. Calculate the Iodine Number.

### Step 1 — Iodine–Hydrogen Equivalence
Catalytic hydrogenation adds one H₂ across each C=C double bond. I₂ addition across a C=C consumes **one mole of I₂ per double bond**. Therefore, **1 mole H₂ ≡ 1 mole I₂** per double bond.

### Step 2 — Mass of I₂ Equivalent

$$m_{I_2} = n_{H_2} \times M_{I_2} = 2.55 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol} \times 254\,\text{g/mol} = 0.6477\,\text{g}$$

### Step 3 — Normalize to 100 g of Fat

$$\text{Iodine Number} = \frac{0.6477 \times 100}{1.0} = 64.77 \approx 65$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** An I.N. ≈ 65 indicates a **moderately unsaturated** oil. (Pure linseed oil typically has I.N. ≈ 170–200; the discrepancy here suggests the sample was partially hydrogenated or blended.)

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Architecture of Biomolecular Classes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BIOMOLECULES]:::root --> B[CARBOHYDRATES]
    A --> C[PROTEINS]
    A --> D[LIPIDS]
    A --> E[NUCLEIC ACIDS]

    B --> B1[Monosaccharides]
    B --> B2[Disaccharides]
    B --> B3[Polysaccharides]

    B1 --> B1b[Fructose]
    B1 --> B1c[Galactose]

    B2 --> B2c[Lactose]

    B3 --> B3b[Glycogen]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Peptide Bond Formation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Amino Acid 1: NH2-CHR1-COOH] -->|alpha carboxyl activates| X
    A2[Amino Acid 2: NH2-CHR2-COOH] -->|alpha amino attacks| X
    X[Condensation Reaction] -->|releases H2O| Y[Peptide Bond C-N]
    Y --> Z1[1 bond: Dipeptide]
    Z1 --> Z2[2-10 bonds: Oligopeptide]
    Z2 --> Z3[10+ bonds: Polypeptide]
    Z3 --> Z4[Functional Protein: Tertiary Fold]
    Z4 --> Z5[Quaternary Assembly: Multimer]

    classDef react fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457,color:#AD1457
    classDef prod fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20
    class A1,A2,X react
    class Y,Z1,Z2,Z3,Z4,Z5 prod
```

## 4.3 Workflow — DNA Double Helix Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D[DNA Double Helix: B form] -->|Transcription| M[mRNA]
    M -->|Translation at Ribosome| P[Polypeptide Chain]
    P -->|Folding| F[Functional Protein]
    F -->|Drives cellular processes| OUT[Metabolism, Structure, Signaling]

    D -->|Replication| D2[Identical Daughter DNA]

    classDef dna fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#4A148C
    classDef rna fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#BF360C
    classDef prot fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20
    classDef out fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#0D47A1

    class D,D2 dna
    class M rna
    class P,F prot
    class OUT out
```

## 4.4 Block Architecture — Phospholipid Bilayer (Membrane Foundation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TopLayer[Extracellular Side - Outer Leaflet]
        H1[Polar Phosphate Head]:::head
        T1[H1 connects to FA Tail 1]:::tail
        T2[H1 connects to FA Tail 2]:::tail
    end

    subgraph MidLayer[Hydrophobic Core]
        FA1[Saturated FA chain]:::tail
        FA2[Unsaturated FA chain kinked]:::tail
    end

    subgraph BotLayer[Cytoplasmic Side - Inner Leaflet]
        H2[Polar Phosphate Head]:::head
        T3[H2 connects to FA Tail 1]:::tail
        T4[H2 connects to FA Tail 2]:::tail
    end

    H1 --- FA1
    H1 --- FA2
    FA1 --- T3
    FA2 --- T4
    H2 --- T3
    H2 --- T4

    classDef head fill:#FFEB3B,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000000
    classDef tail fill:#90A4AE,stroke:#37474F,color:#FFFFFF
    class H1,H2 head
    class T1,T2,T3,T4,FA1,FA2 tail
```

## 4.5 Conceptual Map — Hierarchical Protein Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Primary Structure: AA sequence] -->|H-bonds| P2[Secondary: alpha-helix or beta-sheet]
    P2 -->|hydrophobic ionic disulfide| P3[Tertiary: 3D fold of one chain]
    P3 -->|subunit assembly| P4[Quaternary: multimer]
    P1 -.->|denaturation breaks| P0[Unfolded Random Coil]
    P2 -.->|denaturation breaks| P0
    P3 -.->|denaturation breaks| P0
    P4 -.->|denaturation breaks| P0
    P0 -->|renaturation possible| P3

    classDef lvl fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#0D47A1
    classDef denat fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#B71C1C
    class P1,P2,P3,P4 lvl
    class P0 denat
```

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
**Define the following terms with one example each:**
(a) **Reducing sugar**
(b) **Essential amino acid**
(c) **Saturated fatty acid**

**Model Answer:**

> **(a) Reducing sugar:** A carbohydrate that possesses a **free anomeric hydroxyl group (—OH at C1 in aldoses or C2 in ketoses)** and can therefore reduce Cu²⁺ (Benedict's / Fehling's reagent) to Cu⁺ (brick-red Cu₂O precipitate) in alkaline solution. **Example:** Glucose.
>
> **(b) Essential amino acid:** An amino acid that **cannot be synthesized by the human body** in adequate quantities and must be obtained from the diet. **Example:** Valine (one of the 9 essential amino acids for adults: Val, Leu, Ile, Phe, Trp, Met, Thr, Lys, His).
>
> **(c) Saturated fatty acid:** A fatty acid in which **all carbon–carbon bonds in the hydrocarbon chain are single bonds** (no C=C double bonds). The chain is "saturated" with hydrogen. **Example:** Stearic acid (CH₃(CH₂)₁₆COOH, C18:0).

### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**Differentiate between the following with one example each:**
(a) **DNA and RNA** (sugar, base, structure, location)
(b) **Saponification number and Iodine number**

**Model Answer:**

> **(a) DNA vs RNA:**

| Feature | DNA | RNA |
|---|---|---|
| Sugar | 2′-deoxyribose | Ribose (2′-OH present) |
| Bases | A, T, G, C | A, U, G, C (Uracil replaces Thymine) |
| Structure | Double-stranded helix | Usually single-stranded |
| Location | Nucleus, mitochondria, chloroplasts | Nucleolus, cytoplasm, ribosomes |
| Example | Human genomic DNA on chr. 1 | Messenger RNA (mRNA) |

> **(b) Saponification number vs Iodine number:**

| Feature | Saponification Number | Iodine Number |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | mg KOH required to saponify 1 g of fat | g of I₂ absorbed by 100 g of fat |
| Indicates | Average chain length of fatty acids (high = short chains) | Degree of unsaturation (high = many C=C) |
| High in | Coconut oil (~250) | Linseed oil (~170) |
| Low in | Stearic acid-rich fats | Fully saturated fats |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format)

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Carbohydrates and Proteins Focus
**[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **classification of carbohydrates** with suitable examples. Explain **mutarotation** with reference to D-glucose. Add a note on the **structure of maltose and sucrose**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **four levels of protein structure** with a clear diagram. Describe the **biuret test** and its chemical basis.

---

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**1. Classification of carbohydrates** (3 marks):

Carbohydrates are classified based on the number of sugar units (degree of polymerization) and the complexity of the molecule:

- **Monosaccharides** — single sugar units, cannot be hydrolysed further. **Examples:** Glucose (aldohexose, C₆H₁₂O₆), Fructose (ketohexose), Galactose, Ribose.
- **Disaccharides** — two monosaccharide units joined by a glycosidic bond. **Examples:** Maltose (α-1,4), Sucrose (α-1,β-2), Lactose (β-1,4).
- **Oligosaccharides** — 3–10 monosaccharide units. **Examples:** Raffinose (trisaccharide).
- **Polysaccharides** — many monosaccharide units (>10), may be **homopolysaccharides** (one type of sugar) or **heteropolysaccharides** (more than one type). **Examples:** Starch, glycogen, cellulose (homopolysaccharides); hyaluronic acid (heteropolysaccharide).

**2. Mutarotation in D-glucose** (2 marks):

D-glucose in solution cyclizes to form a **6-membered pyranose ring** via an intramolecular reaction between the **C1 aldehyde** and the **C5 hydroxyl group**. This creates a new chiral center at **C1 (anomeric carbon)**, giving rise to two anomers:
- **α-D-glucopyranose** — —OH at C1 is **axial (down)** in Haworth projection; specific rotation = **+112°**.
- **β-D-glucopyranose** — —OH at C1 is **equatorial (up)** in Haworth projection; specific rotation = **+18.7°**.

When either pure anomer is dissolved in water, the optical rotation changes with time until an **equilibrium value of +52.5°** is reached. This phenomenon is called **mutarotation**, caused by the **interconversion of α and β anomers via the open-chain aldehyde form** (the **acyclic intermediate**). It is catalyzed by acid or base.

**3. Structures of maltose and sucrose** (2 marks):

- **Maltose** (α-D-glucopyranosyl-(1→4)-D-glucopyranose): Two glucose units joined by an **α-1,4-glycosidic bond**. The second glucose retains a **free anomeric —OH** (reducing sugar). Maltose is the repeating unit of starch (amylose component).
- **Sucrose** (α-D-glucopyranosyl-(1→2)-β-D-fructofuranoside): One **α-D-glucose** linked to one **β-D-fructose** via a **1→2 glycosidic bond** involving **both anomeric carbons**. Since neither anomeric carbon has a free —OH, sucrose is a **non-reducing sugar**. It is the common table sugar (cane / beet sugar).

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**1. Four levels of protein structure** (5 marks):

(See **SECTION 4.5** Mermaid diagram for visual reference.)

- **Primary (1°) structure:** The **linear sequence** of amino acids in a polypeptide chain, held together by **covalent peptide bonds**. Sequence determines all higher-order structures. Theoretically denaturation does not break peptide bonds. **Example:** Bovine insulin (51 residues, two chains A and B).
- **Secondary (2°) structure:** Local regular conformations stabilized by **hydrogen bonds** between the **backbone N–H of one peptide bond** and the **C=O of another**. Two main types: **α-helix** (right-handed, 3.6 residues/turn, H-bond between residue *i* and *i+4*) and **β-pleated sheet** (parallel or antiparallel strands). **Example:** Keratin (α-helix), silk fibroin (β-sheet).
- **Tertiary (3°) structure:** The **overall 3D fold** of a single polypeptide chain, stabilized by R-group interactions: **hydrophobic interactions, hydrogen bonds, ionic (salt bridges), van der Waals, and covalent disulfide bonds (S–S between two Cys residues)**. **Example:** Myoglobin (single chain, 8 α-helices, one heme group).
- **Quaternary (4°) structure:** The **association of two or more polypeptide subunits** (each with its own tertiary fold) into a single functional multimeric protein. Subunits may be identical (homodimer) or different (heterodimer). **Example:** Hemoglobin (2 α-chains + 2 β-chains, 4 subunits total).

**2. Biuret test and its chemical basis** (2 marks):

The **Biuret test** is a general test for **proteins and peptides containing at least two peptide bonds**.

- **Reagent:** CuSO₄ in strong NaOH (sometimes with K-Na tartrate to stabilize Cu²⁺).
- **Chemical basis:** In alkaline solution, **Cu²⁺ ions chelate with the nitrogen atoms of peptide bonds** (the lone pair on N donates to Cu²⁺), forming a **violet/purple-colored coordination complex**. The intensity of color, measured at **540 nm**, is directly proportional to the protein concentration (used in quantitative protein estimation).
- **Negative result (blue color)** indicates free amino acids or dipeptides only.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Lipids and Nucleic Acids Focus
**[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify **lipids** with examples. Describe the **saponification reaction** of a triglyceride and define the **saponification number**. What does a high saponification number indicate?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **structure of a nucleotide**. Explain the **Watson–Crick model of DNA double helix** with a neatly labelled diagram. Add a note on **Chargaff's rule**.

---

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**1. Classification of lipids** (3 marks):

Lipids are classified into three broad categories based on their hydrolysis products and chemical structure:

- **Simple lipids** — Esters of fatty acids with various alcohols.
  - **Triglycerides (triacylglycerols):** glycerol esterified with 3 fatty acids. Storage form of fat. *Example:* Adipose tissue triolein.
  - **Waxes:** long-chain alcohol + fatty acid. *Example:* Beeswax (myricyl palmitate).
- **Compound (conjugated) lipids** — Fatty acids esterified with alcohol + additional groups.
  - **Phospholipids:** glycerol + 2 FA + phosphate + alcohol. *Example:* Phosphatidylcholine (lecithin).
  - **Glycolipids (cerebrosides):** sphingosine + FA + sugar. *Example:* Galactocerebroside (brain).
  - **Lipoproteins:** lipid + protein (transport in blood). *Example:* LDL, HDL.
- **Derived lipids** — Hydrolysis products of simple/compound lipids, or related molecules.
  - **Steroids:** four fused rings (cyclopentanoperhydrophenanthrene). *Example:* Cholesterol, testosterone, estradiol.
  - **Fat-soluble vitamins:** A, D, E, K.
  - **Terpenes:** isoprene (C₅) polymers. *Example:* β-carotene (pro-vitamin A).

**2. Saponification reaction** (2 marks):

Saponification is the **alkaline hydrolysis** of a triglyceride using a strong base (NaOH or KOH) to yield **glycerol and 3 molecules of soap (the sodium/potassium salt of fatty acids)**:

$$\text{CH}_2\text{OCOR}_1 \, | \, \text{CHOCOR}_2 \, | \, \text{CH}_2\text{OCOR}_3 \; + \; 3\,NaOH \;\longrightarrow\; \text{CH}_2\text{OH-CHOH-CH}_2\text{OH} \; + \; R_1COONa \; + \; R_2COONa \; + \; R_3COONa$$

The sodium salts of long-chain fatty acids (C12–C18) form **soaps** that are water-soluble because the polar —COO⁻ end is hydrophilic and the long hydrocarbon tail is hydrophobic, allowing micelle formation and grease emulsification.

**3. Saponification number** (2 marks):

$$\text{Saponification Number} = \frac{\text{mass of KOH (mg) required to saponify 1 g of fat}}$$

- A **high saponification number** indicates **short-chain fatty acids** (low molecular weight) — the fat has more carboxyl groups per gram.
  - *Example:* Coconut oil (rich in lauric acid, C12) has a **high saponification number (~250)** — used traditionally for soap making.
- A **low saponification number** indicates **long-chain fatty acids** (high molecular weight).
  - *Example:* Rapeseed oil (~170).

---

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**1. Structure of a nucleotide** (2 marks):

A **nucleotide** is the monomeric building block of nucleic acids. It consists of three components covalently linked:

1. **Nitrogenous base** — a purine (A, G — double ring) or a pyrimidine (C, T/U — single ring), attached to C1 of the sugar via an N-glycosidic bond.
2. **Pentose sugar** — **2′-deoxyribose** in DNA, **ribose** in RNA. The C1′ of the sugar is bonded to the base; C3′ and C5′ hydroxyls are involved in polymerization.
3. **Phosphate group(s)** — one or more phosphate groups esterified to the **5′-OH of the sugar** (in nucleotides; in nucleic acids the 3′-OH is also involved in the phosphodiester linkage).

> **Phosphodiester bond:** The linkage between adjacent nucleotides in DNA/RNA is a **3′→5′ phosphodiester bond**, formed when the **3′-OH of one sugar** attacks the **α-phosphate of the 5′-triphosphate of the next nucleotide**, releasing **pyrophosphate (PPᵢ)**.

**2. Watson–Crick DNA double helix** (3 marks):

In **1953**, James Watson and Francis Crick (building on X-ray data from Rosalind Franklin and Maurice Wilkins) proposed the double-helix model of B-form DNA:

- Two right-handed **antiparallel** polynucleotide strands wind around a common axis.
- The **sugar-phosphate backbone** is on the outside; the **nitrogenous bases** project inward, perpendicular to the helix axis.
- Bases pair in a **complementary, specific manner**: **A = T (2 H-bonds)**, **G ≡ C (3 H-bonds)**.
- The two strands are held together by **Watson–Crick hydrogen bonds** and stabilized by **base-stacking hydrophobic interactions** between adjacent base pairs.

**Key structural parameters of B-DNA:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Helix diameter | **2.0 nm = 20 Å** |
| Rise per base pair | **3.4 Å = 0.34 nm** |
| Pitch per turn | **34 Å = 3.4 nm** |
| Base pairs per turn | **10** |
| Twist per base pair | **36°** |
| Major groove | Wide, accessible to proteins |
| Minor groove | Narrow, less accessible |

**3. Chargaff's rule** (2 marks):

Erwin Chargaff's empirical analysis of DNA from various species (1947–1950) revealed:

$$[A] = [T] \quad ; \quad [G] = [C]$$

or in ratio form: **A/G = T/C = 1**. Furthermore, the **%GC content is species-specific** (e.g., *E. coli*: ~51%, *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*: ~65%, *Plasmodium falciparum*: ~19%).

**Significance:**
- Provided the **first chemical evidence** for base pairing in DNA.
- Showed that **DNA, not protein, was the genetic material carrier** (constant stoichiometry consistent with a template).
- The ratio [A+T]/[G+C] varies across species but is **constant within a species** — basis of **DNA hybridization** used in taxonomy and forensics.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "reducing" with "non-reducing" sugars:** Sucrose is non-reducing (both anomeric carbons engaged in glycosidic bond). Students often wrongly call sucrose reducing because "it tastes sweet." **Lose 1 mark.**
> 2. **Drawing the DNA strands as parallel, not antiparallel.** Watson–Crick model **requires antiparallel** orientation (one strand 5′→3′, the other 3′→5′). **Lose 1 mark.**
> 3. **Stating iodine number is "amount of iodine"** — it is **mass of I₂ absorbed by 100 g of fat**, not the volume. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 4. **Forgetting the "free anomeric hydroxyl" definition** of a reducing sugar — vague definitions lose marks. Use exact syllabus terminology.
> 5. **Writing pI for acidic or basic amino acids using the same formula** — pI is the **average of the two pKₐ values flanking the neutral (zwitterionic) species**. For aspartate: pI = (pKₐ₁ + pKₐ₂)/2 = (1.99 + 3.90)/2 = 2.95. **Lose 1 mark** if student uses wrong pair.
> 6. **In saponification, students often forget the (×3)** stoichiometry for triacylglycerol.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1, PCBBT302 Biomolecules (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

**🧬 Carbohydrates**
- General formula: $(CH_2O)_n$, n ≥ 3.
- **D vs L:** D-sugar has —OH on right at highest-numbered chiral C.
- **α vs β anomer:** α = —OH down (axial), β = —OH up (equatorial) at C1 in Haworth.
- **Mutarotation:** change in optical rotation to equilibrium value (+52.5° for glucose) due to α ⇌ open-chain ⇌ β interconversion.
- **Glycosidic bond** = C1–O–C linkage (maltose α-1,4; sucrose α-1,β-2).
- **Benedict's test positive:** glucose, fructose, maltose, lactose. **Negative:** sucrose.
- **Glycogen:** highly branched animal storage polysaccharide; **Starch:** plant storage (amylose linear + amylopectin branched); **Cellulose:** β-1,4 linear, structural.

**🧪 Amino Acids & Proteins**
- 20 standard α-amino acids; differ only in R group.
- **Zwitterion** at physiological pH: H₃N⁺–CHR–COO⁻.
- **Peptide bond = amide bond** between α-COOH and α-NH₂; partial double-bond character → **planar**.
- **pI (neutral AA) = (pKₐ₁ + pKₐ₂) / 2**.
- **Primary (sequence) → Secondary (H-bonds) → Tertiary (R-group interactions) → Quaternary (subunits)**.
- **Denaturation** disrupts 2°, 3°, 4° — but **NOT 1°** structure.

**🥑 Lipids**
- **Triglyceride = glycerol + 3 fatty acids** via ester bonds.
- **Iodine number:** degree of unsaturation (g I₂ / 100 g fat).
- **Acid number:** mg KOH / g to neutralize free FA.
- **Saponification number:** mg KOH / g to saponify 1 g fat (high = short chain).
- **Phospholipid bilayer:** hydrophilic head outside, hydrophobic tails inside — basis of biological membranes.
- **Saturated = solid; Unsaturated = liquid at room temperature** (oils).

**🧬 Nucleic Acids**
- **Nucleotide = base + sugar + phosphate.**
- **DNA:** deoxyribose, **T** (thymine), double-stranded, A=T (2 H-bonds), G≡C (3 H-bonds).
- **RNA:** ribose, **U** (uracil), single-stranded.
- **Phosphodiester bond:** 3′–O–P–O–5′ linkage.
- **Chargaff's rule:** [A]=[T], [G]=[C].
- **B-DNA dimensions:** 20 Å diameter, 3.4 Å rise/bp, 34 Å pitch, 10 bp/turn.
- **Tₘ (long DNA) = 69.3 + 0.41(%GC)**.

> ✅ **End of Module 1 Notes — PCBBT302 Biochemistry, KTU 2024 Scheme.**
> **Stay exam-ready, master the formulas, draw neat labelled diagrams, and use exact KTU terminology in your answers.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carbohydrates: Simple Sugars, Polysaccharides, and Complex Carbohydrates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT302 - BIOCHEMISTRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Carbohydrates: Simple Sugars, Polysaccharides, and Complex Carbohydrates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Carbohydrates** are defined as polyhydroxy aldehydes or polyhydroxy ketones, or compounds that can be hydrolyzed to yield these functional units. The term is derived from their general empirical formula, which historically resembled "hydrated carbon" (Cₙ(H₂O)ₙ), although this is a simplification and exceptions exist (e.g., deoxyribose, C₅H₁₀O₄).

Formally, carbohydrates are classified as **saccharides** (from the Greek *sakkharon*, meaning sugar) and are the most abundant class of biomolecules on Earth, serving critical roles in energy storage, structural integrity, and cellular recognition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style)**
> Carbohydrates are optically active polyhydroxy aldehydes (aldoses) or polyhydroxy ketones (ketoses) and their derivatives. They constitute the primary source of metabolic energy (~**4 kcal/g** or **~17 kJ/g**) and serve as structural components and recognition molecules in biological systems.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

Think of carbohydrates as the **"Petrol and Bricks" of living systems**:
- **Simple sugars (Monosaccharides)** act like instant petrol — they are quickly burned by cells to release immediate energy (think of glucose in your bloodstream giving you a quick burst of energy).
- **Polysaccharides** act like coal stockpiles or bricks — they are long chains of sugars stored in compressed form (starch in plants, glycogen in animals) or used as structural building material (cellulose in plant cell walls).
- **Complex carbohydrates (glycoconjugates)** act like the **"molecular ID cards"** embedded in cell membranes — they help cells recognize each other, much like how name tags or QR codes help identify individuals in a crowd.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Chemical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values for Carbohydrate Chemistry**
> - **Caloric value of carbohydrates:** **4.0 kcal/g (≈ 17 kJ/g)**
> - **Standard optical rotation of D-glucose (α-D-glucose):** **+112.2°**
> - **Standard optical rotation of β-D-glucose:** **+18.7°**
> - **Equilibrium specific rotation of D-glucose in water:** **+52.7°**
> - **Solubility of glucose in water at 25°C:** **~91 g/100 mL**
> - **Glycogen molecular weight range:** **10⁶ to 10⁸ Da (Daltons)**
> - **Cellulose molecular weight:** **~50,000 to 2,500,000 Da**
> - **Mean corpuscular glucose concentration (blood):** **~5 mM (fasting)**
> - **Characteristic IR absorption (O–H stretch):** **~3300–3500 cm⁻¹**
> - **General empirical formula:** **Cₙ(H₂O)ₙ** (where n ≥ 3)

### 1.4 Classification Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Hierarchy of Carbohydrates**
> Carbohydrates are classified based on the number of saccharide units they contain upon hydrolysis:
>
> | Class | Number of Units | Example | Function |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Monosaccharides | 1 | Glucose, Fructose | Instant energy, building blocks |
> | Disaccharides | 2 | Sucrose, Maltose, Lactose | Transport sugars, dietary sugars |
> | Oligosaccharides | 3–10 | Raffinose, Stachyose | Cell recognition, glycoproteins |
> | Polysaccharides | >10 | Starch, Glycogen, Cellulose | Storage, structural support |

The topic at hand — *Simple Sugars, Polysaccharides, and Complex Carbohydrates* — thus spans the entire architectural hierarchy of carbohydrate biochemistry.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Monosaccharides — The Building Blocks

**Monosaccharides** are the simplest, non-hydrolyzable carbohydrate units. They are classified by:
1. **Functional group:**
   - **Aldoses** — contain an aldehyde (–CHO) group (e.g., glucose, galactose, ribose).
   - **Ketoses** — contain a ketone (C=O) group (e.g., fructose, ribulose).
2. **Number of carbon atoms:**
   - **Trioses (C3), Tetroses (C4), Pentoses (C5), Hexoses (C6), Heptoses (C7).**

> [!NOTE]
> **Biological Importance of Common Monosaccharides**
> - **D-Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆):** Blood sugar; the universal fuel of metabolism.
> - **D-Fructose (C₆H₁₂O₆):** Fruit sugar; the sweetest natural sugar.
> - **D-Galactose (C₆H₁₂O₆):** Component of lactose; vital for glycoproteins.
> - **D-Ribose (C₅H₁₀O₅):** Backbone of RNA.
> - **2-Deoxy-D-Ribose (C₅H₁₀O₄):** Backbone of DNA.

### 2.2 Stereochemistry: D and L Isomers

Carbohydrates exhibit **chirality** at every carbon (except the carbonyl carbon and the terminal CH₂OH). The configuration is designated by reference to the **penultimate carbon** (the highest-numbered chiral carbon):
- **D-sugar** → –OH on the right (in Fischer projection)
- **L-sugar** → –OH on the left

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Naturally occurring sugars in living systems are predominantly **D-isomers**. Enzymes (which themselves are chiral) only recognize the D-forms in most metabolic pathways.

### 2.3 Cyclic Structure & Haworth Projection

In aqueous solution, monosaccharides with **5 or more carbons** predominantly exist as **cyclic hemiacetals or hemiketals** (intramolecular reaction between the carbonyl and a hydroxyl group).

- Aldoses (e.g., glucose) → form **pyranose** rings (6-membered, resembling pyran).
- Ketoses (e.g., fructose) → form **furanose** rings (5-membered, resembling furan).

The carbon derived from the carbonyl group is called the **anomeric carbon** (C1 in aldoses, C2 in ketoses). It can adopt two configurations:
- **α-anomer** → –OH below the ring plane (axial in chair form).
- **β-anomer** → –OH above the ring plane (equatorial in chair form).

### 2.4 Mutarotation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mutarotation** is the change in specific optical rotation of an optically active sugar in solution until it reaches an equilibrium value. When pure α-D-glucose (+112.2°) or pure β-D-glucose (+18.7°) is dissolved in water, the rotation changes until it stabilizes at **+52.7°**. This phenomenon results from the interconversion of α and β anomers via the open-chain form (called the **mutarotation equilibrium**).

### 2.5 Disaccharides — Glycosidic Bonds

Disaccharides are formed when two monosaccharides join via a **glycosidic bond** (a condensation reaction releasing one H₂O). The bond is named by the anomeric carbons involved:

- **Maltose (Glc α-1,4 Glc):** Malt sugar; product of starch hydrolysis.
- **Lactose (Gal β-1,4 Glc):** Milk sugar.
- **Sucrose (Glc α-1,2 β Fru):** Table sugar; the only non-reducing common disaccharide (no free anomeric carbon).

### 2.6 Polysaccharides — Storage and Structural Giants

**A. Storage Polysaccharides:**
- **Starch** (plants): Two components — **Amylose** (unbranched α-1,4 glucose) and **Amylopectin** (α-1,4 backbone with α-1,6 branches every 24–30 residues).
- **Glycogen** (animals): Highly branched α-1,4 glucose with α-1,6 branches every 8–12 residues; stored mainly in liver and muscle.

**B. Structural Polysaccharides:**
- **Cellulose** (plants): Linear β-1,4 glucose polymer; the most abundant organic polymer on Earth.
- **Chitin** (animals/fungi): β-1,4 N-acetylglucosamine (GlcNAc); forms exoskeletons.

**C. Structural Difference:** The human digestive enzyme **α-amylase** cleaves **α-glycosidic bonds** but not β-glycosidic bonds. This is why **cellulose is indigestible to humans** (it is dietary fiber) — we lack cellulase.

### 2.7 Complex Carbohydrates (Glycoconjugates)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Glycoconjugates** are carbohydrate chains covalently linked to proteins or lipids. They are critical for cell-cell recognition, signaling, and immune response.

| Glycoconjugate | Composition | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Glycoproteins** | Short, branched oligosaccharides on proteins | Cell recognition, hormones (e.g., erythropoietin), blood group antigens |
| **Proteoglycans** | Long GAG chains on a core protein | Cartilage, extracellular matrix, hydration |
| **Glycolipids** | Carbohydrate on lipid (cerebrosides, gangliosides) | Nerve cell membranes, blood group antigens |

**Glycosaminoglycans (GAGs)** are the polysaccharide chains of proteoglycans, often sulfated and uronic acid–containing:
- **Hyaluronic acid** (non-sulfated)
- **Chondroitin sulfate, Heparan sulfate, Keratan sulfate, Dermatan sulfate**

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet & Quick Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Key Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| General formula (sugar) | $\mathrm{C_n(H_2O)_n}$ | n = number of carbons |
| Energy released | E = m × 4.0 | **4.0 kcal/g (17 kJ/g)** |
| Molecular weight of glucose | $\mathrm{C_6H_{12}O_6}$ | **180.16 g/mol** |
| Degree of polymerization (DP) | $\mathrm{DP} = \dfrac{\text{Total mass}}{\text{Monomer mass}}$ | Unitless |
| Optical rotation (Mutarotation) | $[\alpha]_D^T = \dfrac{\alpha_{\text{obs}}}{l \cdot c}$ | $[\alpha]_D^{20}$ for D-glucose = **+52.7°** |
| Anomeric carbon position (aldose) | C1 | Bond with OH/H determines α/β |
| Glycosidic bond hydrolysis | $\mathrm{C_{12}H_{22}O_{11} + H_2O \rightarrow 2\,C_6H_{12}O_6}$ | Sucrose → Glucose + Fructose |
| Cellulose repeat unit | Cellobiose (2 × Glc) | β-1,4 linkage |
| Glycogen branch interval | α-1,6 branch every 8–12 residues | High solubility |
| Amylopectin branch interval | α-1,6 branch every 24–30 residues | Less soluble |
| Reducing sugar test | Positive: Fehling's / Benedict's / Tollens' | Free –CHO or hemiacetal |
| Molisch test (general) | All carbohydrates + α-naphthol + H₂SO₄ | Purple/violet ring |

### 2.9 Real-World & Engineering Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Carbohydrate Chemistry Matters Beyond Biology**
> - **Bioprocess Engineering:** Fermentation industries (ethanol, antibiotics) depend on carbohydrate substrates (molasses, starch hydrolysates).
> - **Food Engineering:** Glycemic index calculations require knowledge of glucose polymers and their digestion rates.
> - **Pharmaceutical Engineering:** Heparin (a GAG) is the most widely used anticoagulant — derived from animal tissues through carbohydrate extraction.
> - **Biomaterial Science:** Chitosan (deacetylated chitin) is used in wound dressings, water purification, and biodegradable packaging.
> - **Medical Diagnostics:** HbA1c (glycated hemoglobin) is a 3-month glucose indicator — central to diabetes monitoring.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equimolar Composition of Carbohydrates

**Problem:** Show analytically why a hexose has the empirical formula C₆H₁₂O₆.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Identify the carbonyl group and the hydroxyl groups:**
> A hexose is a 6-carbon monosaccharide. The carbonyl carbon contributes one C=O group. Each of the remaining 5 carbons bears a hydroxyl (–OH) group (in the open-chain form).

> **Step 2 — Write the open-chain skeleton:**
> The general form is: C₆ backbone with one aldehyde (or ketone) terminus and five CHOH groups (except the terminal CH₂OH).

$$
\mathrm{HOCH_2\!-\!(CHOH)_4\!-\!CHO \;\; (D\text{-glucose, open chain})}
$$

> **Step 3 — Count atoms:**
> - Carbon: 6
> - Hydrogen: from 5 OH groups = 5, from 4 CH groups = 4, from CH₂ = 2, from CHO = 1 → **Total H = 12**
> - Oxygen: from 5 OH groups = 5, from CHO = 1 → **Total O = 6**

$$
\boxed{\mathrm{C_6H_{12}O_6}}
$$

> **Step 4 — Verify the (CH₂O)ₙ pattern:**
> For n = 6: $\mathrm{(CH_2O)_6 = C_6H_{12}O_6}$ ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Equilibrium Specific Rotation (Mutarotation)

**Problem:** Pure α-D-glucose has a specific rotation of **+112.2°**, and pure β-D-glucose has a specific rotation of **+18.7°**. At equilibrium, the mixture shows a specific rotation of **+52.7°**. Determine the percentage of each anomer at equilibrium.

Let the fraction of α-anomer be $x$ and the fraction of β-anomer be $(1-x)$.

**Setting up the equation:**

$$
[\alpha]_{\text{eq}} = x \cdot [\alpha]_\alpha + (1-x) \cdot [\alpha]_\beta
$$

**Substituting the values:**

$$
52.7 = x \cdot 112.2 + (1-x) \cdot 18.7
$$

**Expanding the right-hand side:**

$$
52.7 = 112.2x + 18.7 - 18.7x
$$

**Combining like terms:**

$$
52.7 = 93.5x + 18.7
$$

**Solving for x:**

$$
93.5x = 52.7 - 18.7 = 34.0
$$

$$
x = \frac{34.0}{93.5} = 0.3636
$$

**Therefore:**

$$
\boxed{\alpha\text{-D-glucose} = 36.4\% \quad ; \quad \beta\text{-D-glucose} = 63.6\%}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Inference:** The β-anomer is thermodynamically favored at equilibrium (~2:1 ratio) because the C1–OH is in the **equatorial position** in the chair conformation, which is sterically and electronically more stable.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Hydrolysis of a Polysaccharide (Starch → Glucose)

**Problem:** A 1.62 g sample of starch (assumed pure amylopectin, average DP = 5000) is hydrolyzed completely. Calculate the mass of glucose produced and the mass of water consumed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Determine moles of starch:**
> Molar mass of starch ≈ DP × 162 g/mol (because each glucose residue loses H₂O during polymerization: 180 − 18 = 162 g/mol per residue).

$$
M_{\text{starch}} = 5000 \times 162 = 810{,}000 \;\text{g/mol}
$$

$$
n_{\text{starch}} = \frac{1.62}{810{,}000} = 2.0 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{mol}
$$

> **Step 2 — Moles of glucose released:**
> Each starch molecule yields DP glucose units (after hydrolyzing all glycosidic bonds):

$$
n_{\text{glucose}} = 2.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 5000 = 0.010 \;\text{mol}
$$

> **Step 3 — Mass of glucose produced:**

$$
m_{\text{glucose}} = 0.010 \times 180.16 = 1.8016 \;\text{g}
$$

> **Step 4 — Moles of water consumed (one per glycosidic bond):**
> Number of bonds per starch molecule = DP − 1 = 4999

$$
n_{\text{water}} = 2.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 4999 \approx 0.01 \;\text{mol}
$$

$$
m_{\text{water}} = 0.01 \times 18.015 = 0.180 \;\text{g}
$$

> **Step 5 — Mass balance check:**

$$
m_{\text{starch}} + m_{\text{water}} = 1.62 + 0.18 = 1.80 \;\text{g} \approx m_{\text{glucose}} \;\;\checkmark
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sugar Identification Tests

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Diagnostic Tests for Carbohydrates**

| Test | Reagent | Positive Result | Identifies |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Molisch** | α-naphthol + conc. H₂SO₄ | Violet ring at junction | **All carbohydrates** |
| **Benedict's / Fehling's** | Cu²⁺ (alkaline) | Brick-red Cu₂O precipitate | **Reducing sugars** |
| **Tollens' (Silver mirror)** | Ag⁺ (ammoniacal) | Silver mirror on glass | **Reducing sugars** |
| **Barfoed's** | Cu²⁺ (acidic) | Red ppt (in 1–2 min) | **Monosaccharides** (vs. disaccharides) |
| **Seliwanoff's** | Resorcinol + HCl | Cherry-red (ketoses) / faint (aldoses) | **Ketoses (fructose)** |
| **Bial's** | Orcinol + FeCl₃ + HCl | Green color | **Pentoses (ribose)** |
| **Iodine test** | I₂/KI solution | Blue-black (starch), Red-brown (glycogen) | **Storage polysaccharides** |
| **Osazone test** | Phenylhydrazine | Characteristic crystal shapes | **Glucose, Fructose, Lactose, Maltose** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sucrose is a non-reducing sugar** because both anomeric carbons (C1 of glucose and C2 of fructose) are tied up in the glycosidic bond. It gives a negative Benedict's test.

---

### 3.5 Step-by-Step: Ring Closure (Hemiacetal Formation)

**Problem:** Show how D-glucose in its open-chain form cyclizes to form α-D-glucopyranose.

> **Step 1 — Open-chain D-glucose has the C5 hydroxyl group (–OH) positioned to attack the C1 aldehyde carbon intramolecularly.**

> **Step 2 — Nucleophilic addition:** The lone pair on the C5 oxygen attacks the electrophilic C1 carbonyl carbon. This forms a new C–O bond and converts the C=O double bond into a C–OH single bond.

> **Step 3 — Hemiacetal formation:** The C1 carbon now bears a new –OH group (the anomeric hydroxyl) and is bonded to the ring oxygen. The product is a 6-membered ring (pyranose).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Open-chain D-glucose} \;&\xrightarrow{\text{intramolecular nucleophilic addition}} \;\text{cyclic hemiacetal} \\[4pt]
\text{C1 (sp², trigonal)} \;&\longrightarrow\; \text{C1 (sp³, tetrahedral, anomeric)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 4 — Anomer assignment:**
> - If the new –OH on C1 is on the **right** in Fischer projection (below the plane in Haworth) → **α-anomer**.
> - If on the **left** (above the plane) → **β-anomer**.

> **Step 5 — Mutarotation equilibrium:** In aqueous solution, the ring opens and recloses repeatedly, interconverting α ↔ open chain ↔ β, until equilibrium is reached (~36% α, ~64% β at 25 °C).

---

### 3.6 Step-by-Step: Sucrose Inversion

**Problem:** Explain the inversion of sucrose and calculate the specific rotation of an equimolar hydrolyzed mixture.

> **Step 1 — Sucrose:** $[\alpha]_D^{20} = +66.5°$ (dextrorotatory)

> **Step 2 — Hydrolysis yields:** D-Glucose (52.7°) + D-Fructose (–92.0°)

> **Step 3 — Mixture specific rotation (called "invert sugar" because the sign of rotation has inverted):**

$$
[\alpha]_{\text{mix}} = \frac{[\alpha]_{\text{glucose}} + [\alpha]_{\text{fructose}}}{2} = \frac{52.7 + (-92.0)}{2} = \frac{-39.3}{2} = -19.65°
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The rotation has flipped from positive (+66.5°) to negative (–19.65°). This is why the hydrolysis of sucrose is called **inversion**, and the resulting equimolar mixture is called **invert sugar** (used in honey, confectionery, and royal jelly).

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define carbohydrates. How are they classified based on the number of saccharide units? Give one example for each class.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Carbohydrates are polyhydroxy aldehydes or polyhydroxy ketones, or substances that yield these compounds on hydrolysis. They have the general empirical formula $\mathrm{C_n(H_2O)_n}$ and are the major source of metabolic energy in living systems.

> **Classification (2 marks):** Carbohydrates are classified based on the number of monosaccharide units released on hydrolysis:
> - **Monosaccharides (single unit):** Cannot be hydrolyzed further. Example — **Glucose** (C₆H₁₂O₆).
> - **Disaccharides (two units):** Yield two monosaccharides on hydrolysis. Example — **Sucrose** (yields glucose + fructose).
> - **Oligosaccharides (3–10 units):** Example — **Raffinose** (a trisaccharide).
> - **Polysaccharides (many units):** Example — **Starch** (a polymer of glucose, found in plants).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is mutarotation? Explain with reference to D-glucose.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Mutarotation is the phenomenon in which the specific optical rotation of a freshly dissolved sugar in water gradually changes until it reaches a constant equilibrium value.

> **Explanation (2 marks):** When pure α-D-glucose (specific rotation **+112.2°**) is dissolved in water, the ring opens to the open-chain form and recloses to produce β-D-glucose. This causes the rotation to decrease to a final value of **+52.7°**. Similarly, pure β-D-glucose (**+18.7°**) rises to the same equilibrium value. The equilibrium mixture contains approximately **36% α-anomer and 64% β-anomer**, with the β-form predominating because its anomeric –OH is in the more stable equatorial position.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**
**(a)** Describe the structure, occurrence, and biological importance of any **three** important disaccharides. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Explain the structure of starch and glycogen. Compare their structural and functional properties. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

### (a) Three Important Disaccharides (7 marks)

**1. Maltose (2 marks)**
- **Structure:** Two glucose units linked by an **α-1,4-glycosidic bond**. Molecular formula: C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁.
- **Formation:** Produced by the enzymatic hydrolysis of starch by amylase (e.g., during seed germination). "Malt sugar" is found in germinating barley.
- **Biological importance:** Intermediate in starch digestion; a reducing sugar (free anomeric carbon on the second glucose).

**2. Lactose (2 marks)**
- **Structure:** Galactose linked to glucose via a **β-1,4-glycosidic bond**. Molecular formula: C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁.
- **Occurrence:** Found exclusively in the milk of mammals (~5% in cow's milk, ~7% in human milk).
- **Biological importance:** Primary carbohydrate source for infants; hydrolyzed by the enzyme **lactase** in the small intestine. Lactose intolerance results from lactase deficiency.

**3. Sucrose (2.5 marks)**
- **Structure:** Glucose (α) linked to fructose (β) via an **α-1,β-2 glycosidic bond** (involving both anomeric carbons). Molecular formula: C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁.
- **Occurrence:** Common table sugar; abundant in sugarcane and sugar beet.
- **Biological importance:** A **non-reducing sugar** (no free anomeric carbon). It is the major transport sugar in plants (translocated through phloem). Hydrolyzed by the enzyme **invertase** (sucrase) to yield glucose and fructose ("invert sugar").
- **Sweetness:** Sucrose is the reference standard (sweetness = 1.0); fructose is sweeter (1.7), glucose is less sweet (0.7).

### (b) Starch and Glycogen (7 marks)

**Starch — Structure (2.5 marks):**
Starch is the principal storage polysaccharide of plants, stored in chloroplasts (transient) and amyloplasts (permanent, e.g., in seeds, tubers). It consists of two components:
- **Amylose (~15–20%):** A long, **unbranched** chain of D-glucose units linked by **α-1,4-glycosidic bonds**. It is water-soluble and forms a helical structure.
- **Amylopectin (~80–85%):** A highly branched polymer. The backbone has **α-1,4 linkages**, with **α-1,6 branch points** occurring every **24 to 30 glucose residues**. It is insoluble in cold water but swells in hot water (gelatinization).

**Glycogen — Structure (2.5 marks):**
- The storage polysaccharide of animals, found primarily in **liver (~6–10% by weight)** and **skeletal muscle (~1–2%)**.
- Structurally similar to amylopectin but **more highly branched**, with **α-1,6 branch points every 8 to 12 residues**.
- The high branching creates many non-reducing ends, allowing **rapid mobilization of glucose** during metabolic demand (e.g., muscle contraction, blood glucose regulation).
- Glycogen particles are associated with the enzymes of glycogen metabolism (glycogen synthase, branching enzyme, phosphorylase) in "glycogen granules."

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Feature | Starch | Glycogen |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Plants | Animals (liver, muscle) |
| Branch interval | Every 24–30 residues | Every 8–12 residues |
| Degree of branching | Moderate | Very high |
| Solubility | Amylopectin swells in hot water | Colloidal in cold water |
| Iodine test | Blue-black (amylose), red-violet (amylopectin) | Red-brown |
| Function | Long-term energy storage in plants | Rapid-mobilization energy reserve |

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1 — Internal Choice Alternative]**
**(a)** Discuss the structure and biological significance of complex carbohydrates (glycoconjugates). Describe glycoproteins, proteoglycans, and glycolipids with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Define glycosaminoglycans (GAGs). Explain the structure and functions of any three GAGs with their clinical significance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

### (a) Complex Carbohydrates — Glycoconjugates (7 marks)

> **Definition (1 mark):** Glycoconjugates are biomolecules in which one or more carbohydrate chains (oligosaccharides or polysaccharides) are covalently linked to a protein or lipid. They are essential for cell-cell communication, immune recognition, and structural integrity.

**1. Glycoproteins (2 marks):**
- Carbohydrate chains (short, branched, often 2–15 sugars) are attached to a protein backbone.
- **N-linked glycans:** Attached to the amide N of asparagine (Asn). The first sugar is always N-acetylglucosamine (GlcNAc).
- **O-linked glycans:** Attached to the hydroxyl O of serine or threonine. The first sugar is usually N-acetylgalactosamine (GalNAc).
- **Examples:** Erythropoietin (EPO, recombinant therapeutic), blood group antigens (ABO system), mucins (protective lining of gut and respiratory tract), antibodies (IgG has one N-linked glycan on each heavy chain critical for Fc receptor binding).

**2. Proteoglycans (2 marks):**
- A "bottle brush" structure: a **core protein** with one or more **long, linear, unbranched glycosaminoglycan (GAG) chains** attached.
- GAG chains are highly negatively charged (sulfate and uronic acid groups) and attract water, giving tissues **resilience and hydration**.
- **Examples:** Aggrecan (in cartilage, with ~100 chondroitin sulfate and ~50 keratan sulfate chains), syndecan (cell-surface proteoglycan), perlecan (basement membranes).

**3. Glycolipids (2 marks):**
- Carbohydrate chains attached to a lipid (usually ceramide or glycerol).
- Found almost exclusively on the **outer leaflet of the plasma membrane**, where the carbohydrate head group forms part of the **glycocalyx**.
- **Examples:**
  - **Cerebrosides:** One sugar (Glc or Gal) on ceramide. Major component of myelin sheath.
  - **Gangliosides:** Complex oligosaccharides (with sialic acid, N-acetylneuraminic acid) on ceramide. Abundant in nerve cell membranes.
  - **ABO blood group antigens** are glycolipids (and glycoproteins) on red blood cells.

### (b) Glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) (7 marks)

> **Definition (1 mark):** GAGs are long, unbranched polysaccharides composed of repeating **disaccharide units**, where one of the two sugars is always an **amino sugar** (GlcNAc or GalNAc) and the other is usually a **uronic acid** (glucuronic or iduronic acid) or galactose. Most GAGs are **sulfated** (except hyaluronic acid).

**1. Hyaluronic acid (2 marks):**
- **Structure:** Repeating unit of **D-glucuronic acid (GlcA) + N-acetyl-D-glucosamine (GlcNAc)** linked by β-1,3 bonds, with β-1,4 links between disaccharide units. **Not sulfated**; the only GAG synthesized in the **Golgi** (others are made in the Golgi or attached to protein via a linkage region).
- **Functions:** Acts as a lubricant in synovial fluid (joints); a major component of the vitreous humor of the eye; provides hydration and turgor to skin and cartilage.
- **Clinical significance:** Hyaluronic acid injections are used in **osteoarthritis treatment** and cosmetic dermal fillers. Degradation is by hyaluronidase.

**2. Chondroitin sulfate (2 marks):**
- **Structure:** Repeating unit of **GlcA + GalNAc**, with sulfate groups at C4 or C6 of GalNAc.
- **Functions:** Predominant GAG in **cartilage, bone, heart valves, and tendons**. Binds collagen fibrils to provide tensile strength.
- **Clinical significance:** Used as a **dietary supplement for osteoarthritis**; degraded by chondroitinases. Mutations in the synthesis pathway cause connective tissue disorders.

**3. Heparan sulfate (2 marks):**
- **Structure:** Repeating unit of **GlcA (or IdoA) + GlcNAc**, with variable sulfation patterns (the most complex GAG).
- **Functions:** Found on cell surfaces (as proteoglycans like syndecan). Regulates **growth factor signaling** (binds FGF, VEGF). Also a key component of the glomerular basement membrane.
- **Clinical significance:** **Heparin** is a highly sulfated, closely related GAG used clinically as an **anticoagulant** (binds antithrombin III, enhancing its inhibition of thrombin and Factor Xa). Heparin overdose is reversed with **protamine sulfate**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Drawing the wrong anomer in Haworth projections:** A common mistake is putting the C1–OH of α-D-glucose on top. Remember: in D-sugars, α = anomeric –OH **below the plane** (trans to CH₂OH at C5).
> 2. **Confusing the branch-point glycosidic bond:** In glycogen/amylopectin, the branch is **α-1,6**; in chitin, the bond is **β-1,4**. Mixing these up costs full marks.
> 3. **Calling sucrose a reducing sugar:** Sucrose is **NON-reducing** because both anomeric carbons are engaged in the glycosidic linkage. This is a favourite 2-mark trap.
> 4. **Incorrect linkage for maltose vs. cellobiose:** Maltose = **α-1,4**; cellobiose (cellulose unit) = **β-1,4**. The α vs. β orientation changes digestibility entirely.
> 5. **Forgetting to write the mutarotation equation or values:** Examiners expect the numerical values (+112.2°, +18.7°, +52.7°) — vague statements lose marks.
> 6. **In glycoconjugate questions, students often forget to specify N-linked vs. O-linked for glycoproteins** — this distinction is essential for full marks.

---
