> ✅ **Energy value:** **4.0 kcal/g (17 kJ/g)**.
>
> ✅ **Classification:** Mono- (1), Di- (2), Oligo- (3–10), Poly- (>10) saccharides.
>
> ✅ **Aldoses vs. Ketoses:** Aldehyde at C1 (e.g., glucose) vs. ketone at C2 (e.g., fructose).
>
> ✅ **D vs. L:** Based on configuration of the penultimate (highest-numbered chiral) carbon. Naturally occurring sugars are D.
>
> ✅ **Anomers:** α (anomeric –OH below) and β (anomeric –OH above) — defined at the hemiacetal carbon.
>
> ✅ **Mutarotation values for D-glucose:** α = +112.2°; β = +18.7°; equilibrium = +52.7° (~36% α, ~64% β).
>
> ✅ **Glycosidic bond formation:** Condensation of two anomeric carbons with loss of H₂O.
>
> ✅ **Sucrose = non-reducing** (no free anomeric C); Maltose, Lactose = reducing.
>
> ✅ **Sucrose hydrolysis → invert sugar:** specific rotation drops from +66.5° to –19.65°.
>
> ✅ **Starch = Amylose (α-1,4) + Amylopectin (α-1,4 with α-1,6 branches every 24–30 residues).**
>
> ✅ **Glycogen:** More branched than amylopectin (branch every 8–12 residues); animal storage.
>
> ✅ **Cellulose:** β-1,4 glucose; indigestible to humans (no cellulase); structural polysaccharide.
>
> ✅ **Chitin:** β-1,4 GlcNAc; structural polysaccharide of arthropods and fungi.
>
> ✅ **Glycoconjugates:** Glycoproteins, Proteoglycans, Glycolipids — all function in cell recognition.
>
> ✅ **GAGs:** Repeating disaccharides with amino sugar; usually sulfated. Examples: Hyaluronic acid, Chondroitin sulfate, Heparan sulfate, Keratan sulfate, Dermatan sulfate.
>
> ✅ **Heparin:** Sulfated GAG; clinical anticoagulant; antagonized by protamine sulfate.
>
> ✅ **Diagnostic tests:** Molisch (general), Benedict's/Fehling's/Tollens' (reducing), Seliwanoff's (ketoses), Bial's (pentoses), Iodine (starch/glycogen).
>
> ✅ **Mnemonic for sweet sugars:** **"Fructose is the sweetest"** — Fructose > Sucrose > Glucose > Galactose > Maltose > Lactose.
>
> ✅ **Heparin vs. Hyaluronic acid:** Hyaluronic acid is **non-sulfated**; Heparin is the most sulfated GAG.
>
> ✅ **Glycoprotein linkage types:** **N-linked** (to Asn, first sugar GlcNAc) and **O-linked** (to Ser/Thr, first sugar usually GalNAc).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lipids: Fatty Acids, Phospholipids, Sphingolipids, Cholesterol, Steroids, and Prostaglandins
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT302 - BIOCHEMISTRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lipids: Fatty Acids, Phospholipids, Sphingolipids, Cholesterol, Steroids, and Prostaglandins

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lipids** are a diverse group of naturally occurring hydrophobic or amphipathic organic molecules that are insoluble in water but soluble in non-polar solvents such as **chloroform (CHCl₃)**, **diethyl ether (C₂H₅OC₂H₅)**, **benzene (C₆H₆)**, and **acetone (CH₃COCH₃)**. Biochemically, lipids constitute a broad class of biomolecules that include fats, oils, waxes, phospholipids, sphingolipids, sterols (e.g., cholesterol), fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K), and prostaglandins. They serve as structural components of biological membranes, concentrated energy reserves, signaling molecules, and protective insulating layers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Think of lipids as the **"Swiss Army Knife" of the cell**. Just as a Swiss Army knife has multiple tools (blade, scissors, screwdriver) for different jobs, lipids perform multiple biological roles:
> - **Energy bank account** (triglycerides store ~**9 kcal/g**, more than double carbohydrates' 4 kcal/g)
> - **Waterproof raincoat** (waxes on leaves and skin)
> - **Brick-and-mortar of cell walls** (phospholipid bilayer)
> - **Chemical messengers** (steroid hormones, prostaglandins)
> - **Shock absorbers** (fat pads around organs)

A simpler analogy: imagine lipids as **bricks in a wall**. The phospholipids are the bricks (hydrophilic head + hydrophobic tail), and the cell membrane is the wall. The cholesterol molecules act like **mortar** filling gaps to control fluidity.

### 1.3 Broad Classification of Lipids

| Category | Examples | Key Feature |
|----------|----------|-------------|
| **Simple Lipids** | Triglycerides, Waxes | Esters of fatty acids with alcohols |
| **Compound Lipids** | Phospholipids, Sphingolipids, Glycolipids | Contain additional groups (phosphate, sugar, etc.) |
| **Derived Lipids** | Fatty acids, Steroids, Prostaglandins | Hydrolysis products of simple/compound lipids |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Reference Values

- **Saponification value range:** **180–200 mg KOH/g of fat** (common vegetable oils)
- **Iodine number range:** **30–200 g I₂/100 g of fat** (depends on unsaturation)
- **Average energy yield:** **~9.0 kcal/g (37.6 kJ/g)** for fats vs. **4.0 kcal/g (16.7 kJ/g)** for carbohydrates
- **Standard chain length range in biological FA:** **C₄ to C₃₆**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fatty Acids (FA) — The Building Blocks

A **fatty acid** is a carboxylic acid with a long aliphatic chain (usually **4–36 carbon atoms**), which may be saturated or unsaturated. The general formula is:

$$\text{CH}_3(\text{CH}_2)_n\text{COOH}$$

where $n$ typically ranges from 2 to 34.

**Classification by Saturation:**

- **Saturated Fatty Acids (SFA):** Contain only single bonds (C–C). Examples: palmitic acid (C16:0), stearic acid (C18:0).
- **Monounsaturated Fatty Acids (MUFA):** Contain one C=C double bond. Example: oleic acid (C18:1, Δ9).
- **Polyunsaturated Fatty Acids (PUFA):** Contain two or more C=C double bonds. Examples: linoleic acid (C18:2, Δ9,12), linolenic acid (C18:3, Δ9,12,15).

**Nomenclature Convention:** **Cx:y** where **x = number of carbons** and **y = number of double bonds**. Position of the double bond is indicated by **Δⁿ** (counting from the carboxyl end) or **ωⁿ** (counting from the methyl end).

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential Fatty Acids (EFA)** cannot be synthesized by the human body and must be obtained from the diet: **Linoleic acid (LA, ω-6)** and **α-Linolenic acid (ALA, ω-3)**.

### 2.2 Phospholipids

Phospholipids are amphipathic lipids containing a **phosphate group**. They are the principal constituents of biological membranes.

**Structure:** A phospholipid has:
1. A **hydrophilic head** (phosphate group + glycerol/sphingosine + polar group)
2. Two **hydrophobic tails** (fatty acid chains)

**Sub-classes:**

- **Glycerophospholipids:** Based on **sn-glycerol-3-phosphate**. Examples: phosphatidylcholine (lecithin), phosphatidylethanolamine (cephalin), phosphatidylserine, phosphatidylinositol.
- **Sphingophospholipids:** Based on **sphingosine**. Example: **sphingomyelin**.

### 2.3 Sphingolipids

Sphingolipids are built on the **C18 amino alcohol backbone called sphingosine** (or its saturated form, sphinganine). They are abundant in **myelin sheath of neurons**.

**Major Classes:**

- **Ceramides:** Sphingosine + 1 fatty acid (amide linkage).
- **Sphingomyelin:** Ceramide + phosphocholine (found in nerve cell membranes).
- **Glycosphingolipids (Cerebrosides/Gangliosides):** Ceramide + sugar(s). Gangliosides contain **N-acetylneuraminic acid (NANA/sialic acid)**.

### 2.4 Cholesterol & Steroids

**Cholesterol** is a sterol with the molecular formula **C₂₇H₄₆O** and melting point **148–150 °C**. Its structure consists of:
- **Four fused rings** (3 six-membered cyclohexane rings + 1 five-membered cyclopentane ring) — the **cyclopentanoperhydrophenanthrene (sterane) nucleus**
- A **hydroxyl group (-OH)** at C-3 (polar head)
- A **double bond** between C-5 and C-6
- Two angular methyl groups (C-18 and C-19)
- An **8-carbon aliphatic side chain** at C-17

**Steroids** are derivatives of cholesterol. They include:
- **Sex hormones:** testosterone, estrogens, progesterone
- **Corticosteroids:** cortisol, aldosterone
- **Bile acids:** cholic acid, chenodeoxycholic acid
- **Vitamin D** (cholecalciferol)

### 2.5 Prostaglandins (PGs)

Prostaglandins are **C₂₀ lipid derivatives** synthesized from **arachidonic acid (C20:4, ω-6)** via the **cyclooxygenase (COX) pathway**. They contain a **five-membered cyclopentane ring** with two side chains.

**Naming:** PGF, PGE, PGD, PGI (prostacyclin), TXA (thromboxane) — letters indicate the ring substituents.

**Functions:** Inflammation, pain, fever, vasodilation, platelet aggregation, GI protection, labor induction.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Significance |
|-----------|---------|-------|--------------|
| **Saponification Value (SV)** | $SV = \dfrac{56 \times 1000}{\text{M.W. of fat}}$ | mg KOH/g fat | Measures average molecular weight; **high SV → low M.W. fatty acids** |
| **Iodine Number (IN)** | $IN = \dfrac{(B - S) \times N \times 12.69}{W}$ | g I₂/100 g fat | Measures unsaturation; **high IN → more double bonds** |
| **Acid Value (AV)** | $AV = \dfrac{V \times N \times 56}{W}$ | mg KOH/g fat | Measures free fatty acids (rancidity) |
| **Acetyl Value** | $AV_{\text{acetyl}} = \dfrac{56 \times 1000 \times (S' - S)}{W \times [1 - (S/1000)]}$ | mg KOH/g fat | Measures hydroxyl groups |
| **Reichert-Meissl Value (RMV)** | Volume × 0.1 N alkali / 5 g fat | mL | Measures volatile short-chain FAs |
| **Polenske Value (PV)** | Similar to RMV but for non-volatile insoluble FAs | mL | Distinguishes animal vs. vegetable fats |
| **Energy from fat** | $E = m \times 9.0$ | kcal (for m grams) | Energy yield of lipid |

Where in the iodine number formula: $B$ = blank titration, $S$ = sample titration, $N$ = normality of sodium thiosulfate, $W$ = weight of fat (g), and 12.69 = equivalent weight of I₂ / 10.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering & Biological Utility

- **Food industry:** Determining oil adulteration and quality using SV and IN.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** PGs as drugs (misoprostol, alprostadil); COX inhibitors (aspirin, ibuprofen).
- **Cosmetics:** Waxes, sterols in skin creams.
- **Membrane biology:** Designing liposomes for **targeted drug delivery**.
- **Diagnostics:** Cholesterol & triglyceride estimation in blood predicts cardiovascular risk.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Saponification Value and Mean Molecular Weight

**Step 1:** Write the saponification reaction (hydrolysis of a triglyceride by KOH).

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{KOH} \rightarrow \text{Glycerol} + 3\,\text{RCOOK}$$

**Step 2:** Let the average molecular weight of the triglyceride be $M$. Then 1 mole of fat requires 3 moles of KOH (molecular weight **56 g/mol**).

$$\text{Mass of KOH needed for 1 g fat} = \dfrac{3 \times 56}{M} \text{ grams}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to mg per gram of fat (Saponification Value):

$$SV = \dfrac{3 \times 56 \times 1000}{M} = \dfrac{168000}{M}$$

So the mean molecular weight of the triglyceride is:

$$M = \dfrac{168000}{SV}$$

**Step 4:** Mean molecular weight of one fatty acid chain:

$$M_{\text{FA}} = \dfrac{M - \text{M.W. of glycerol}}{3} = \dfrac{M - 92}{3}$$

**Worked Example:** A fat has $SV = 200$. Calculate mean molecular weight.

$$M = \dfrac{168000}{200} = 840 \text{ g/mol}$$

Mean fatty acid molecular weight:

$$M_{\text{FA}} = \dfrac{840 - 92}{3} = \dfrac{748}{3} \approx 249.3 \text{ g/mol}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Iodine Number

**Step 1:** ICl (Wijs reagent) or IBr adds across C=C double bonds.

$$\text{>C=C<} + \text{ICl} \rightarrow \text{>CI-CCl<}$$

**Step 2:** Excess ICl is reacted with KI to release I₂.

$$\text{ICl} + \text{KI} \rightarrow \text{KCl} + \text{I}_2$$

**Step 3:** Liberated I₂ is titrated with standard sodium thiosulfate (Na₂S₂O₃).

$$\text{I}_2 + 2\,\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow 2\,\text{NaI} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}_4\text{O}_6$$

**Step 4:** The iodine number formula (with $B$ = blank titre, $S$ = sample titre):

$$IN = \dfrac{(B - S) \times N \times 12.69}{W}$$

**Derivation of 12.69:** Equivalent weight of iodine (I₂) is **126.9 g/eq**. 1 mL of 1 N thiosulfate ≡ 0.1269 g I₂. To express as **g I₂ per 100 g fat**, multiply by 100/W. So the constant becomes **0.1269 × 100 = 12.69**.

**Worked Example:** 0.5 g of oil is treated with Wijs reagent. Blank titre = 50.0 mL of 0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃; sample titre = 32.5 mL.

$$IN = \dfrac{(50.0 - 32.5) \times 0.1 \times 12.69}{0.5} = \dfrac{17.5 \times 0.1 \times 12.69}{0.5}$$

$$= \dfrac{22.21}{0.5} = 44.42 \text{ g I}_2/100\text{ g fat}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Acid Value

**Step 1:** Free fatty acids (FFA) in fat are neutralized by KOH.

$$\text{RCOOH} + \text{KOH} \rightarrow \text{RCOOK} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2:** The acid value is mg KOH required to neutralize FFA in 1 g of fat.

$$AV = \dfrac{V \times N \times 56}{W}$$

where $V$ = titre volume, $N$ = normality of KOH, $W$ = weight of fat (g), 56 = molecular weight of KOH.

**Worked Example:** 2 g of oil requires 4.0 mL of 0.1 N KOH for neutralization.

$$AV = \dfrac{4.0 \times 0.1 \times 56}{2} = \dfrac{22.4}{2} = 11.2 \text{ mg KOH/g}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Properties of Common Fatty Acids

| Fatty Acid | Formula | C:D Notation | Melting Pt (°C) | Iodine Number | Source |
|------------|---------|--------------|----------------|---------------|--------|
| Butyric | C₄H₈O₂ | C4:0 | −7.9 | 0 | Butter |
| Palmitic | C₁₆H₃₂O₂ | C16:0 | 63.1 | 0 | Palm oil, animal fat |
| Stearic | C₁₈H₃₆O₂ | C18:0 | 69.6 | 0 | Animal fat, cocoa butter |
| Oleic | C₁₈H₃₄O₂ | C18:1, Δ9 | 13.4 | 89.9 | Olive oil |
| Linoleic | C₁₈H₃₂O₂ | C18:2, Δ9,12 | −5.0 | 181.0 | Sunflower, corn oil |
| Linolenic | C₁₈H₃₀O₂ | C18:3, Δ9,12,15 | −11.0 | 273.5 | Linseed oil, fish oil |
| Arachidonic | C₂₀H₃₂O₂ | C20:4, Δ5,8,11,14 | −49.5 | 333.5 | Animal tissue, fish oil |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the trend: as the number of double bonds increases, the melting point decreases and iodine number increases. This is because **cis-double bonds introduce kinks** that prevent tight molecular packing.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Major Phospholipid Classes

| Phospholipid | Phosphoric Acid + Base | Abbreviation | Net Charge | Location |
|--------------|------------------------|--------------|------------|----------|
| Phosphatidylcholine | Choline | PC (Lecithin) | Neutral (zwitterion) | Outer leaflet of plasma membrane |
| Phosphatidylethanolamine | Ethanolamine | PE (Cephalin) | Neutral (zwitterion) | Inner leaflet of plasma membrane |
| Phosphatidylserine | Serine | PS | Negative (at pH 7) | Inner leaflet (flips to outer during apoptosis) |
| Phosphatidylinositol | Inositol | PI | Negative | Membrane signaling (PIP₂, PIP₃) |
| Sphingomyelin | Choline (on ceramide) | SM | Neutral | Myelin sheath, brain |

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Steroid Examples

| Steroid | Source | Major Function |
|---------|--------|----------------|
| Cholesterol | All animal cells, synthesized in liver | Membrane fluidity, precursor to all steroids |
| Testosterone | Testes (Leydig cells) | Male sex hormone, anabolic |
| Estradiol | Ovaries, placenta | Female sex hormone |
| Progesterone | Corpus luteum | Pregnancy maintenance |
| Cortisol | Adrenal cortex (zona fasciculata) | Glucocorticoid, anti-inflammatory |
| Aldosterone | Adrenal cortex (zona glomerulosa) | Mineralocorticoid, Na⁺/K⁺ balance |
| Cholecalciferol | Skin (UV-B) → liver → kidney | Vitamin D₃, calcium homeostasis |
| Cholic acid | Liver (from cholesterol) | Bile acid, fat emulsification |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification of Lipids (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    A --> C[Compound Lipids]
    A --> D[Derived Lipids]
    B --> B1[Triglycerides - Fats and Oils]
    B --> B2[Waxes - Esters of FA with long-chain alcohols]
    C --> C1[Phospholipids]
    C --> C2[Sphingolipids]
    C --> C3[Glycolipids]

    C1 --> C1B[Sphingophospholipids - Sphingomyelin]
    C1A --> C1A1[Phosphatidylcholine - Lecithin]
    C1A --> C1A2[Phosphatidylethanolamine - Cephalin]
    C1A --> C1A3[Phosphatidylserine]
    C1A --> C1A4[Phosphatidylinositol]

```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Phospholipid Bilayer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Extracellular Aqueous Environment]:::env --> B[Phospholipid Head - Hydrophilic]:::head
    B --> C[Fatty Acid Tail 1 - Hydrophobic]:::tail
    C --> D[Fatty Acid Tail 2 - Hydrophobic]:::tail
    D --> E[Phospholipid Head - Hydrophilic]:::head
    E --> F[Intracellular Aqueous Environment]:::env

    G --> D
    H[Glycoprotein]:::glyc --> B
    classDef env fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000

```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Fatty Acid Saturation Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FA[Fatty Acid]:::root --> SAT[Saturated FA - Single Bonds Only]:::sat
    FA --> UN[Unsaturated FA]:::root
    UN --> MUFA[Monounsaturated FA - 1 Double Bond]:::mu
    UN --> PUFA[Polyunsaturated FA - 2 or More Double Bonds]:::pu
    SAT --> S1[Stearic Acid C18:0]:::sat
    SAT --> S2[Palmitic Acid C16:0]:::sat
    MUFA --> M1[Oleic Acid C18:1]:::mu
    MUFA --> M2[Palmitoleic Acid C16:1]:::mu
    PUFA --> P1[Linoleic Acid C18:2 - omega 6 EFA]:::pu
    PUFA --> P2[Linolenic Acid C18:3 - omega 3 EFA]:::pu
    PUFA --> P3[Arachidonic Acid C20:4]:::pu
    classDef root fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef sat fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000
    classDef mu fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef pu fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Arachidonic Acid Cascade (Prostaglandin Synthesis)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Membrane Phospholipid]:::mem --> B[Phospholipase A2]:::enz
    B --> C[Arachidonic Acid C20:4]:::fa
    C --> D[Cyclooxygenase COX 1 and 2]:::enz
    C --> E[Lipoxygenase LOX]:::enz
    D --> F[PGG2 and PGH2]:::pg
    F --> G[PGE2]:::pg
    F --> H[PGF2 alpha]:::pg
    F --> I[PGI2 Prostacyclin]:::pg
    F --> J[TXA2 Thromboxane]:::pg
    E --> K[Leukotrienes LT]:::lt
    classDef mem fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000

```

### 4.5 Block Diagram: Cholesterol Structural Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CH[Cholesterol C27H46O]:::root --> R1[Ring A - Cyclohexane with 3-OH]:::ring
    CH --> R2[Ring B - Cyclohexane with C5-C6 double bond]:::ring
    CH --> R3[Ring C - Cyclohexane]:::ring
    CH --> R4[Ring D - Cyclopentane]:::ring
    CH --> M1[Angular Methyl C18 at C13]:::me
    CH --> M2[Angular Methyl C19 at C10]:::me
    CH --> SC[Aliphatic Side Chain C20 to C27 at C17]:::sc
    CH --> P[Precursor Function]:::prec
    P --> P1[Vitamin D3]:::prec
    P --> P2[Bile Acids]:::prec
    P --> P3[Sex Hormones]:::prec
    P --> P4[Corticosteroids]:::prec
    classDef root fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef ring fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000

    classDef sc fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef prec fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define essential fatty acids. Give two examples with their omega designations.**

**Model Answer:**

Essential fatty acids (EFAs) are fatty acids that cannot be synthesized de novo by the human body and must be obtained from the diet because the body lacks the desaturase enzymes required to introduce double bonds at positions beyond Δ9. They are required for normal growth, development, and membrane function.

**Examples:**
1. **Linoleic acid (LA)** — C18:2, ω-6 (double bonds at Δ9,12)
2. **α-Linolenic acid (ALA)** — C18:3, ω-3 (double bonds at Δ9,12,15)

**Functions:** Components of membrane phospholipids, precursors of eicosanoids (prostaglandins, leukotrienes), regulation of inflammation and blood pressure.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is saponification value? How is it related to the molecular weight of a fat?**

**Model Answer:**

The **saponification value (SV)** is defined as the number of milligrams of **potassium hydroxide (KOH)** required to completely saponify (hydrolyze) **1 gram of fat** or oil. It is an indirect measure of the average molecular weight of the fatty acids in the triglyceride.

**Relation to molecular weight:**

$$SV = \dfrac{3 \times 56 \times 1000}{M} = \dfrac{168000}{M}$$

where $M$ = average molecular weight of the triglyceride. Rearranging:

$$M = \dfrac{168000}{SV}$$

**Inference:** A **high SV** indicates **low molecular weight fatty acids** (many short-chain FAs), while a **low SV** indicates **high molecular weight fatty acids** (longer chains). For example, coconut oil has high SV (~250) due to abundant lauric acid, while olive oil has moderate SV (~190).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1 Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify lipids in detail. Explain the structure and biological significance of phospholipids with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is the iodine number? Describe the principle and procedure for the determination of iodine number with the Wijs method. Mention the formula used.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Classification of Lipids:**

**(I) Simple Lipids:** Esters of fatty acids with alcohols.
- **Fats and Oils:** Triglycerides (triacylglycerols) — glycerol + 3 fatty acids.
- **Waxes:** Esters of fatty acids with long-chain monohydric alcohols (e.g., beeswax — myricyl palmitate).

**(II) Compound (Conjugated) Lipids:** Lipids with additional groups.
- **Phospholipids:** Contain phosphate + nitrogenous base (lecithin, cephalin).
- **Sphingolipids:** Contain sphingosine + FA (sphingomyelin, cerebrosides, gangliosides).
- **Glycolipids:** Contain carbohydrate (cerebrosides, gangliosides).
- **Lipoproteins:** Lipid + protein (LDL, HDL, VLDL).
- **Sulfolipids and Aminolipids.**

**(III) Derived Lipids:** Hydrolysis products of simple/compound lipids — fatty acids, glycerol, steroids, prostaglandins, fat-soluble vitamins.

**Structure of Phospholipids:**

A phospholipid is amphipathic. Example: **Phosphatidylcholine (lecithin).**
- **Backbone:** sn-Glycerol-3-phosphate
- **Position 1 & 2:** Two fatty acid chains (hydrophobic tails)
- **Position 3:** Phosphate + choline (hydrophilic head)

**General Structure (schematic):**

$$\text{Choline–O–PO}_4^-\text{–O–CH}_2\text{–CH(OOCR}_1\text{)–CH}_2\text{–OOCR}_2$$

**Biological Significance:**
1. **Membrane structure:** Form the lipid bilayer of all biological membranes.
2. **Surfactant action:** Dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine is the lung surfactant (prevents alveolar collapse).
3. **Cell signaling:** Phosphatidylinositol-4,5-bisphosphate (PIP₂) generates IP₃ and DAG second messengers.
4. **Lipoprotein formation:** Phospholipid monolayer coats LDL, HDL, VLDL.
5. **Detergents and emulsifiers:** Lecithin emulsifies fats in bile for digestion.
6. **Bile:** Phosphatidylcholine in bile prevents cholesterol gallstones.

**Examples:** Phosphatidylethanolamine (cephalin), phosphatidylserine, phosphatidylinositol, cardiolipin (mitochondrial inner membrane).

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Definition:** Iodine number (or iodine value) is the number of **grams of iodine (or ICl/IBr) absorbed by 100 g of fat or oil**. It is a measure of the degree of unsaturation (the number of C=C double bonds).

**Principle (Wijs Method):** Iodine monochloride (ICl) is allowed to react with the C=C double bonds of unsaturated fatty acids, with ICl adding across each double bond. The **unreacted (excess) ICl** is then liberated as I₂ by reaction with KI, and the liberated I₂ is back-titrated with standard **sodium thiosulfate (Na₂S₂O₃)** using starch indicator.

**Reactions:**

**Step 1: Addition of ICl across double bond**

$$\text{R–CH=CH–R}' + \text{ICl} \rightarrow \text{R–CHI–CHCl–R}'$$

**Step 2: Reaction of excess ICl with KI**

$$\text{ICl} + \text{KI} \rightarrow \text{KCl} + \text{I}_2$$

**Step 3: Titration of I₂ with thiosulfate**

$$\text{I}_2 + 2\,\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow 2\,\text{NaI} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}_4\text{O}_6$$

**Procedure:**
1. Weigh **W g** of oil/fat in a clean dry iodine flask.
2. Add **25 mL of Wijs reagent (ICl in glacial acetic acid)**; shake well and store in the **dark for 30 minutes** to complete the addition.
3. Add **20 mL of 10% KI solution** — the unreacted ICl liberates I₂ (brown color).
4. Titrate the liberated I₂ against standard **0.1 N Na₂S₂O₃** until a pale yellow color is obtained.
5. Add **1–2 mL of starch indicator** — solution turns blue-black.
6. Continue titration until the **blue color just disappears**. Note the titre value $S$ mL.
7. Run a **blank** (without fat) using the same reagent volumes. Note the titre value $B$ mL.

**Formula:**

$$IN = \dfrac{(B - S) \times N \times 12.69}{W}$$

**Significance of IN:**
- Linseed oil IN ≈ 175–200 (highly unsaturated → drying oil)
- Olive oil IN ≈ 75–90 (monounsaturated)
- Coconut oil IN ≈ 7–10 (highly saturated)

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1 Choice — Alternative Set]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the structure, classification, and biological importance of sphingolipids. Differentiate between cerebrosides and gangliosides.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the structure of cholesterol. Discuss the role of cholesterol in membrane fluidity and its biochemical importance as a precursor.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Structure of Sphingolipids:** Built on the **18-carbon amino alcohol sphingosine** (or its saturated form sphinganine). The ceramide core is formed when a **fatty acid is linked via an amide bond to the amino group at C-2 of sphingosine**.

**Sphingosine structure:**

$$\text{CH}_3(\text{CH}_2)_{12}\text{–CH=CH–CH(OH)–CH(NH}_2\text{)–CH}_2\text{OH}$$

**Classification of Sphingolipids:**

1. **Ceramides:** Sphingosine + 1 fatty acid. Acts as the parent compound.
2. **Sphingomyelin (SM):** Ceramide + phosphocholine. Major myelin lipid.
3. **Glycosphingolipids (GSL):** Ceramide + sugar(s). Further classified as:
   - **Cerebrosides:** Ceramide + 1 monosaccharide (glucose = glucocerebroside; galactose = galactocerebroside). No charge.
   - **Sulfatides:** Cerebroside + sulfate. Negatively charged.
   - **Gangliosides:** Ceramide + oligosaccharide containing **N-acetylneuraminic acid (NANA/sialic acid)**. Negatively charged. Examples: GM1, GM2, GD1a.

**Differences between Cerebrosides and Gangliosides:**

| Feature | Cerebrosides | Gangliosides |
|---------|--------------|--------------|
| Sugar moiety | One monosaccharide | Oligosaccharide chain |
| Sialic acid (NANA) | Absent | Present (1 or more) |
| Net charge | Neutral | Negative (acidic) |
| Example | Galactocerebroside | GM1, GD3 |
| Location | Myelin sheath | Neuronal membranes, gray matter |
| Disease | Krabbe disease | Tay-Sachs disease (GM2) |

**Biological Importance:**
- Major myelin lipids (electrical insulation of nerves).
- Cell recognition and signaling (glycan code on GSLs).
- Apoptosis regulation (ceramide as pro-apoptotic signal).
- Receptor function in pathogens (e.g., cholera toxin binds GM1).

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Structure of Cholesterol (C₂₇H₄₆O):**

Cholesterol has four key structural features:
1. **Sterane nucleus:** 4 fused hydrocarbon rings — three 6-membered (A, B, C) and one 5-membered (D).
2. **C-3 hydroxyl group (–OH):** Polar, gives weak amphipathic character.
3. **Δ5-6 double bond:** Unsaturation in ring B.
4. **C-17 aliphatic side chain:** 8 carbons (C20–C27).
5. **Two angular methyl groups:** C-18 at C-13; C-19 at C-10.

**Schematic representation (Mermaid block format):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cholesterol]:::root --> B[Rings A B C D Sterane Nucleus]:::ring
    A --> C[Hydroxyl at C3 - Polar Head]:::pol
    A --> D[Double Bond at C5-C6]:::db
    A --> E[Side Chain C20-C27 at C17]:::sc
    A --> F[Angular Methyls C18 and C19]:::me
    classDef root fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef ring fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000,color:#000
    classDef pol fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef db fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef sc fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef me fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080,color:#000
```

**Role in Membrane Fluidity:**

The **plasma membrane fluidity** depends on the balance between saturated and unsaturated FAs and the cholesterol content. Cholesterol acts as a **"fluidity buffer"**:
- **At high temperature:** Cholesterol **restricts** the movement of phospholipid tails → **decreases fluidity** (membrane stabilizer).
- **At low temperature:** Cholesterol **prevents tight packing** of fatty acid tails → **increases fluidity** (prevents solidification).
- The rigid sterane ring system interacts with the hydrocarbon tails, while the –OH group hydrogen-bonds with the phospholipid head groups and water.

**Cholesterol as a Biochemical Precursor:**

Cholesterol is the precursor for:

1. **Bile Acids** (cholic acid, chenodeoxycholic acid) — synthesized in liver, emulsify dietary fats.
2. **Steroid Hormones:**
   - **Sex hormones:** Progesterone → testosterone → estradiol
   - **Corticosteroids:** Cortisol (gluconeogenesis) and aldosterone (electrolyte balance)
3. **Vitamin D₃ (Cholecalciferol):** Synthesized in skin upon UV-B exposure → 25-OH-D₃ in liver → 1,25-(OH)₂-D₃ (calcitriol) in kidney.
4. **Lipoproteins and cell membranes** (structural role).

**Biochemical Importance:**
- Component of plasma membranes (20–25% of membrane lipid).
- Precursor of all steroid hormones and bile acids.
- Major risk factor in **atherosclerosis** when serum levels exceed **200 mg/dL** (LDL/HDL ratio).
- Gallstone formation when bile cholesterol precipitates.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Lipid Questions:**
> 1. **Saponification Value — Don't confuse the molecular weight:** $M$ refers to the **triglyceride**, not the fatty acid. Mean FA molecular weight = $(M - 92)/3$.
> 2. **Iodine Number — Don't use 127:** The constant is **12.69**, not 126.9 or 127, because iodine number is expressed as **g I₂ per 100 g fat**, not per gram.
> 3. **Essential Fatty Acids — Don't miss omega designation:** Always mention the ω-3 or ω-6 position alongside Δ notation.
> 4. **Sphingolipids — Don't confuse with phospholipids:** Sphingomyelin is technically a sphingophospholipid; do not double-count it.
> 5. **Cholesterol structure — Don't omit the Δ5-6 double bond:** Examiners specifically test for the unsaturation position.
> 6. **Prostaglandin synthesis — Don't say "from cholesterol":** PGs come from **arachidonic acid**, NOT cholesterol. Cholesterol gives steroid hormones.
> 7. **Cerebroside vs. Ganglioside — The key difference is NANA:** Examiners always check for the presence/absence of sialic acid.
> 8. **Sign Convention in Bilayer:** PC and SM are predominantly on the **outer leaflet**; PE and PS are on the **inner leaflet**. Get this wrong and you lose 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Lipids** = water-insoluble, organic solvent-soluble biomolecules; **~9 kcal/g** energy.
- **Fatty acids** are C4–C36 carboxylic acids; **nomenclature C:D, Δⁿ/ωⁿ**.
- **Essential FAs** = **Linoleic (ω-6) and α-Linolenic (ω-3)** — dietary essential.
- **Saturated FAs** → straight chain → **high melting point**; **Unsaturated FAs** → cis-kink → **low melting point**.
- **Phospholipids** are **amphipathic** (hydrophilic head + 2 hydrophobic tails) — main membrane lipid.
- **Sphingolipids** are built on **sphingosine backbone** — key in **myelin sheath**.
- **Cerebrosides = 1 sugar; Gangliosides = oligosaccharide + sialic acid (NANA)**.
- **Cholesterol structure:** 4 fused rings (3× cyclohexane + 1× cyclopentane), **–OH at C3**, **Δ5-6 double bond**, **C17 side chain**, **2 angular methyls**.
- **Cholesterol is a fluidity buffer** and precursor of **bile acids, steroid hormones, vitamin D₃**.
- **Prostaglandins** are from **arachidonic acid** via **COX pathway**; key types: **PGE₂, PGF₂α, PGI₂, TXA₂**.
- **Saponification Value** $SV = 168000/M$ — high SV → short-chain FAs.
- **Iodine Number** $IN = (B-S) \times N \times 12.69 / W$ — high IN → more double bonds.
- **Acid Value** $AV = V \times N \times 56 / W$ — measures **rancidity / FFA**.
- **PGI₂ (prostacyclin) vasodilates; TXA₂ vasoconstricts & aggregates platelets** — Aspirin inhibits COX-1 → ↓ TXA₂.
- **Tay-Sachs disease** = deficiency of **β-hexosaminidase A** → GM2 ganglioside accumulation.
- **Gaucher's disease** = deficiency of **β-glucocerebrosidase** → glucocerebroside accumulation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proteins: Primary, Secondary, Tertiary and Quaternary Structure, and Membrane Proteins
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT302 - BIOCHEMISTRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Proteins: Primary, Secondary, Tertiary and Quaternary Structure, and Membrane Proteins

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 1 — PROTEIN ARCHITECTURE & MEMBRANE BIOLOGY

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 🔬 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Proteins** are biological macromolecules composed of one or more **polypeptide chains** of **α-amino acid** residues linked by **peptide (amide) bonds**. Each protein possesses a defined **hierarchical architecture** consisting of four levels of structural organization: **primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary structure**, dictated by the linear sequence encoded in its corresponding gene.

### 🧠 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **highly engineered suspension bridge**:

| Structural Level | Engineering Analogy | Biological Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Primary | The raw steel cables shipped to the site | Linear sequence of amino acids |
| Secondary | Pre-twisted helical wire ropes | α-helix / β-sheet (local folding) |
| Tertiary | The complete 3D bridge structure | Compact folded 3D shape of one chain |
| Quaternary | Multiple bridges forming a network | Multiple chains assembling together |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The **sequence → structure → function** paradigm is the *central dogma of structural biochemistry*. Loss of any one structural level (due to denaturation, mutation, or misfolding) generally abolishes biological activity. This forms the basis for **Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark)** KTU examination questions.

### 📊 The Four Structural Hierarchies — At a Glance

| Level | Definition | Stabilized By | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary (1°)** | Linear sequence of amino acids | **Covalent peptide bonds** | Most fundamental; determines all higher orders |
| **Secondary (2°)** | Local regular folding patterns (H-bonding) | **Hydrogen bonds** of backbone | α-helix, β-pleated sheet, β-turns |
| **Tertiary (3°)** | Overall 3D shape of one polypeptide | Hydrophobic, ionic, H-bond, disulfide | Active site formation |
| **Quaternary (4°)** | Assembly of multiple polypeptide subunits | Subunit–subunit non-covalent interactions | Allosteric regulation (e.g., Hemoglobin) |

### 🧬 A Standard α-Amino Acid — The Building Block

$$ \text{H}_2\text{N} - \underset{\text{α-carbon}}{\text{CH(R)}} - \text{COOH} $$

Where **R** represents the variable **side chain**. At physiological **pH = 7.4**, amino acids exist predominantly as **zwitterions**:

$$ \text{H}_3\text{N}^+ - \text{CH(R)} - \text{COO}^- $$

> [!TIP]
> Only **20 standard amino acids** are genetically encoded. The "R" group distinguishes them — Glycine has R = H (simplest), Tryptophan has the largest indole ring.

### 🔗 The Peptide Bond — The Master Link

When the α-carboxyl group of one amino acid condenses with the α-amino group of another (loss of **one H₂O molecule**), a **peptide bond** is formed:

$$ \text{Amino Acid}_1 - \text{COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{N} - \text{Amino Acid}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Amino Acid}_1 - \text{CO} - \text{NH} - \text{Amino Acid}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resonance & Planarity:** The peptide bond is **planar** due to partial double-bond character (~40% double-bond). This was proven by **Linus Pauling** (1951). The **ω (omega) torsion angle** is restricted to **180° (trans) or 0° (cis)**. The *trans* configuration is strongly favored (except for **proline**).

**Key features of the peptide bond:**
- **Bond length:** ~1.32 Å (intermediate between single C–N at 1.45 Å and double C=N at 1.25 Å)
- **Rigid and planar**
- **Uncharged** but **polar**
- Gives rise to **dipole moment** along the C=O···H–N hydrogen bond direction

### 🧫 Membrane Proteins — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Membrane proteins** are proteins that are either permanently attached to, or associated with, the **lipid bilayer** of biological membranes (~**30%** of the human proteome). They mediate communication, transport, signaling, and energy transduction across the membrane barrier.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — A Level-by-Level Dissection

## 2.1 Primary (1°) Structure — The Linear Blueprint

### Definition in KTU Terminology
> [!NOTE]
> The **primary structure** of a protein is the **linear, covalent sequence of amino acid residues** joined together by peptide bonds, beginning from the **N-terminus (free α-amino group)** and ending at the **C-terminus (free α-carboxyl group)**.

### Operational Breakdown

- **Sequence Direction:** Always read **N → C** (5' → 3' genetic direction).
- **Numbering Convention:** The first residue (with free α-NH₃⁺) is residue #1.
- **Bond Type:** **Covalent peptide bonds** only — no non-covalent interactions are part of the primary structure.
- **Information Content:** The entire three-dimensional folding information of a protein is *encoded* in its primary sequence (**Anfinsen's Dogma**, 1972 Nobel Prize).

### Anfinsen's Thermodynamic Hypothesis

$$ \Delta G_{\text{folding}} = G_{\text{native}} - G_{\text{denatured}} < 0 $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anfinsen's Experiment (Ribonuclease A):** Removal of urea (denaturant) and β-mercaptoethanol (disulfide reducer) allowed **spontaneous refolding** → activity restored → primary structure determines 3D fold.

### Side Chain (R-group) Classification — KTU Exam Hot Topic

| Class | Members | Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Non-polar (Hydrophobic)** | Ala, Val, Leu, Ile, Pro, Phe, Trp, Met | Cluster in protein interior |
| **Polar Uncharged** | Ser, Thr, Cys, Tyr, Asn, Gln | H-bond with water |
| **Positively Charged (Basic)** | Lys, Arg, His | pKₐ > 7 (protonated at pH 7) |
| **Negatively Charged (Acidic)** | Asp, Glu | pKₐ ≈ 4 (deprotonated at pH 7) |
| **Aromatic (UV 280 nm)** | Trp, Tyr, Phe | Used for protein quantification |

### Peptide Bond Geometry (Pauling–Corey Parameters)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_\alpha - \text{C} - \text{N} - \text{C}_\alpha &\quad \text{(Planar atoms)} \\
\phi\ (\text{phi}) &= \text{rotation around } \text{C}_\alpha - \text{N} \text{ bond} \\
\psi\ (\text{psi}) &= \text{rotation around } \text{C}_\alpha - \text{C} \text{ bond} \\
\omega\ (\text{omega}) &= \text{rotation around } \text{C} - \text{N} \text{ (peptide bond)} = 180°\ (\text{trans})
\end{aligned}
$$

> The **Ramachandran Plot** maps allowed (φ, ψ) combinations — a high-yield KTU concept.

## 2.2 Secondary (2°) Structure — Local Folding

### 2.2.1 The α-Helix

> [!NOTE]
> A **right-handed helical** conformation stabilized by **intramolecular hydrogen bonds** between the **C=O of residue n** and the **N–H of residue (n+4)**.

**Geometric Parameters (Pauling, 1951):**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Residues per turn | **3.6** |
| Rise per residue | **1.5 Å (0.15 nm)** |
| Pitch (rise per turn) | **5.4 Å (0.54 nm)** |
| H-bond direction | Parallel to helix axis |
| φ, ψ angles | (≈ −57°, ≈ −47°) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "Alpha Helix H-bond: N+4 ahead"** → The C=O of residue *n* hydrogen-bonds with the N–H of residue *n+4*. For 3.6 residues/turn, after 18 residues, you have exactly **5 turns**.

### 2.2.2 β-Pleated Sheet

Formed when **two or more extended polypeptide chains (β-strands)** align side by side.

- **H-bond pattern:** Between **C=O and N–H** of *adjacent strands* (inter-strand), perpendicular to strand direction.
- **Strand rise:** ~**3.5 Å per residue**
- **Sheet types:**
  - **Parallel β-sheet:** N→C direction same in all strands; H-bonds are *non-linear*.
  - **Antiparallel β-sheet:** N→C direction opposite; H-bonds are *linear and stronger*.

### 2.2.3 β-Turns (Reverse Turns)

- Connect adjacent **antiparallel β-strands**.
- **4 residues** in a tight loop (often with **Pro** at position 2 and **Gly** at position 3).
- Stabilized by a single H-bond between residue *n* C=O and residue *n+3* N–H.
- Critical in **globular proteins** for chain reversal.

### 2.2.4 Other Motifs
- **3₁₀ helix:** 3 residues/turn, H-bond to n+3 (tight, rare).
- **π-helix:** 4.4 residues/turn, H-bond to n+5 (very rare).
- **Random coil:** Non-repetitive, irregular structure (not "structureless" — fluctuating).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Amino acid propensity in α-helix:** Ala, Leu, Met, Glu, Gln are strong helix-formers. **Proline** acts as a **helix breaker** (no N–H for H-bonding, rigid ring). **Glycine** is also destabilizing (too flexible).

### KTU Formula Sheet — Secondary Structure

| Concept | Formula / Parameter | Numerical Value |
|---|---|---|
| α-helix pitch | $p = n \cdot r$ | $3.6 \times 1.5 = 5.4$ Å |
| Rise/turn | $r \cdot n$ | 5.4 Å |
| H-bond span (α-helix) | $n \rightarrow (n+4)$ | 4 residues apart |
| H-bond span (3₁₀ helix) | $n \rightarrow (n+3)$ | 3 residues apart |
| H-bond span (π-helix) | $n \rightarrow (n+5)$ | 5 residues apart |
| β-strand rise/residue | $d$ | 3.5 Å |
| Mean residue weight (protein) | $M_{\text{res}}$ | **110 Da** |

## 2.3 Tertiary (3°) Structure — The 3D Fold

> [!NOTE]
> The **tertiary structure** is the overall **three-dimensional arrangement of all atoms** in a single polypeptide chain, including the spatial positioning of every secondary structural element, loops, and side chains.

### Forces Stabilizing Tertiary Structure

| Interaction Type | Strength (kJ/mol) | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Hydrophobic interactions** | 4–12 | Val, Leu, Ile buried inside |
| **Hydrogen bonds** | 8–40 | Tyr-OH ··· Ser-OH |
| **Van der Waals (London)** | 0.4–4 | Packing of side chains |
| **Ionic (salt bridges)** | 12–20 | Lys⁺ ··· Asp⁻ |
| **Disulfide bonds** | **~200** (covalent) | Cys–S–S–Cys |
| **Coordinate (metal)** | Variable | His–Zn²⁺ in Zn-fingers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Disulfide bridges** are the **only covalent** side-chain crosslinks. They form only in **oxidizing environments** (extracellular proteins like insulin, lysozyme, antibodies). Cytoplasmic proteins rarely have them.

### Domains
Independent, compact, globular folding units within a single polypeptide. Often correspond to **functional units** (e.g., DNA-binding domain, kinase domain).

### Motifs & Folds
- **Helix-turn-helix** (DNA binding)
- **β-α-β barrel** (TIM barrel — many enzymes)
- **Greek key** (4 β-strands)
- **Zinc finger** (Cys₂His₂ with Zn²⁺)

## 2.4 Quaternary (4°) Structure — Multi-subunit Assembly

> [!NOTE]
> The **quaternary structure** refers to the **spatial arrangement and stoichiometry of multiple folded polypeptide subunits (protomers)** in a functional protein complex.

### Examples (KTU Hot List)

| Protein | Number of Subunits | Composition | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hemoglobin** | 4 | α₂β₂ | O₂ transport |
| **Immunoglobulin G (IgG)** | 4 | 2H + 2L | Antibody |
| **DNA Polymerase III** | 10+ | Multi-subunit | Replication |
| **Virus capsids** | Many | Identical monomers | Protection |

### Symmetry Types
- **Dimer, Trimer, Tetramer** — e.g., **Hemoglobin** = tetramer with **C₂ symmetry**.
- **Homo-oligomer:** identical subunits (e.g., **Hexokinase** in some forms).
- **Hetero-oligomer:** different subunits (e.g., **Lactate dehydrogenase** = H₄ or M₄ = isoforms).

### Forces in Quaternary Structure
Same non-covalent forces as tertiary (H-bonds, ionic, hydrophobic, VdW), plus sometimes inter-subunit **disulfide bonds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Allosteric Regulation (KTU High-Yield):** Quaternary structure enables **cooperative binding** (e.g., O₂ binding to hemoglobin follows a **sigmoidal** curve unlike myoglobin's **hyperbolic** curve).

## 2.5 Membrane Proteins — The Lipid Interface

### 2.5.1 Classification

> [!NOTE]
> Membrane proteins are classified based on the **mode of interaction with the lipid bilayer**.

| Type | Mode of Attachment | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Integral / Intrinsic** | Embedded in bilayer; require detergent (e.g., SDS, Triton X-100) to solubilize | Glycophorin A, Bacteriorhodopsin |
| **Peripheral / Extrinsic** | Loosely attached to membrane surface; soluble after mild treatment (pH, ionic strength) | Cytochrome c, Annexins |
| **Lipid-anchored** | Covalently attached to a lipid moiety embedded in bilayer | GPI-anchored proteins, Src-kinase |

### 2.5.2 Integral Membrane Protein Topology

| Subtype | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Type I** | N-terminus extracellular, single TMD, C-terminus cytoplasmic | Insulin receptor |
| **Type II** | N-terminus cytoplasmic, single TMD, C-terminus extracellular | Transferrin receptor |
| **Type III** | Multiple TMDs, both termini cytoplasmic | GPCR (G-protein coupled receptor) |
| **Type IV** | Multiple TMDs, termini on opposite sides | Aquaporin |
| **Lipid-anchored** | No TMD, lipid tail in bilayer | GPI-anchored proteins |

### 2.5.3 Special Classes
- **GPCRs (G-protein-coupled receptors):** **7 transmembrane α-helices** — largest receptor family in humans.
- **β-Barrel proteins:** Found in **outer membranes of bacteria, mitochondria, chloroplasts** (e.g., OmpF porin — 16-stranded β-barrel).
- **Peripheral binding partners:** Bind to membrane lipids via **PH domains, C1 domains, C2 domains**.

### 2.5.4 Detergent Solubilization (KTU Concept)

> [!TIP]
> Integral membrane proteins are extracted using **amphipathic detergents** that mimic the bilayer. **Critical Micelle Concentration (CMC)** is the minimum detergent concentration at which micelles form. Common detergents: **SDS (anionic), Triton X-100 (non-ionic), CHAPS (zwitterionic)**.

$$ \text{Detergent micelle} = \text{Hydrophobic core (lipid tails)} + \text{Hydrophilic head} $$

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering & Biotech Utility

| Application | Protein Utilized |
|---|---|
| Recombinant insulin (Diabetes) | Engineered E. coli / yeast expression |
| mAb therapeutics (Cancer, COVID-19) | CHO cells producing IgG |
| Biosensors | Glucose oxidase / Luciferase |
| Industrial enzymes (Detergents) | Subtilisin (Bacillus) |
| Membrane protein drug targets | GPCR-targeted drugs (β-blockers, opioids) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Analysis & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Pitch and Rise of α-Helix

**Given:** $n = 3.6$ residues per turn, rise per residue $r = 1.5$ Å.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pitch (P)} &= n \times r \\
&= 3.6 \times 1.5 \text{ Å} \\
&= 5.4 \text{ Å} \\
&= 0.54 \text{ nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The α-helix advances 1.5 Å per residue along the helical axis; after 3.6 residues, exactly one complete turn of 5.4 Å has been traversed.

## 3.2 Problem: Determining the Length of an α-Helical Domain

**Q:** A transmembrane domain of a GPCR consists of **25 amino acids** arranged in a perfect α-helix. Calculate the **length** of this helix along the membrane normal.

**Step 1:** Identify the rise per residue.

$$ r = 1.5 \text{ Å per residue} $$

**Step 2:** Multiply by number of residues.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= n \times r \\
&= 25 \times 1.5 \text{ Å} \\
&= 37.5 \text{ Å} \\
&= 3.75 \text{ nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with lipid bilayer thickness.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Plasma membrane thickness} &\approx 35\text{–}40 \text{ Å} \\
\text{Helix length} &= 37.5 \text{ Å} \quad \text{(spans bilayer comfortably)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This is a classic KTU-style problem — connecting **structure** to **function** (membrane spanning).

## 3.3 Problem: Molecular Weight Estimation from Residue Number

**Q:** A protein has **350 amino acid residues**. Estimate its molecular weight. (Average residue weight = 110 Da).

**Step 1:** Apply the standard formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{res}} &= 110 \text{ Da} \\
n_{\text{res}} &= 350 \\
M_{\text{protein}} &= n_{\text{res}} \times M_{\text{res}} \\
&= 350 \times 110 \text{ Da} \\
&= 38{,}500 \text{ Da} \\
&\approx 38.5 \text{ kDa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Account for water loss during peptide bond formation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Water molecules lost} &= n_{\text{res}} - 1 = 349 \\
\text{Mass of water lost} &= 349 \times 18 \text{ Da} = 6282 \text{ Da} \\
M_{\text{actual}} &\approx 38{,}500 - 6282 \approx 32{,}218 \text{ Da}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For rough KTU calculations, the simple formula $M \approx 110 \times n_{\text{res}}$ is sufficient and widely used.

## 3.4 Problem: Hydrogen Bonds in an α-Helix

**Q:** A protein domain forms a continuous α-helix of **30 residues**. How many **internal hydrogen bonds** stabilize this helix? (Assume the first 4 N-terminal residues cannot H-bond; the last 4 C-terminal residues cannot donate to a residue beyond the helix).

**Step 1:** H-bond is between residue $n$ and $(n+4)$.

**Step 2:** Number of H-bonds = (residues available to donate) − 4 (first 4 residues lack partner behind) − 4 (last 4 residues can't donate forward into helix? — actually, the 4 final residues cannot H-bond as *donors* to later residues).

$$ N_{\text{H-bonds}} = (n - 4) = 30 - 4 = 26 \text{ H-bonds} $$

> [!TIP]
> General formula: For an α-helix of length $n$ residues → internal H-bonds ≈ $n - 4$.

## 3.5 Problem: Isoelectric Point (pI) Determination

**Q:** Calculate the pI of a tripeptide: **Gly–Asp–Lys**.

Given pKₐ values:
- α-COOH (C-terminus) = 3.65
- Asp side chain (–COOH) = 3.90
- α-NH₃⁺ (N-terminus) = 9.60
- Lys side chain (–NH₃⁺) = 10.50

**Step 1:** Identify the two pKₐ values **bracketing** the net zero charge state.

$$ \text{pI} = \frac{pK_{a1} + pK_{a2}}{2} \quad \text{(for acidic side chain)} $$

**Step 2:** The relevant pKₐ values for the **neutral species**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Below pI:} &\quad \text{Net charge} = +1\ (\text{Lys}^+ \text{ protonated}) \\
\text{Above pI:} &\quad \text{Net charge} = -1\ (\text{Asp}^- \text{ deprotonated, C-term}^-)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Take average of **Asp side chain pKₐ** and **α-NH₃⁺ pKₐ** (the two values between which the net charge passes through zero):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{pI} &= \frac{pK_a(\text{Asp}) + pK_a(\text{N-term})}{2} \\
&= \frac{3.90 + 9.60}{2} \\
&= \frac{13.50}{2} \\
&= 6.75
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — KTU Problem-Type → Method

| KTU Question Type | Method / Formula Used |
|---|---|
| Estimate MW of protein | $M = n_{\text{res}} \times 110$ Da |
| Length of α-helix | $L = n_{\text{res}} \times 1.5$ Å |
| Number of H-bonds in α-helix | $N = n - 4$ |
| Pitch of α-helix | $5.4$ Å |
| pI of acidic peptide | Average of two lowest pKₐ |
| pI of basic peptide | Average of two highest pKₐ |
| Length of β-strand | $n_{\text{res}} \times 3.5$ Å |
| Subunit stoichiometry | Identify homo vs. hetero-oligomer |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** 
Define the **quaternary structure** of proteins. Give two examples with subunit composition.

**Model Answer:**
The quaternary structure of a protein refers to the **spatial arrangement and stoichiometric assembly of multiple folded polypeptide subunits (protomers)** into a single functional protein complex. The subunits are held together by **non-covalent interactions** (and occasionally disulfide bridges), not peptide bonds.

**Examples:**
1. **Hemoglobin (Hb):** Tetrameric protein composed of two α-chains (141 AA each) and two β-chains (146 AA each) — represented as **α₂β₂** — with each subunit binding one heme group for O₂ transport.
2. **Immunoglobulin G (IgG):** Y-shaped tetramer composed of **2 heavy (H) chains** and **2 light (L) chains**, linked by disulfide bonds; involved in antigen recognition and immune effector functions.

> [!TIP]
> Quaternary structure enables **cooperative binding** (allostery) and **regulatory flexibility** absent in monomeric proteins.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
List the **different types of secondary structures** found in proteins and state the **type of bond** that stabilizes each.

**Model Answer:**

| Secondary Structure | Description | Bond Type |
|---|---|---|
| **α-Helix** | Right-handed helix; 3.6 residues/turn; H-bond between C=O of residue n and N–H of residue (n+4) | **Intramolecular H-bonds** (backbone) |
| **β-Pleated Sheet** | Extended polypeptide strands (parallel/antiparallel) connected laterally | **Inter-strand H-bonds** |
| **β-Turn (Reverse turn)** | Tight loop of 4 residues reversing chain direction | **H-bond between C=O of residue n and N–H of residue (n+3)** |
| **3₁₀ helix** | Tight helix, 3 residues/turn | H-bonds between n and (n+3) |
| **π-helix** | Wide helix, 4.4 residues/turn | H-bonds between n and (n+5) |
| **Random coil** | Irregular non-repetitive region | Weak, fluctuating H-bonds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All secondary structures are predominantly stabilized by **hydrogen bonds of the polypeptide backbone**, NOT side-chain interactions.

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Option 1) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a)** Describe the **primary structure of proteins** with a note on peptide bond geometry and its significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the **α-helix and β-pleated sheet** secondary structures, including their geometric parameters and H-bonding patterns. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

**Primary Structure Definition:**
The primary structure of a protein is the **linear, covalent sequence of amino acid residues** linked by **peptide (amide) bonds**, beginning from the **N-terminal residue** (with free α-NH₃⁺) and ending at the **C-terminal residue** (with free α-COO⁻).

**Peptide Bond Formation:**
A peptide bond is formed by a **condensation (dehydration)** reaction between the α-carboxyl group of one amino acid and the α-amino group of another, releasing **one molecule of water**:

$$ \text{H}_2\text{N–CHR}_1\text{–COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{N–CHR}_2\text{–COOH} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{N–CHR}_1\text{–CO–NH–CHR}_2\text{–COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Geometry of the Peptide Bond (Pauling, 1951):**

The peptide bond exhibits **partial double-bond character** (~40%) due to **resonance** between two canonical forms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O=C–N–H} &\leftrightarrow \text{^–O–C=N}^+\text{–H}
\end{aligned}
$$

Consequences:
- The **C–N bond length is ~1.32 Å** (between single 1.45 Å and double 1.25 Å).
- The four atoms **C=O, N–H, and the two adjacent Cα** are **coplanar** (rigid planar unit).
- The **ω (omega) torsion angle** is restricted to **180° (trans)** or **0° (cis)**. *Trans* is favored (~1000:1) except when **proline** is involved (cis probability increases).
- Free rotation occurs only around **φ (phi)** (Cα–N bond) and **ψ (psi)** (Cα–C bond).

**Significance:**
- The primary structure is **genetically determined** and **species-specific** (e.g., Human insulin differs from bovine insulin in 3 residues).
- It determines the **higher-order structure** (Anfinsen's hypothesis).
- Single amino acid substitutions can cause disease — e.g., **Glu → Val at position 6 of β-globin** → **Sickle Cell Anemia**.
- Information for **evolutionary studies** is encoded here (sequence homology).

---

### **Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**α-Helix:**

The α-helix is a **right-handed helical** secondary structure proposed by **Pauling and Corey (1951)** based on X-ray diffraction data of α-keratin.

| Geometric Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Residues per turn | **3.6** |
| Rise per residue | **1.5 Å** |
| Pitch (rise per turn) | **5.4 Å** |
| φ, ψ angles | ≈ −57°, ≈ −47° |
| Handedness | Right-handed (in L-amino acids) |

**Hydrogen Bonding Pattern:**
Each peptide C=O of residue *n* forms a **hydrogen bond with the N–H of residue (n+4)**. These H-bonds run **parallel to the helical axis**, conferring maximum stability.

**Side Chain Orientation:**
Side chains project **outward** from the helix axis, avoiding steric clash with the backbone.

**Helix Formers vs. Breakers:**
- Strong formers: **Ala, Leu, Met, Glu, Lys**
- Helix breakers: **Pro** (no N–H, rigid), **Gly** (too flexible)

**β-Pleated Sheet:**

In contrast to the α-helix, β-strands are **extended** (φ ≈ −135°, ψ ≈ +135°). Strands align laterally to form sheets via **inter-strand hydrogen bonds** between C=O and N–H groups.

| Feature | Antiparallel Sheet | Parallel Sheet |
|---|---|---|
| Strand orientation | Alternating N→C | Same N→C direction |
| H-bond linearity | **Linear and strong** | **Distorted and weaker** |
| Rise per residue | ~3.5 Å | ~3.2 Å |

The sheet has a **pleated (zig-zag)** appearance when viewed edge-on, arising from tetrahedral geometry at the Cα.

**Examples:**
- **Silk fibroin:** Antiparallel β-sheet (β-keratin).
- **Carbonic anhydrase:** Mixed parallel + antiparallel β-sheets.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "Alpha: H-bond ahead 4, Beta: H-bond beside"** → α-helix is intra-chain longitudinal; β-sheet is inter-chain lateral.

---

### **Question B (Option 2) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the **tertiary structure of proteins**, elaborating on the **various forces that stabilize** it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **classification of membrane proteins** with suitable examples and a note on **G-protein coupled receptors (GPCRs)**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

**Tertiary Structure Definition:**
The tertiary structure of a protein is the **three-dimensional arrangement of all atoms** in a single polypeptide chain, including the spatial disposition of every secondary structural element, loops, turns, and side chains. It defines the **overall fold** of the molecule — whether globular (compact, spherical), fibrous (elongated), or membrane-embedded.

**Forces Stabilizing Tertiary Structure:**

**1. Hydrophobic Interactions (~4–12 kJ/mol):**
The most important driving force for folding. Non-polar side chains (Val, Leu, Ile, Phe, Trp, Met) cluster in the **interior core** away from water → entropic gain as water is released from ordered clathrate cages.

**2. Hydrogen Bonds (~8–40 kJ/mol):**
Form between polar side chains:
- Tyr–OH ··· Asp-COO⁻
- Ser–OH ··· Glu-COO⁻
- Backbone amide ··· side chain OH

**3. Ionic (Salt Bridge) Interactions (~12–20 kJ/mol):**
Attractive electrostatic bonds between oppositely charged side chains:
- Lys⁺ (–NH₃⁺) ··· Asp⁻ (–COO⁻)
- Arg⁺ ··· Glu⁻

**4. Van der Waals (London Dispersion) Forces (~0.4–4 kJ/mol):**
Weak, transient, distance-dependent (~1/r⁶) attractive forces between closely packed non-polar atoms. Collectively significant in the densely packed interior.

**5. Disulfide Bonds (Covalent, ~200 kJ/mol):**
Form between **two cysteine residues** under oxidizing conditions, creating a **covalent –S–S– bridge**:

$$ 2\ \text{Cys–SH} \longrightarrow \text{Cys–S–S–Cys} + 2\ \text{H}^+ + 2\ \text{e}^- $$

Found in **extracellular proteins** (insulin, lysozyme, antibodies, BSA). Help in stabilizing the native fold.

**6. Coordinate (Metal) Bonds:**
Side chains (His, Cys, Asp) act as **ligands** for metal ions (Zn²⁺, Fe²⁺, Mg²⁺, Cu²⁺) → metalloproteins (e.g., **Zn-finger** transcription factors, **cytochrome** with heme-Fe).

**Examples:**
- **Myoglobin:** Monomeric globular protein; 8 α-helices; hydrophobic pocket holds heme.
- **Lysozyme:** Mixed α/β structure; first enzyme with solved 3D structure (Phillips, 1965).

**Biological Significance:**
- Defines the **active site** of enzymes.
- Provides **specific binding surfaces** for ligands.
- Loss of tertiary structure → **denaturation** (loss of biological activity).

---

### **Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Membrane Protein Classification:**

Membrane proteins are classified into three broad categories based on the **mode of interaction with the lipid bilayer**:

**1. Integral (Intrinsic) Membrane Proteins:**
- **Strongly embedded** within the hydrophobic core of the lipid bilayer.
- Can be solubilized **only by detergents** (e.g., SDS, Triton X-100) or organic solvents.
- Contain **hydrophobic transmembrane domains (TMDs)** that interact with the fatty acyl chains of phospholipids.

**Subtypes based on topology:**

| Type | Topology | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Type I** | N-terminus extracellular, single TMD, C-terminus cytoplasmic | Insulin receptor, Glycophorin A |
| **Type II** | N-terminus cytoplasmic, C-terminus extracellular | Transferrin receptor |
| **Type III / IV** | Multiple TMDs (polytopic) | GPCR, Aquaporin, Bacteriorhodopsin |
| **β-Barrel** | β-sheet barrel in outer membranes | OmpF, OmpA porins |

**2. Peripheral (Extrinsic) Membrane Proteins:**
- **Loosely attached** to the membrane surface via ionic, H-bond, or hydrophobic interactions with **lipid head groups** or **other integral proteins**.
- **Soluble** in aqueous buffers — do NOT require detergents.
- Examples: **Cytochrome c** (interacts with cardiolipin), **Annexins** (Ca²⁺-dependent lipid binding), **Peripheral subunits of ATP synthase**.

**3. Lipid-Anchored Membrane Proteins:**
- Lack a transmembrane domain but are **covalently attached to a lipid moiety** (fatty acid, isoprenoid, or glycosylphosphatidylinositol — GPI) that inserts into the bilayer.
- Examples:
  - **GPI-anchored proteins:** Alkaline phosphatase, acetylcholinesterase (RBC).
  - **Fatty acylated:** Src-family tyrosine kinases (myristoylation/palmitoylation).
  - **Prenylated:** Ras GTPases (farnesylation).

**4. Note on G-Protein Coupled Receptors (GPCRs):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GPCRs** form the **largest family of membrane receptors** in humans — **>800 members**, targeted by ~**35% of FDA-approved drugs**.

**Structural Features:**
- **7 transmembrane α-helical domains** (heptahelical topology).
- **N-terminus extracellular**, **C-terminus intracellular**.
- **3 extracellular loops** and **3 intracellular loops**.
- Extracellular ligand-binding site for hormones, neurotransmitters, odors, light.
- Intracellular loops interact with heterotrimeric **G-proteins** (Gα, Gβ, Gγ).

**Mechanism of Activation:**
1. Ligand binds to extracellular domain → conformational change.
2. GPCR acts as a **guanine nucleotide exchange factor (GEF)** for Gα.
3. GDP → GTP exchange on Gα → dissociation into Gα-GTP and Gβγ.
4. Downstream signaling via **adenylyl cyclase / cAMP, PLC / IP₃ / DAG, ion channels**.

**Examples of GPCRs:**
- **Rhodopsin** (vision)
- **β-adrenergic receptor** (epinephrine signaling)
- **Muscarinic acetylcholine receptor**
- **Dopamine receptors (D1–D5)**
- **Opioid receptors (μ, κ, δ)**

**Clinical Significance:**
- **β-blockers** (metoprolol) → block β-adrenergic GPCRs.
- **Antihistamines** → block H1 histamine GPCRs.
- **Opioid analgesics** → activate opioid GPCRs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing levels:** Students often mix up "tertiary" and "quaternary" structures. *Tertiary* = one chain folded; *Quaternary* = multiple chains assembled.
> 2. **Forgetting the H-bond direction:** Many write "α-helix H-bond is between residue n and n+3" — *wrong*; it is **n+4**.
> 3. **Cis vs. Trans:** Students forget that the peptide bond is *predominantly trans*; only proline allows cis.
> 4. **Disulfide bonds:** Marks lost if not mentioned that they form only in **oxidizing environments** (extracellular, secreted proteins).
> 5. **Membrane protein types:** Many students describe only integral and peripheral, forgetting **lipid-anchored** (GPI, prenylated) — frequently asked.
> 6. **Hydrophobic effect misattribution:** Students call it a "bond" — it is a **thermodynamic effect**, not a true attractive interaction.
> 7. **GPCR structure:** Forgetting that GPCRs have **7 TMDs** and that the **N-terminus is extracellular**.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 HIGH-YIELD POINTS TO REMEMBER:**

### 🔹 Primary Structure
- ✅ Linear sequence of amino acids (N → C terminus)
- ✅ Stabilized by **covalent peptide bonds** only
- ✅ Peptide bond is **planar, partial double-bond, trans (180°)**
- ✅ **Anfinsen's dogma** — sequence determines fold
- ✅ Single AA change → sickle cell anemia (HbS: Glu → Val at β6)

### 🔹 Secondary Structure
- ✅ Stabilized by **H-bonds of the backbone**
- ✅ **α-helix:** 3.6 residues/turn, 1.5 Å rise, 5.4 Å pitch, **H-bond: n → n+4**
- ✅ **β-sheet:** Parallel & Antiparallel; **3.5 Å/residue**
- ✅ **β-turn:** 4 residues; H-bond: n → n+3; Pro-Gly common
- ✅ **Proline** = helix breaker; **Glycine** = flexible disruptor

### 🔹 Tertiary Structure
- ✅ 3D fold of a single chain
- ✅ Forces: hydrophobic (dominant), H-bonds, ionic, VdW, **disulfide (covalent)**, metal-coordinate
- ✅ Defines **active site** of enzymes
- ✅ Denaturation = loss of 3° (and higher) structure

### 🔹 Quaternary Structure
- ✅ Assembly of multiple subunits
- ✅ Examples: **Hemoglobin (α₂β₂), IgG (2H+2L), virus capsids**
- ✅ Enables **allosteric regulation** and **cooperativity**
- ✅ **Homo-oligomer** (same subunits) vs. **Hetero-oligomer** (different)

### 🔹 Membrane Proteins
- ✅ **Integral (intrinsic)** — embedded, need detergents
- ✅ **Peripheral (extrinsic)** — surface-bound, water-soluble
- ✅ **Lipid-anchored** — GPI / fatty acyl / prenyl
- ✅ **GPCRs** = 7-TM receptors; largest drug target family
- ✅ **β-barrel** = outer membranes of bacteria/mitochondria

### 🔹 Quick Numerics
- ✅ Average residue MW = **110 Da**
- ✅ α-helix pitch = **5.4 Å**
- ✅ α-helix rise/residue = **1.5 Å**
- ✅ β-strand rise = **3.5 Å**
- ✅ Plasma membrane thickness ≈ **35–40 Å** (spanned by ~20–25 aa α-helix)
- ✅ Detergent CMC = critical micelle concentration

### 🔹 High-Yield Examples
| Protein | Structural Feature |
|---|---|
| **α-Keratin** | Coiled-coil α-helix (fibrous) |
| **Silk Fibroin** | Antiparallel β-sheet (fibrous) |
| **Hemoglobin** | Quaternary (α₂β₂ tetramer) |
| **Myoglobin** | Monomeric, 8 α-helices, heme |
| **Insulin** | 2 chains (A & B) with disulfide bonds |
| **Lysozyme** | First enzyme with known 3D structure |
| **GPCRs** | 7-TM receptors |
| **Cytochrome c** | Peripheral membrane protein |

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Strategy:** Focus on **diagrams** of peptide bond geometry, α-helix parameters, hemoglobin quaternary structure, and the membrane protein classification chart. KTU examiners reward well-labeled diagrams — even a partial answer with a clear sketch can score **8–9 out of 14 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nucleic Acids: Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary Structure of DNA and RNA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT302 - BIOCHEMISTRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nucleic Acids: Primary, Secondary, and Tertiary Structure of DNA and RNA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nucleic Acids** are high-molecular-weight biopolymers composed of repeating units called **nucleotides**. Each nucleotide consists of three covalently linked components: (i) a **nitrogenous base** (a purine or pyrimidine derivative), (ii) a **pentose sugar** (D-ribose in RNA or 2'-deoxy-D-ribose in DNA), and (iii) a **phosphate group** esterified to the 5'-hydroxyl of the sugar. Nucleic acids serve as the molecular repository of genetic information and direct the synthesis of proteins in all living organisms.

There are two major classes of nucleic acids:

| Class | Sugar | Bases | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DNA** (Deoxyribonucleic acid) | 2'-deoxy-D-ribose | A, G, C, T | Long-term storage of genetic information |
| **RNA** (Ribonucleic acid) | D-ribose | A, G, C, U | Messenger, adapter, catalytic and regulatory roles |

> [!NOTE]
> **Polynucleotide** is the formal name for the long chain formed when nucleotides are joined by **3'→5' phosphodiester bonds**. The directional nature of this linkage (5'→3' reading) is a defining structural feature in KTU Board Examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine that a nucleic acid is the **master architectural blueprint** of a construction company (the cell).

- The **DNA** is the **sealed master copy** stored safely in the main office (the nucleus). It is never sent out directly — only photocopies are issued.
- Each **photocopy (mRNA)** is carried by a courier (ribosome) to the workshop floor (cytoplasm), where the construction crew reads the instructions to assemble the building (protein).
- The **alphabet** of this blueprint consists of only **4 letters** (A, T/U, G, C) — yet with combinations of three letters (**codons**), 20 different "construction materials" (amino acids) can be specified.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Just as a sentence has grammar (primary structure), folding pattern (secondary structure), and 3D shape (tertiary structure), nucleic acids too exhibit all three hierarchical levels of organization.

### 1.3 Critical Constants and Metrics

- **Helical diameter of B-DNA:** **~2.0 nm** (20 Å)
- **Rise per base pair (B-DNA):** **0.34 nm** (3.4 Å)
- **Pitch of helix (B-DNA):** **3.4 nm** (10 base pairs per turn)
- **Chargaff's ratio:** **A = T** and **G ≡ C** (in double-stranded DNA)
- **Average molecular weight per base pair:** **~660 Da** (or **~1.08 × 10⁻²¹ g/bp**)
- **Molar extinction coefficient of dsDNA at 260 nm:** **~6,600 M⁻¹·cm⁻¹** per base pair
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Levels of Nucleic Acid Structure

#### 2.1.1 Primary Structure of Nucleic Acids

The **primary structure** is defined as the **linear sequence of nucleotides** linked by **3'→5' phosphodiester bonds**, conventionally written in the **5'→3' direction**.

**Components of a nucleotide:**
1. **Phosphate group** (PO₄³⁻) — confers acidic property and negative charge.
2. **Pentose sugar** — ribose (RNA) or deoxyribose (DNA).
3. **Nitrogenous base** — purines (A, G) or pyrimidines (C, T, U).

**Why this matters:** The order of bases carries the **genetic code**. A change in even one base (point mutation) can alter the protein produced — the molecular basis of genetic diseases like sickle-cell anaemia.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Purines (double ring):** Adenine (A), Guanine (G)
> **Pyrimidines (single ring):** Cytosine (C), Thymine (T), Uracil (U)

**Phosphodiester bond formation:**

$$\text{Nucleotide-3'-OH} + \text{5'-O-PO}_3\text{-Nucleotide} \xrightarrow{\text{DNA/RNA polymerase}} \text{3'-5' phosphodiester bond} + \text{PP}_i$$

The release of pyrophosphate (PPᵢ) and its subsequent hydrolysis to 2 Pᵢ makes the polymerization reaction **thermodynamically irreversible**.

#### 2.1.2 Secondary Structure of Nucleic Acids

The **secondary structure** refers to the regular, repeating 3D arrangement of the polynucleotide chain(s), most famously the **double helix** proposed by **Watson and Crick (1953)**.

**Key features of B-DNA (the physiologically relevant form):**
- **Right-handed** double helix.
- **Antiparallel** strands: one runs 5'→3', the other 3'→5'.
- **Base pairing rules:** A pairs with T via **2 hydrogen bonds**; G pairs with C via **3 hydrogen bonds**.
- **Major groove** (wide, 2.2 nm) and **minor groove** (narrow, 1.2 nm) — critical for protein recognition.
- Bases are stacked **perpendicular** to the helix axis at a distance of **0.34 nm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Chargaff's Rule (Erwin Chargaff, 1950):** In any double-stranded DNA, the mole fraction of A equals that of T, and G equals C. This was the empirical clue that led Watson and Crick to deduce base pairing.
> $$\%A = \%T \quad \text{and} \quad \%G = \%C$$
> Therefore: $$\%A + \%T + \%G + \%C = 100\%$$

**RNA secondary structures** are typically **single-stranded** but fold back on themselves to form:
- **Hairpin loops**
- **Bulges**
- **Internal loops**
- **Stem-loops**
- **Pseudoknots**

These are stabilized by **intra-strand base pairing** (A=U, G≡C, and the wobble pair G–U).

#### 2.1.3 Tertiary Structure of Nucleic Acids

The **tertiary structure** is the **overall 3D folding** of the polynucleotide chain(s) in space, including supercoiling, bending, and interactions with proteins.

**Forms of DNA tertiary structure:**

| Form | Helix Handedness | Base Pairs per Turn | Rise per bp | Major Groove | Biological Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A-DNA** | Right | 11 | 0.256 nm | Narrow, deep | Dehydrated DNA, RNA-DNA hybrids |
| **B-DNA** | Right | 10.5 | 0.34 nm | Wide | Physiological standard |
| **Z-DNA** | Left | 12 | 0.37 nm | Flat | Alternating GC sequences, regulatory roles |

**Supercoiling:** Closed circular DNA (e.g., in bacteria, mitochondria) is **supercoiled** to compactly store genetic material. The **linking number (Lk)** is conserved and given by:

$$Lk = Tw + Wr$$

where **Tw (twist)** is the number of helical turns and **Wr (writhe)** is the number of supercoils.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Chargaff ratio | $\%A = \%T, \quad \%G = \%C$ | Molar percentages |
| Total base composition | $A + T + G + C = 100\%$ | Mole percent |
| Purine = Pyrimidine | $\text{Pur} = \text{Pyr}$ | Valid for dsDNA |
| A-T hydrogen bonds | 2 per pair | Weak |
| G-C hydrogen bonds | 3 per pair | Stronger |
| GC content | $\%GC = \dfrac{G+C}{A+T+G+C} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| Melting temperature (Wallace rule) | $T_m = 2^\circ C \times (A+T) + 4^\circ C \times (G+C)$ | Approximation for short oligos |
| Melting temperature (salt-adjusted) | $T_m = 81.5 + 16.6 \log_{10}[Na^+] + 0.41 \times \%GC - \dfrac{600}{N}$ | °C, for longer DNA |
| Absorbance ratio purity | $\dfrac{A_{260}}{A_{280}} \approx 1.8$ (pure dsDNA) | Ratio |
| Hyperchromic effect | $A_{260}^{\text{denatured}} > A_{260}^{\text{native}}$ | Increase on melting |
| Linking number | $Lk = Tw + Wr$ | Topological invariant |
| Average bp mass | $1 \text{ bp} \approx 660 \text{ Da}$ | Dalton |
| Genome length (bp) | $L = \dfrac{MW \text{ (Da)}}{660}$ | Base pairs |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Wallace rule ($T_m = 2(A+T) + 4(G+C)$) is valid only for oligonucleotides **< 25 bp**. For longer DNA, always use the salt-adjusted formula. Examiners specifically test this distinction.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Primer designing** in PCR (Polymerase Chain Reaction) — requires accurate $T_m$ prediction.
- **DNA microarrays** and **biosensors** exploit the reversible nature of base pairing (hybridization).
- **Forensic DNA fingerprinting** uses STR (Short Tandem Repeat) analysis — relies on precise primary sequence information.
- **mRNA vaccine design** (e.g., COVID-19 vaccines) requires optimization of RNA secondary structure to maximize protein expression.
- **CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing** depends on the recognition of PAM sequences and complementary base pairing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Linking Number and Supercoiling

The **linking number** (Lk) is a topological property of covalently closed circular DNA. It describes how many times one strand of DNA crosses the other when the molecule is projected onto a plane.

$$Lk = Tw + Wr$$

**Step 1 — Definition of Twist (Tw):**
Twist is the total number of helical turns of the double helix. For relaxed B-DNA:

$$Tw_0 = \dfrac{N}{10.5}$$

where $N$ is the total number of base pairs. (10.5 bp per turn for B-DNA.)

**Step 2 — Definition of Writhe (Wr):**
Writhe is the number of times the helix axis crosses itself in 3D space. Wr = 0 for a relaxed planar circle.

**Step 3 — Linking number conservation:**
Topoisomerases can change Lk by cutting and rejoining DNA strands, but **Lk cannot change spontaneously** without enzyme action.

**Step 4 — Supercoiling density (σ):**

$$\sigma = \dfrac{Lk - Lk_0}{Lk_0} = \dfrac{\Delta Lk}{Lk_0}$$

For most bacterial DNAs, $\sigma \approx -0.06$ (negatively supercoiled), which facilitates strand separation during replication and transcription.

**Example Calculation:**
Consider a plasmid of **5,000 bp**. Find relaxed Lk, Lk when 25 supercoils are introduced, and the supercoiling density.

**Solution:**

$$Lk_0 = \dfrac{5000}{10.5} \approx 476.19$$

$$\Delta Lk = -25 \text{ (negative supercoiling)}$$

$$Lk = 476.19 - 25 = 451.19$$

$$\sigma = \dfrac{-25}{476.19} \approx -0.0525$$

This corresponds to a moderately negatively supercoiled plasmid, typical of *E. coli* plasmids.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Melting Temperature (Tm)

The melting temperature ($T_m$) is the temperature at which **50%** of the DNA double helix dissociates into single strands.

**Method A — Wallace Rule (for oligos ≤ 25 bp):**

$$T_m = 2^\circ C \cdot (A + T) + 4^\circ C \cdot (G + C)$$

**Example:** A 20-mer primer with 8 A-T pairs and 12 G-C pairs.

**Step-by-step:**

$$T_m = 2 \times 8 + 4 \times 12 = 16 + 48 = 64^\circ C$$

**Method B — Salt-Adjusted (for long DNA):**

$$T_m = 81.5 + 16.6 \log_{10}[Na^+] + 0.41 \cdot \%GC - \dfrac{600}{N}$$

**Example:** A DNA of 1,000 bp with 50% GC content in 0.1 M Na⁺.

**Step-by-step substitution:**

$$T_m = 81.5 + 16.6 \log_{10}(0.1) + 0.41 \times 50 - \dfrac{600}{1000}$$

$$T_m = 81.5 + 16.6 \times (-1) + 20.5 - 0.6$$

$$T_m = 81.5 - 16.6 + 20.5 - 0.6 = 84.8^\circ C$$

This is the typical $T_m$ of a bacterial chromosome with 50% GC content.

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Chargaff's Rule Application

Given a DNA sample with **30% Adenine**, determine the percentage of all other bases.

**Step 1:** By Chargaff's rule:
$$\%A = \%T$$
$$\%T = 30\%$$

**Step 2:** Calculate %G + %C:
$$\%G + \%C = 100 - (\%A + \%T) = 100 - (30 + 30) = 40\%$$

**Step 3:** By Chargaff's second rule:
$$\%G = \%C$$
$$\%G = \%C = 20\%$$

**Verification:**
$$\%A + \%T + \%G + \%C = 30 + 30 + 20 + 20 = 100\% \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Hydrogen Bonding in Base Pairs

**Adenine–Thymine (A–T) base pair:**

A–T forms **2 hydrogen bonds**:

- Bond 1: A(N6-H) ··· O=C(T) — donor–acceptor.
- Bond 2: A(N1) ··· H-N(T)(N3) — acceptor–donor.

$$\text{A} \underset{\text{H-bond 1}}{\rightleftarrows} \text{T} \quad \text{(H-bond 2)}$$

**Guanine–Cytosine (G–C) base pair:**

G–C forms **3 hydrogen bonds**:

- Bond 1: G(O6) ··· H-N(C)(N4).
- Bond 2: G(N1-H) ··· N(C)(N3).
- Bond 3: G(N2-H) ··· O=C(C)(N2).

$$\text{G} \underset{3 \text{ H-bonds}}{\rightleftarrows} \text{C}$$

**Stacking energy contribution (per bp):**

$$\Delta G_{\text{stack}} \approx -2 \text{ to } -10 \text{ kJ/mol (at 37}^\circ\text{C)}$$

$$\Delta G_{\text{AT pair}} \approx -7.0 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$\Delta G_{\text{GC pair}} \approx -14.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

This explains why **GC-rich DNA has higher $T_m$** than AT-rich DNA.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: DNA vs. RNA — Side-by-Side

| Feature | DNA | RNA |
|---|---|---|
| Sugar | 2'-deoxy-D-ribose | D-ribose |
| Bases | A, G, C, T | A, G, C, U |
| Strand(s) | Usually double | Usually single |
| Helix | B-form (right-handed) | A-form (single strand folds) |
| Base pairing | A=T, G≡C | A=U, G≡C, G–U (wobble) |
