| Stability | Highly stable (no 2'-OH) | Less stable (2'-OH vulnerable to hydrolysis) |
| Location | Nucleus, mitochondria | Nucleus, cytoplasm, ribosome |
| Function | Genetic storage | Coding, decoding, catalysis, regulation |
| Alkaline hydrolysis | Resistant | Susceptible (2'-OH attacks 3'-5' bond) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchy of Nucleic Acid Structure (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Primary Structure] --> N2[Secondary Structure]
    N2 --> N3[Tertiary Structure]
    N1a[Nucleotide Sequence 5 to 3] --> N1
    N2a[Double Helix Watson Crick 1953] --> N2
    N3a[Supercoiling and Histone Binding] --> N3
    N1b[Phosphodiester Bond Backbone] --> N1a
    N1c[Sugar Phosphate Chain] --> N1b
    N2b[Base Pairing A-T and G-C] --> N2a
    N2c[Antiparallel Strands] --> N2b
    N3b[A-DNA B-DNA Z-DNA Forms] --> N3a
    N3c[Nucleosome Packaging] --> N3b
```

### 4.2 DNA Double Helix Architecture (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Strand 1 runs 5 to 3] --> B1[Antiparallel Orientation]
    A2[Strand 2 runs 3 to 5] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Major Groove Width 2.2 nm]
    B1 --> C2[Minor Groove Width 1.2 nm]
    B1 --> D1[Base Pairing Hydrogen Bonds]
    D1 --> E1[A-T pair 2 H-bonds]
    D1 --> E2[G-C pair 3 H-bonds]
    D1 --> F1[Helical Diameter 2.0 nm]
    D1 --> F2[Rise per bp 0.34 nm]
    D1 --> F3[10.5 bp per turn]
```

### 4.3 Central Dogma Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[DNA in Nucleus] --> P2[Transcription]
    P2 --> P3[Pre mRNA]
    P3 --> P4[5 Capping and 3 Poly A Tail]
    P4 --> P5[Mature mRNA]
    P5 --> P6[Export to Cytoplasm]
    P6 --> P7[Translation at Ribosome]
    P7 --> P8[Polypeptide Chain]
    P8 --> P9[Functional Protein]
    P9 --> P10[Cellular Phenotype]
```

### 4.4 RNA Types and Their Tertiary Folds

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[RNA Polymer Types] --> R1[mRNA Linear Coding]
    R0 --> R2[tRNA Cloverleaf L shaped]
    R0 --> R3[rRNA Highly Folded Ribozyme]
    R0 --> R4[snRNA Small Nuclear Spliceosome]
    R0 --> R5[miRNA Short Regulatory Duplex]
    R0 --> R6[lncRNA Long Non Coding Scaffold]
    R2 --> R2a[Acceptor Stem with CCA 3 end]
    R2 --> R2b[Anticodon Loop Reads Codon]
    R3 --> R3a[Peptidyl Transferase Center]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Interpretation:** The hierarchy flows unidirectionally. Each level of organization depends on the integrity of the previous level. A single-base mutation in primary structure (DNA level) can ripple up to alter tertiary structure (protein folding) and ultimately the phenotype.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define primary structure of nucleic acids. Differentiate between the sugars present in DNA and RNA.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **primary structure of nucleic acids** is the linear sequence of nucleotides linked together by **3'→5' phosphodiester bonds**, conventionally written in the **5'→3' direction**. The nucleotide sequence carries the genetic information.

**Difference between sugars:**

| Feature | DNA | RNA |
|---|---|---|
| Sugar | 2'-deoxy-D-ribose | D-ribose |
| 2' position | -H (no hydroxyl) | -OH (hydroxyl) |

The presence of 2'-OH in RNA makes it more susceptible to alkaline hydrolysis than DNA. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*State Chargaff's rule. A double-stranded DNA contains 22% Adenine. Calculate the percentage of all other bases.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Chargaff's Rule:** In double-stranded DNA, the molar amount of adenine equals thymine, and guanine equals cytosine.

$$\%A = \%T \quad \text{and} \quad \%G = \%C$$

Given: **%A = 22%**, therefore **%T = 22%**.

$$\%G + \%C = 100 - (22 + 22) = 56\%$$

$$\%G = \%C = 28\%$$

**Final answer:** A = 22%, T = 22%, G = 28%, C = 28%. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Describe the Watson-Crick model of DNA double helix. List any **seven salient features** of B-DNA. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the structural features of **A-DNA, B-DNA, and Z-DNA** with suitable diagrams. Discuss the biological significance of each form. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Watson-Crick Model of DNA Double Helix (7 marks):**

In **1953**, James Watson and Francis Crick, based on X-ray diffraction data of Rosalind Franklin and Chargaff's rules, proposed the double helix model of DNA.

**Seven Salient Features of B-DNA:**

1. **Right-handed double helix** with two antiparallel polynucleotide chains.
2. **Sugar-phosphate backbone** on the outside, bases on the inside.
3. **Base pairing rules:** A pairs with T (2 hydrogen bonds); G pairs with C (3 hydrogen bonds).
4. **Helical diameter:** **2.0 nm**.
5. **Rise per base pair:** **0.34 nm**.
6. **Base pairs per turn:** **10.5**.
7. **Pitch of helix:** **3.4 nm**.
8. Presence of **major groove** (2.2 nm) and **minor groove** (1.2 nm) — important for protein binding.
9. Bases are nearly **perpendicular** to the helix axis; planes of bases are stacked by **π-π hydrophobic interactions**.

**(b) Comparison of A, B, Z-DNA (7 marks):**

| Parameter | A-DNA | B-DNA | Z-DNA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Helix type | Right-handed | Right-handed | Left-handed |
| bp per turn | 11 | 10.5 | 12 |
| Rise per bp | 0.256 nm | 0.34 nm | 0.37 nm |
| Major groove | Narrow, deep | Wide | Flat |
| Sugar pucker | C3'-endo | C2'-endo | Alternating |
| Glycosidic bond | Anti | Anti | Alternating syn-anti |
| Conditions | Dehydrated, RNA-DNA hybrids | Physiological | Alternating GC, high salt |

**Biological Significance:**

- **B-DNA** is the predominant physiological form under cellular conditions.
- **A-DNA** is favoured in dehydrated conditions and forms RNA-DNA hybrids during transcription.
- **Z-DNA** is induced by alternating purine-pyrimidine sequences (CpG repeats), methylated cytosines, and negative supercoiling. It plays roles in **gene regulation, chromatin remodelling, and innate immunity**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently confuse "rise per base pair" with "pitch of helix". **Pitch = Rise × (base pairs per turn)**. Writing **3.4 nm** for the rise will cost 1 mark; correct value is **0.34 nm**.

---

#### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **DNA supercoiling**. Derive the relationship between linking number, twist, and writhe. Calculate the supercoiling density of a 6,000 bp plasmid with 35 negative supercoils. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **primary, secondary, and tertiary structures of RNA** with special reference to **tRNA**. Draw and explain the **cloverleaf structure**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DNA Supercoiling and Linking Number (7 marks):**

**Concept:** Supercoiling is the additional twisting of the DNA double helix upon itself, beyond the relaxed state. It is essential for compact packaging of DNA in cells (e.g., nucleoids in bacteria, chromatin in eukaryotes).

**Types of supercoiling:**
- **Negative supercoiling** (underwinding, default in cells) — favours strand separation.
- **Positive supercoiling** (overwinding) — generated ahead of replication forks.

**Derivation of Lk = Tw + Wr:**

For a covalently closed circular DNA (cccDNA), the linking number (Lk) is a topological invariant:

$$Lk = Tw + Wr$$

where:
- **Tw** = total helical turns (twist) = N / 10.5 for relaxed B-DNA.
- **Wr** = number of supercoils (writhe).

**Calculations for 6,000 bp plasmid:**

$$Lk_0 = \dfrac{6000}{10.5} = 571.43$$

$$\Delta Lk = -35$$

$$Lk = 571.43 - 35 = 536.43$$

$$\sigma = \dfrac{\Delta Lk}{Lk_0} = \dfrac{-35}{571.43} \approx -0.0613$$

This is close to the physiological supercoiling density of *E. coli* DNA ($\sigma \approx -0.06$).

**(b) RNA Structure and tRNA Cloverleaf (7 marks):**

**Primary structure:** Linear sequence of ribonucleotides joined by 3'→5' phosphodiester bonds. RNA contains **uracil** instead of thymine and has **2'-OH groups**.

**Secondary structure:** Single-stranded RNA folds back to form intra-strand **stem-loop (hairpin)** structures stabilized by A-U and G-C base pairs. Common motifs: hairpins, bulges, internal loops, pseudoknots.

**Tertiary structure:** The 3D folding of RNA, stabilized by:
- Base stacking.
- Non-Watson-Crick interactions (e.g., base triples).
- Metal ion (Mg²⁺) coordination.
- Tertiary contacts between distant loops.

**tRNA — The Cloverleaf Model:**

tRNA is a small (~76 nt) adapter molecule that delivers amino acids to the ribosome.

**Features of the Cloverleaf:**

1. **Acceptor stem** (7 bp): 5'-end and 3'-end with **CCA-3'** sequence; the amino acid attaches to the 3'-OH of the terminal adenosine.
2. **D-arm** (D = dihydrouridine): contains modified base dihydrouridine.
3. **Anticodon arm**: contains the **anticodon loop** (3 nt) that base-pairs with the mRNA codon.
4. **TΨC arm**: contains the modified base **pseudouridine (Ψ)** and ribothymidine.
5. **Variable loop**: length varies between tRNAs.

In the **L-shaped tertiary structure**, the acceptor stem and the anticodon loop are positioned at opposite ends of the molecule (~7 nm apart), which fits perfectly into the ribosome's A, P, and E sites.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often write **Lk = Tw × Wr** instead of **Lk = Tw + Wr**. This is a **topological equation**, not a multiplication. Losing this sign will cost **2 marks**. Also remember that for **relaxed DNA**, $Wr = 0$, so $Lk = Tw$.

### 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU Board Exam Ready):**

- **Nucleotide = phosphate + pentose sugar + nitrogenous base.**
- **DNA sugar** = 2'-deoxy-D-ribose; **RNA sugar** = D-ribose (has 2'-OH).
- **DNA bases** = A, G, C, T; **RNA bases** = A, G, C, U.
- **Purines** (A, G) have a **double ring**; **Pyrimidines** (C, T, U) have a **single ring**.
- **3'→5' phosphodiester bonds** link nucleotides; sequence is written **5'→3'**.
- **Chargaff's rule:** A = T, G = C in double-stranded DNA.
- **Watson-Crick base pairing:** A=T (2 H-bonds), G≡C (3 H-bonds).
- **B-DNA:** right-handed, 10.5 bp/turn, **0.34 nm rise/bp**, **2.0 nm diameter**, major groove wide.
- **A-DNA:** dehydrated, RNA-DNA hybrids, 11 bp/turn.
- **Z-DNA:** left-handed, alternating GC, 12 bp/turn, regulatory roles.
- **Chargaff ratio math:** Given %A, %T = %A; %G = %C = (100 − 2A)/2.
- **Wallace $T_m$ formula:** $T_m = 2(A+T) + 4(G+C)$, valid for oligos < 25 bp.
- **Salt-adjusted $T_m$:** $T_m = 81.5 + 16.6\log[Na^+] + 0.41(\%GC) − 600/N$.
- **Linking number:** $Lk = Tw + Wr$ (topological invariant).
- **Supercoiling density:** $\sigma = (Lk − Lk_0) / Lk_0$; physiological σ ≈ −0.06.
- **A₂₆₀/A₂₈₀ ratio** for pure dsDNA = **1.8**; for pure RNA = **2.0**.
- **Hyperchromic effect:** denatured DNA absorbs more UV at 260 nm than native DNA.
- **RNA secondary structures:** hairpin, bulge, internal loop, pseudoknot.
- **tRNA cloverleaf:** acceptor stem, D-arm, anticodon arm, TΨC arm, variable loop.
- **L-shaped tertiary tRNA:** acceptor end and anticodon loop ~7 nm apart.
- **Average MW per bp:** **660 Da**; genome length (bp) = MW (Da) / 660.
- **Alkaline hydrolysis:** RNA is alkali-labile (2'-OH attacks); DNA is alkali-stable.
- **Central dogma:** DNA → RNA → Protein (replication, transcription, translation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip:** For KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, expect at least one question linking **Chargaff's rule + UV absorbance + $T_m$ calculation** and another on **Watson-Crick model + comparison of DNA forms**. Practice numerical problems thoroughly — they carry guaranteed marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metabolism of Biomolecules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT302 - BIOCHEMISTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Metabolism of Biomolecules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Metabolism** is the complete set of **enzyme-catalyzed biochemical reactions** occurring within a living cell, which sustain life by enabling the organism to grow, reproduce, maintain its structural integrity, and respond to external stimuli. It encompasses the **interconversion of biomolecules** (carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids) and the **transduction of energy** through coupled catabolic (degradative) and anabolic (biosynthetic) pathways.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with the Biochemistry syllabus, *metabolism* refers to the **highly coordinated network of metabolic pathways** that govern the flux of matter and energy in biological systems. The study of metabolism in PCBBT302 emphasizes **pathway mapping, energetics, regulation, and the integration of metabolic routes** across different tissues.

### 1.2 Two Arms of Metabolism

| Branch | Definition | Energy Flow | KTU Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Catabolism** | Degradation of complex molecules into simpler ones | **Exergonic** ($\Delta G < 0$), releases energy | Glycolysis, $\beta$-Oxidation, Proteolysis |
| **Anabolism** | Synthesis of complex molecules from simpler precursors | **Endergonic** ($\Delta G > 0$), consumes energy | Gluconeogenesis, Fatty Acid Synthesis, Protein Synthesis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Students must clearly distinguish **catabolism vs anabolism** as this forms a frequent 3-mark question and is a foundational concept for pathway-based numerical problems on ATP yield.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Factory and Warehouse" Analogy**
> Imagine a city as a living cell. **Catabolism** is like the **power plant** — it breaks down delivered fuel (food) to generate electricity (ATP) and waste (CO₂ + H₂O). **Anabolism** is like the **construction crew** — it uses that electricity (ATP) and raw materials (intermediates from catabolism) to build new structures (proteins, glycogen, lipids). The two arms are connected by a **common currency (ATP, NADH, NADPH)** that flows between them. Without the power plant, construction halts; without construction, the city cannot grow.

### 1.4 Key Physical & Biochemical Constants

- **Standard Free Energy of ATP Hydrolysis:** $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} \approx$ **−30.5 kJ/mol** (−7.3 kcal/mol)
- **Cellular [ATP]/[ADP] ratio:** Maintained around **10:1** to keep phosphorylation potential high
- **Standard physiological pH:** **7.4**
- **Body temperature (core):** **310 K (37 °C)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Central Pathways of Carbohydrate Metabolism

#### A. Glycolysis (Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas Pathway)

**Definition:** A universal **10-step cytosolic pathway** that converts one molecule of **glucose (C₆)** into **two molecules of pyruvate (C₃)**, with the net production of **2 ATP and 2 NADH**.

**Why it matters:** Glycolysis is the **entry point** for all carbohydrate catabolism and operates in the absence of oxygen (anaerobic) or in the presence of oxygen (aerobic), making it central to all life.

**How it works — Operative Logic Steps:**

1. **Investment Phase (Steps 1–5):** 2 ATP consumed to phosphorylate and split glucose into two triose phosphates (G3P and DHAP).
2. **Payoff Phase (Steps 6–10):** Each triose is oxidized and phosphorylated, yielding **4 ATP (substrate-level) and 2 NADH**.
3. **Net Yield:** $4 - 2 = 2$ ATP and 2 NADH per glucose.

#### B. TCA (Krebs / Citric Acid) Cycle

**Definition:** An **8-step mitochondrial matrix cycle** that completely oxidizes **acetyl-CoA** into **2 CO₂**, generating **3 NADH, 1 FADH₂, and 1 GTP (≈ATP)** per turn.

**Why it matters:** It is the **final common oxidative pathway** for carbohydrates, fatty acids (via acetyl-CoA), and amino acids (via keto-acid entry points). It links catabolism to the electron transport chain.

#### C. Gluconeogenesis

**Definition:** The **anabolic synthesis of glucose** from non-carbohydrate precursors (lactate, glycerol, glucogenic amino acids). Occurs primarily in **liver and kidneys**.

**Why it matters:** Maintains **blood glucose homeostasis** during fasting/starvation (between meals).

**How it works — Bypass Reactions (the 3 irreversible steps of glycolysis that are circumvented):**

1. **Pyruvate → Phosphoenolpyruvate (PEP):** Via **Pyruvate Carboxylase (PC)** + **PEP Carboxykinase (PEPCK)** — consumes 2 ATP equivalents.
2. **Fructose-1,6-bisphosphate → Fructose-6-phosphate:** Via **Fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase (FBPase-1)** — hydrolytic, no ATP consumed.
3. **Glucose-6-phosphate → Glucose:** Via **Glucose-6-phosphatase** — hydrolytic.

#### D. Glycogenesis & Glycogenolysis

| Process | Definition | Key Enzyme | KTU Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Glycogenesis** | Synthesis of **glycogen** from UDP-glucose | **Glycogen Synthase** (rate-limiting) | Energy storage, post-prandial |
| **Glycogenolysis** | Breakdown of glycogen to glucose-1-phosphate | **Glycogen Phosphorylase** (rate-limiting) | Fasting blood glucose maintenance |

### 2.2 Lipid Metabolism

#### A. $\beta$-Oxidation of Fatty Acids

**Definition:** The **mitochondrial catabolic pathway** that sequentially removes **2-carbon units (acetyl-CoA)** from the **$\beta$-carbon** of a fatty acyl-CoA.

**Why it matters:** Major source of energy during **fasting, prolonged exercise, and ketogenic states**.

**Step-by-step cycle per 2-carbon unit:**
1. **Oxidation** by Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase → produces **1 FADH₂**
2. **Hydration** by Enoyl-CoA Hydratase
3. **Oxidation** by 3-Hydroxyacyl-CoA Dehydrogenase → produces **1 NADH**
4. **Thiolysis** by $\beta$-Ketothiolase → releases **1 Acetyl-CoA**

#### B. Fatty Acid Synthesis

**Definition:** The **cytosolic anabolic pathway** that builds **palmitate (C16:0)** from **8 molecules of acetyl-CoA**, using **NADPH** as the reducing equivalent.

**Key System:** Catalyzed by the multifunctional enzyme **Fatty Acid Synthase (FAS)** as a homodimer in animals.

### 2.3 Protein / Amino Acid Metabolism

#### A. Transamination

**Definition:** The **reversible transfer of an amino group** from an amino acid to an $\alpha$-keto acid, forming a new amino acid and a new keto acid. Catalyzed by **aminotransferases (transaminases)** with **pyridoxal phosphate (PLP)** as cofactor.

$$\text{Amino Acid}_1 + \alpha\text{-Keto Acid}_2 \rightleftharpoons \alpha\text{-Keto Acid}_1 + \text{Amino Acid}_2$$

#### B. Oxidative Deamination

**Definition:** Removal of the amino group as **free ammonia (NH₃/NH₄⁺)** via **glutamate dehydrogenase**, producing $\alpha$-ketoglutarate and NAD(P)H.

#### C. Urea Cycle (Ornithine Cycle)

**Definition:** The **hepatic mitochondrial + cytosolic cycle** that converts **2 NH₃ + 1 CO₂** into **urea (NH₂–CO–NH₂)** for nitrogen excretion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Fact:** The urea cycle consumes **4 high-energy phosphate bonds** (2 ATP → 2 ADP + 2 Pi, with one ATP further converted to AMP + PPi), making it energetically costly.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes every formula a KTU 2024 student must memorize for numerical and short-answer problems. Note: vertical bars use `\vert` for absolute values to preserve table syntax.

| Parameter / Equation | Mathematical Form | Numerical Value | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ATP yield per glucose (aerobic)** | $2_{\text{(glycolysis)}} + 2_{\text{(TCA)}} + 2\times 2.5_{\text{(NADH)}} + 2\times 1.5_{\text{(FADH}_2)}$ | **30 or 32 ATP** | Older textbook: 36/38 ATP |
| **Net ATP from glycolysis** | $4 - 2$ | **2 ATP + 2 NADH** | Cytosolic |
| **ATP per acetyl-CoA in TCA** | $3 \times 2.5 + 1.5 + 1$ | **10 ATP equivalents** | Modern P/O ratio |
| **ATP per cycle of $\beta$-oxidation** | $1 \times 1.5 + 1 \times 2.5 + 1 \times 10$ | **14 ATP per acetyl-CoA** | Subtract 2 for activation |
| **Total ATP from palmitate (C16)** | $8 \text{ cycles} \rightarrow 8 \times 14 - 2$ | **106 ATP** | Modern estimate |
| **Urea cycle energy cost** | $2 \text{ ATP} + 1 \text{ ATP (as AMP)}$ | **4 high-energy bonds** | In liver |
| **Glycolysis reactions consuming ATP** | Steps 1, 3 | Hexokinase, PFK-1 | Irreversible |
| **Glycolysis reactions producing ATP** | Steps 7, 10 | PGK, PK | Substrate-level phosphorylation |
| **Free energy of ATP hydrolysis** | $\Delta G^{\circ\prime}_{\text{ATP}}$ | **−30.5 kJ/mol** | Standard biochemical conditions |
| **Moles of NADH per acetyl-CoA in TCA** | 3 NADH | 3 | Mitochondrial |
| **P/O ratio (modern)** | NADH $\rightarrow$ 2.5 ATP; FADH₂ $\rightarrow$ 1.5 ATP | — | Reflects actual H⁺/ATP stoichiometry |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Biotechnology

> [!TIP]
> **Why Engineers Study Metabolism:**
> 1. **Bioprocess Engineering:** Designing bioreactors requires balancing the **ATP/NADH demand** of microbial cell factories producing insulin or biofuels.
> 2. **Metabolic Flux Analysis (MFA):** Engineers use stoichiometric models and $^{13}\text{C}$ tracing to **redesign metabolism** for overproduction of amino acids (lysine, glutamate) in *Corynebacterium glutamicum*.
> 3. **Biomedical Diagnostics:** Elevated **ALT/AST (transaminases)** signal liver damage — a direct clinical readout of protein catabolism.
> 4. **Pharmaceutical Design:** Enzyme inhibitors (e.g., **statins** targeting HMG-CoA reductase, **metformin** targeting complex I) are designed using metabolic pathway knowledge.
> 5. **Biofuel Production:** Engineering $\beta$-oxidation in reverse to produce **biodiesel precursors** (fatty acid ethyl esters).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Total ATP Yield from Complete Oxidation of One Glucose Molecule

**Problem Statement:** Using the modern P/O ratios (NADH $\rightarrow$ 2.5 ATP, FADH₂ $\rightarrow$ 1.5 ATP), calculate the total ATP molecules generated from the aerobic oxidation of 1 mole of glucose through **Glycolysis → Pyruvate Dehydrogenase → TCA Cycle → Oxidative Phosphorylation**.

#### Step 1: Glycolysis — Cytosolic Phase

Tracking the reduced cofactors and ATP directly formed:

$$\text{Glucose} + 2 \text{ NAD}^+ + 2 \text{ ADP} + 2 \text{P}_i \rightarrow 2 \text{ Pyruvate} + 2 \text{ NADH} + 2 \text{ ATP} + 2 \text{ H}_2\text{O} + 2 \text{ H}^+$$

**Explanation:** Glucose is split into two pyruvate molecules. Two molecules of NAD⁺ are reduced to NADH at the **glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase** step (Step 6). A net of 2 ATP is formed (4 produced in payoff phase − 2 consumed in investment phase).

#### Step 2: Transport of Cytosolic NADH into Mitochondria

The 2 NADH produced in glycolysis are in the **cytosol** and must be shuttled into the mitochondrial matrix. Two shuttle mechanisms exist:

- **Malate-Aspartate Shuttle (liver, heart, kidney):** Transfers electrons with no loss → yields **2 × 2.5 = 5 ATP**.
- **Glycerol-3-Phosphate Shuttle (muscle, brain):** Transfers electrons via FAD → yields **2 × 1.5 = 3 ATP**.

For KTU calculations, the **malate-aspartate shuttle** is typically assumed unless stated otherwise, giving 5 ATP.

#### Step 3: Pyruvate Dehydrogenase Complex

Each of the 2 pyruvates is decarboxylated and oxidized to acetyl-CoA:

$$2 \text{ Pyruvate} + 2 \text{ NAD}^+ + 2 \text{ CoA-SH} \rightarrow 2 \text{ Acetyl-CoA} + 2 \text{ CO}_2 + 2 \text{ NADH}$$

**NADH produced:** 2 molecules (in mitochondrial matrix).

#### Step 4: TCA Cycle — Per Acetyl-CoA

One turn of the TCA cycle produces:

$$\text{Acetyl-CoA} + 3 \text{ NAD}^+ + \text{FAD} + \text{ADP} + \text{P}_i + 2 \text{ H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2 \text{ CO}_2 + 3 \text{ NADH} + \text{FADH}_2 + \text{ GTP} + 3 \text{ H}^+ + \text{CoA-SH}$$

**For 2 acetyl-CoA (from 1 glucose):**
- 6 NADH
- 2 FADH₂
- 2 GTP (energetically equivalent to 2 ATP)

#### Step 5: Oxidative Phosphorylation — Modern P/O Ratios

| Source | Number of Reduced Cofactors | ATP per Cofactor | Total ATP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Glycolytic NADH (via malate-aspartate) | 2 | 2.5 | 5 |
| Pyruvate dehydrogenase NADH | 2 | 2.5 | 5 |
| TCA NADH | 6 | 2.5 | 15 |
| TCA FADH₂ | 2 | 1.5 | 3 |
| Substrate-level (glycolysis) | — | — | 2 |
| Substrate-level (TCA, GTP) | — | — | 2 |

#### Step 6: Final Summation

$$\text{Total ATP} = 5 + 5 + 15 + 3 + 2 + 2$$

$$\boxed{\text{Total ATP} = 32 \text{ molecules per mole of glucose}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** If the glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle is used (e.g., in skeletal muscle under KTU assumptions), the total becomes **30 ATP** instead of 32 ATP. KTU examiners frequently ask both values to test shuttle-mechanism awareness.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: ATP Yield from $\beta$-Oxidation of Palmitic Acid (C16:0)

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the total number of ATP molecules generated by the complete oxidation of **palmitic acid (C16:0)** via $\beta$-oxidation, TCA cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation.

#### Step 1: Activation of Fatty Acid

Before entering mitochondria, palmitic acid is activated to **palmitoyl-CoA** in the cytosol:

$$\text{Palmitic Acid} + \text{CoA-SH} + \text{ATP} \rightarrow \text{Palmitoyl-CoA} + \text{AMP} + \text{PP}_i$$

This consumes the equivalent of **2 ATP** (since ATP → AMP + PPi is equivalent to hydrolyzing 2 high-energy bonds).

#### Step 2: Number of $\beta$-Oxidation Cycles

For a fatty acid with $n$ carbons, the number of cycles is:

$$\text{Number of cycles} = \frac{n}{2} - 1$$

For palmitic acid ($n = 16$):

$$\text{Cycles} = \frac{16}{2} - 1 = 8 - 1 = 7$$

#### Step 3: Products of $\beta$-Oxidation

Each cycle produces **1 NADH + 1 FADH₂ + 1 Acetyl-CoA**. The final cleavage releases the **last 2 acetyl-CoA** without producing NADH/FADH₂.

**Total products from 7 cycles + final cleavage:**
- NADH: $7 \times 1 = 7$
- FADH₂: $7 \times 1 = 7$
- Acetyl-CoA: $8$ molecules

#### Step 4: ATP Equivalents from Oxidative Phosphorylation

$$\text{ATP from NADH} = 7 \times 2.5 = 17.5$$

$$\text{ATP from FADH}_2 = 7 \times 1.5 = 10.5$$

#### Step 5: ATP from Acetyl-CoA via TCA Cycle

Each acetyl-CoA through TCA yields the equivalent of **10 ATP** (3 NADH × 2.5 + 1 FADH₂ × 1.5 + 1 GTP).

$$\text{ATP from TCA} = 8 \times 10 = 80$$

#### Step 6: Final Summation

$$\text{Total ATP} = 80 + 17.5 + 10.5 - 2 \text{ (activation cost)}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Total ATP} = 106 \text{ molecules per mole of palmitate}}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Regulation of Key Metabolic Pathways

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is essential for KTU 14-mark questions asking "Discuss the regulation of glycolysis/gluconeogenesis." Examiners award marks specifically for stating **effectors, mechanism (allosteric vs covalent), and hormonal context.**

| Pathway | Key Enzyme | Activators | Inhibitors | Hormonal Control |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Glycolysis** | Phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1) | AMP, Fructose-2,6-BP | ATP, Citrate, H⁺ | Insulin ↑ |
| **Gluconeogenesis** | Fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase | ATP, Citrate | AMP, Fructose-2,6-BP | Glucagon ↑, Cortisol ↑ |
| **TCA Cycle** | Isocitrate Dehydrogenase | ADP, Ca²⁺ | ATP, NADH | — |
| **Glycogenesis** | Glycogen Synthase | Glucose-6-P, Insulin | Glucagon (via PKA) | Insulin ↑ |
| **Glycogenolysis** | Glycogen Phosphorylase | AMP, Ca²⁺, Glucagon | ATP, Glucose-6-P | Glucagon ↑, Epinephrine ↑ |
| **$\beta$-Oxidation** | Carnitine Palmitoyltransferase-I (CPT-I) | — | Malonyl-CoA | Glucagon ↑, Insulin ↓ |
| **Fatty Acid Synthesis** | Acetyl-CoA Carboxylase (ACC) | Citrate, Insulin | Palmitoyl-CoA, Glucagon | Insulin ↑ |
| **Urea Cycle** | Carbamoyl Phosphate Synthetase-I | N-Acetylglutamate (NAG) | — | High [NH₃] |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Integrated Metabolic Map

The diagram below provides a **schematic overview of how catabolic and anabolic pathways interconnect** through central intermediates (Acetyl-CoA, Pyruvate, $\alpha$-Ketoglutarate, etc.) and energy currencies (ATP, NADH, NADPH). This is a critical visual aid for KTU long-answer questions on "Integration of Metabolism."

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputA[Food Intake: Carbohydrates] --> glyco[Glycolysis]
    inputB[Food Intake: Lipids] --> lipol[Beta Oxidation]
    inputC[Food Intake: Proteins] --> protAA[Amino Acid Catabolism]
    glyco --> pyr[Pyruvate]
    pyr --> pdh[Pyruvate Dehydrogenase]
    pdh --> accoa[Acetyl CoA]
    lipol --> accoa
    protAA --> accoa
    protAA --> aket[Alpha Ketoglutarate]
    accoa --> tca[TCA Cycle]
    aket --> tca
    tca --> nadh1[NADH FADH2 Pool]
    pdh --> nadh1
    glyco --> nadh1
    nadh1 --> etc[Electron Transport Chain]
    etc --> atp1[ATP Generation via Oxidative Phosphorylation]
    atp1 --> energy[ATP Energy Currency]
    energy --> glneo[Gluconeogenesis]
    energy --> fas[Fatty Acid Synthesis]
    energy --> protS[Protein Synthesis]
    energy --> glygS[Glycogenesis]
    pyr --> glneo
    glneo --> glucoseG[Blood Glucose Maintenance]
    glygS --> glycogen[Stored Glycogen in Liver]
    fas --> lipidD[Stored Triglycerides in Adipose]
    tca --> urea[Urea Cycle]
    protAA --> urea
    urea --> excrete[Nitrogen Excretion as Urea]
    energy --> maintenance[Cellular Work and Biosynthesis]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Regulation of Glycolysis vs Gluconeogenesis (Reciprocal Regulation)

This flowchart illustrates the **reciprocal control** of the two opposing pathways at the **fructose-6-phosphate / fructose-1,6-bisphosphate** substrate cycle (a KTU high-yield topic).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    f6p[Fructose 6 Phosphate] -->|PFK 1 Glycolysis| fbp[Fructose 1 6 Bisphosphate]
    fbp -->|FBPase 1 Gluconeogenesis| f6p
    amp[AMP High Energy Demand] -->|Activates| pfk1[PFK 1]
    amp -->|Inhibits| fbpase[FBPase 1]
    atpc[ATP Citrate Abundant Energy] -->|Inhibits| pfk1
    atpc -->|Activates| fbpase
    f26bpA[Fructose 2 6 Bisphosphate] -->|Strongly Activates| pfk1
    f26bpA -->|Inhibits| fbpase
    insulinH[Hormone Insulin Fed State] -->|Increases| f26bpA
    glucagonH[Hormone Glucagon Fasting] -->|Decreases| f26bpA
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: ATP Accounting from Glucose (Aerobic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    glc[1 Glucose] --> glyStep[Glycolysis]
    glyStep -->|2 ATP Substrate Level| atpCount[ATP Counter]
    glyStep -->|2 NADH Cytosolic| shuttle[Malate Aspartate Shuttle]
    shuttle -->|5 ATP| atpCount
    glyStep --> pyr2[2 Pyruvate]
    pyr2 --> pdh2[Pyruvate Dehydrogenase]
    pdh2 -->|2 NADH| atpCount
    pdh2 --> accoa2[2 Acetyl CoA]
    accoa2 --> tca2[TCA Cycle x2]
    tca2 -->|6 NADH| atpCount
    tca2 -->|2 FADH2| atpCount
    tca2 -->|2 GTP| atpCount
    atpCount --> final[Total 30 or 32 ATP per Glucose]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between catabolism and anabolism. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Catabolism | Anabolism |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Process** | Breakdown of complex molecules into simpler ones | Synthesis of complex molecules from simpler precursors |
| **Energy** | Releases energy (**exergonic**, $\Delta G < 0$) | Requires energy input (**endergonic**, $\Delta G > 0$) |
| **Redox** | Generally oxidations (loss of electrons) | Generally reductions (gain of electrons) |
| **Examples** | Glycolysis, $\beta$-Oxidation, Proteolysis | Gluconeogenesis, Fatty Acid Synthesis, Protein Synthesis |
| **Water** | Often produces H₂O | Often consumes H₂O |

**(2 examples each × 0.5 = 2 marks; 2 distinguishing features × 0.5 = 1 mark; total = 3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is the significance of ATP as the universal energy currency of the cell?**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Central Energy Hub:** ATP serves as the **common intermediate** linking energy-releasing (catabolic) and energy-consuming (anabolic) reactions. **(1 mark)**
2. **High-Energy Phosphate Bonds:** The two phosphoanhydride bonds release a standard free energy of $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -30.5$ kJ/mol upon hydrolysis, providing a usable energy quantum. **(1 mark)**
3. **Phosphoryl Group Transfer:** ATP donates its terminal phosphoryl group to drive otherwise unfavorable reactions (e.g., glucose $\rightarrow$ glucose-6-phosphate in glycolysis), enabling **kinetic coupling**. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently lose marks by **confusing cytoplasmic NADH with mitochondrial NADH** and applying the wrong P/O ratio. Always specify the shuttle mechanism used.
> - In gluconeogenesis questions, omitting any of the **three bypass reactions** (PC + PEPCK, FBPase-1, G6Pase) will cost 2 marks.
> - For $\beta$-oxidation ATP yield, many students forget to **subtract the 2 ATP activation cost** — this is a 1-mark penalty trap.
> - Pathway diagrams without **enzyme names** are marked incomplete. Always label rate-limiting/regulatory enzymes.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Alternative 1

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) Describe the glycolysis pathway. Highlight the energy-consuming and energy-yielding steps with their ATP/NADH yield. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Glycolysis is the **ten-step cytosolic catabolism of glucose (C6) into two pyruvate (C3) molecules**, discovered by **Embden, Meyerhof, and Parnas**. It is divided into two phases: the **investment phase (Steps 1–5)** and the **payoff phase (Steps 6–10)**.

**Step-by-Step Pathway with Key Enzymes:**

*Investment Phase (Energy-consuming):*

1. **Glucose → Glucose-6-phosphate** — Catalyzed by **Hexokinase** (or Glucokinase in liver); consumes **1 ATP**.
2. **Glucose-6-P → Fructose-6-P** — Catalyzed by **Phosphoglucose Isomerase**.
3. **Fructose-6-P → Fructose-1,6-bisphosphate** — Catalyzed by **Phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1)**; consumes **1 ATP** — **rate-limiting step**.
4. **Fructose-1,6-bisP → Dihydroxyacetone phosphate (DHAP) + Glyceraldehyde-3-P (G3P)** — Catalyzed by **Aldolase**.
5. **DHAP ⇌ G3P** — Catalyzed by **Triose Phosphate Isomerase**.

*Payoff Phase (Energy-yielding):*

6. **G3P → 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate** — Catalyzed by **Glyceraldehyde-3-P Dehydrogenase**; produces **1 NADH** per G3P (2 NADH per glucose).
7. **1,3-BPG → 3-Phosphoglycerate** — Catalyzed by **Phosphoglycerate Kinase**; produces **1 ATP** per G3P (2 ATP per glucose) — **substrate-level phosphorylation**.
8. **3-PG → 2-Phosphoglycerate** — Catalyzed by **Phosphoglycerate Mutase**.
9. **2-PG → Phosphoenolpyruvate (PEP)** — Catalyzed by **Enolase**; releases H₂O.
10. **PEP → Pyruvate** — Catalyzed by **Pyruvate Kinase**; produces **1 ATP** per G3P (2 ATP per glucose).

**Net Yield Summary (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{2 \text{ ATP (net)} + 2 \text{ NADH (per glucose)} \rightarrow 2 \text{ Pyruvate}}$$

**Significance:** (1 mark) Glycolysis provides **ATP and NADH for biosynthesis** and feeds pyruvate into the TCA cycle under aerobic conditions, or into **lactate/ethanol fermentation** under anaerobic conditions.

---

**(b) Calculate the total number of ATP molecules generated from the complete aerobic oxidation of one mole of glucose. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Stage 1 — Glycolysis (1 mark):**
$$\text{Net yield: } 2 \text{ ATP (substrate-level)} + 2 \text{ NADH (cytosolic)}$$

**Stage 2 — Malate-Aspartate Shuttle for NADH transport (1 mark):**
$$2 \text{ NADH} \times 2.5 \text{ ATP/NADH} = 5 \text{ ATP}$$

**Stage 3 — Pyruvate Dehydrogenase Complex (1 mark):**
$$2 \text{ Pyruvate} \rightarrow 2 \text{ Acetyl-CoA} + 2 \text{ NADH}$$
$$2 \text{ NADH} \times 2.5 = 5 \text{ ATP}$$

**Stage 4 — TCA Cycle (per acetyl-CoA) (2 marks):**
For 1 acetyl-CoA: 3 NADH + 1 FADH₂ + 1 GTP
For 2 acetyl-CoA: 6 NADH + 2 FADH₂ + 2 GTP

$$\text{ATP from NADH} = 6 \times 2.5 = 15 \text{ ATP}$$
$$\text{ATP from FADH}_2 = 2 \times 1.5 = 3 \text{ ATP}$$
$$\text{ATP from GTP} = 2 \text{ ATP}$$

**Stage 5 — Summation (1 mark):**

$$\text{Total ATP} = \underbrace{2}_{\text{glycolysis SL}} + \underbrace{5}_{\text{glycolysis NADH}} + \underbrace{5}_{\text{PDH NADH}} + \underbrace{15}_{\text{TCA NADH}} + \underbrace{3}_{\text{TCA FADH}_2} + \underbrace{2}_{\text{GTP}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Total ATP} = 32 \text{ per mole of glucose (malate-aspartate shuttle assumed)}}$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative 2

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a) Explain the $\beta$-oxidation pathway of fatty acids with the help of a diagram. Calculate the ATP yield from the complete oxidation of palmitic acid (C16:0). (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Introduction (0.5 mark):**
$\beta$-Oxidation is the **mitochondrial catabolic pathway** that sequentially cleaves **2-carbon acetyl-CoA units** from the carboxyl end of a fatty acyl-CoA, with oxidation occurring at the $\beta$-carbon. It was elucidated by **Franz Knoop (1904)**.

**Prerequisite — Activation (0.5 mark):**
The fatty acid is activated in the cytosol by **Acyl-CoA Synthetase (Thiokinase)** using **1 ATP → AMP + PPi** (cost = 2 high-energy bonds). It is then transported into the mitochondrial matrix via the **Carnitine Shuttle** (CPT-I, Carnitine-Acylcarnitine Translocase, CPT-II).

**Step-by-Step Cycle (per 2-carbon unit) — (3 marks):**

1. **Oxidation (Dehydrogenation):** Acyl-CoA → trans-Δ²-Enoyl-CoA; enzyme: **Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase**; cofactor: **FAD → FADH₂**.
2. **Hydration:** trans-Δ²-Enoyl-CoA → L-3-Hydroxyacyl-CoA; enzyme: **Enoyl-CoA Hydratase**; adds H₂O across the double bond.
3. **Oxidation:** L-3-Hydroxyacyl-CoA → 3-Ketoacyl-CoA; enzyme: **3-Hydroxyacyl-CoA Dehydrogenase**; cofactor: **NAD⁺ → NADH**.
4. **Thiolysis:** 3-Ketoacyl-CoA + CoA-SH → Acetyl-CoA + Acyl-CoA (shortened by 2C); enzyme: **β-Ketothiolase**.

**Per-cycle products: 1 FADH₂ + 1 NADH + 1 Acetyl-CoA.**

**ATP Yield Calculation (3 marks):**

For palmitate (C16:0):

**Step 1: Activation cost** = 2 ATP

**Step 2: Number of $\beta$-oxidation cycles:**
$$n = \frac{16}{2} - 1 = 7 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 3: Acetyl-CoA produced:**
$$\frac{16}{2} = 8 \text{ Acetyl-CoA}$$

**Step 4: Reduced cofactors produced:**
$$\text{NADH} = 7, \quad \text{FADH}_2 = 7$$

**Step 5: ATP equivalents:**

$$\text{From NADH} = 7 \times 2.5 = 17.5 \text{ ATP}$$
$$\text{From FADH}_2 = 7 \times 1.5 = 10.5 \text{ ATP}$$
$$\text{From 8 Acetyl-CoA via TCA} = 8 \times 10 = 80 \text{ ATP}$$

**Step 6: Final summation:**
$$\text{Total ATP} = 80 + 17.5 + 10.5 - 2$$

$$\boxed{\text{Total ATP} = 106 \text{ per mole of palmitic acid}}$$

---

**(b) Discuss the role of the TCA cycle as the central metabolic hub. List the regulatory enzymes and their effectors. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Central Role of TCA Cycle (3 marks):**

1. **Final Common Oxidative Pathway:** Carbohydrates (via acetyl-CoA), fatty acids (via $\beta$-oxidation), and amino acids (via deamination to keto acids like $\alpha$-ketoglutarate, succinyl-CoA, fumarate, oxaloacetate) all converge into the TCA cycle. **(1 mark)**
2. **Energy Production:** Each turn generates **3 NADH + 1 FADH₂ + 1 GTP**, which feed the electron transport chain for ATP synthesis. **(1 mark)**
3. **Anabolic Precursor Supply:** Intermediates are withdrawn for biosynthesis:
   - **Citrate** → Fatty acids, Cholesterol (via cytosolic acetyl-CoA)
   - **$\alpha$-Ketoglutarate** → Glutamate, then other amino acids, purines
   - **Succinyl-CoA** → Heme, Porphyrins
   - **Oxaloacetate** → Aspartate, Pyrimidines, Gluconeogenesis (via PEPCK)
   - This dual catabolic + anabolic role makes it **amphibolic**. **(1 mark)**

**Regulatory Enzymes and Effectors (4 marks — 1 mark each for two key enzymes and 0.5 each for effectors):**

| Enzyme | Activators | Inhibitors | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Citrate Synthase** | Acetyl-CoA, Oxaloacetate | ATP, NADH, Citrate (product inhibition) | Committed step |
| **Isocitrate Dehydrogenase** | ADP, Ca²⁺ | ATP, NADH | Major regulatory point |
| **$\alpha$-Ketoglutarate Dehydrogenase** | Ca²⁺ | NADH, Succinyl-CoA | Similar to PDH complex |
| **Pyruvate Dehydrogenase** (feeds cycle) | CoA, NAD⁺, ADP | Acetyl-CoA, NADH, ATP | Covalent regulation by phosphorylation |

**Key Insight (1 mark):** High-energy charge (high [ATP]/[ADP]) and high NADH inhibit the cycle, while Ca²⁺ (muscle contraction signal) and low energy activate it, demonstrating tight integration with cellular energy status.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:**

- **Definition:** Metabolism = sum of all enzymatic reactions in a cell; divided into **Catabolism (exergonic)** and **Anabolism (endergonic)**.
- **ATP:** Universal energy currency; $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -30.5$ kJ/mol; **cytosolic NADH shuttle matters** for ATP accounting.
- **Glycolysis:** 10 steps, **cytosolic**, net **2 ATP + 2 NADH**, rate-limiting enzyme = **PFK-1**.
- **PDH Complex:** Mitochondrial; links glycolysis to TCA; produces **NADH + Acetyl-CoA + CO₂**; regulated by covalent modification (phosphorylation/dephosphorylation).
- **TCA Cycle:** Mitochondrial; **amphibolic**; rate-limiting = **Isocitrate Dehydrogenase**; yields **3 NADH + 1 FADH₂ + 1 GTP** per acetyl-CoA.
- **Modern ATP Yield:** NADH = **2.5 ATP**; FADH₂ = **1.5 ATP**; glucose = **30 or 32 ATP** (depending on shuttle); palmitate = **106 ATP**.
- **Gluconeogenesis:** Occurs in **liver + kidney**; bypasses 3 irreversible glycolysis steps using **PC, PEPCK, FBPase-1, G6Pase**; consumes **4 ATP + 2 GTP equivalents per glucose**.
- **Glycogen Metabolism:** Synthase (glycogenesis) vs Phosphorylase (glycogenolysis); reciprocally regulated by **phosphorylation (PKA)**.
- **$\beta$-Oxidation:** Occurs in **mitochondria**; needs carnitine shuttle; produces **1 NADH + 1 FADH₂ + 1 Acetyl-CoA per cycle**.
- **Fatty Acid Synthesis:** Occurs in **cytosol**; uses **Acetyl-CoA Carboxylase (ACC)** as rate-limiting enzyme; requires **NADPH, ATP, Biotin**.
- **Protein Catabolism:** **Transamination (PLP-dependent)** → **Oxidative Deamination (Glutamate DH)** → **Urea Cycle (4 ATP cost)**.
- **Urea Cycle:** Two mitochondrial steps (CPS-I, OTC) + three cytosolic steps (ASS, ASL, arginase); produces **urea + fumarate** (fumarate re-enters TCA).
- **Amphibolic Intermediates:** Acetyl-CoA, $\alpha$-ketoglutarate, oxaloacetate, succinyl-CoA — central to integration of all metabolic pathways.
- **Reciprocal Regulation:** Catabolic and anabolic pathways at the same substrate cycle are **reciprocally regulated** to prevent futile cycling (e.g., PFK-1 vs FBPase-1).
- **Hormonal Master Switch:** **Insulin (fed)** activates anabolic pathways; **Glucagon/Epinephrine (fasting)** activate catabolic pathways.
- **Clinical Correlates:** ALT/AST for liver damage; elevated ammonia for urea cycle defects; ketone bodies for $\beta$-oxidation overflow (diabetes, fasting).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Metabolism: Aerobic, Anaerobic, Anabolic, and Catabolic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT302 - BIOCHEMISTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Overview of Metabolism: Aerobic, Anaerobic, Anabolic, and Catabolic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧬 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Metabolism? (The Master Definition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Metabolism** is the complete set of *enzyme-catalyzed* chemical reactions occurring within a living organism that are necessary to maintain life. These reactions allow organisms to grow, reproduce, maintain their structures, and respond to their environment. Metabolism is broadly divided into two complementary processes: **Catabolism** (breakdown) and **Anabolism** (buildup).

In simpler terms, metabolism is the **sum total of all chemical traffic inside a living cell** — every molecule being built, every molecule being broken, and every joule of energy being transferred.

## 1.2 The "Factory City" Analogy 🏙️

Imagine a **bustling industrial city** (your cell):

| Cellular Concept | City Analogy | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Catabolism** | ♻️ Waste Recycling & Demolition Plants | Old buildings (complex molecules) are torn down into bricks (simple molecules), releasing energy. |
| **Anabolism** | 🏗️ Construction & Manufacturing Sector | Bricks (simple molecules) are assembled into new buildings (complex biomolecules), consuming energy. |
| **Aerobic Respiration** | 🔥 Coal-Fired Power Plant (with oxygen) | Highly efficient, produces large energy output, but cannot operate without oxygen. |
| **Anaerobic Respiration/Fermentation** | 🔋 Battery Backup Generator | Operates without oxygen, less efficient, used during emergencies (e.g., heavy exercise). |
| **ATP** | ⚡ Universal Electric Currency | The "cash" that powers every machine in the city. |
| **Enzymes** | 👷 Specialized Workers | Each one performs ONE specific job at incredible speed. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU 2024 Takeaway:** Metabolism is **NEVER** just one reaction — it is an **integrated network** of pathways. Catabolism and Anabolism occur **simultaneously** in every living cell, every second.

## 1.3 The Four Master Categories of Metabolism

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically requires you to master these four classifications:

### 🔻 A. Catabolism (Destructive Metabolism)
- **Greek root:** *kata-* = "downward"
- Complex macromolecules → Simple molecules
- **Energy is RELEASED** (Exergonic, ΔG < 0)
- Examples: Glycolysis, β-Oxidation, Proteolysis

### 🔺 B. Anabolism (Constructive Metabolism)
- **Greek root:** *ana-* = "upward"
- Simple molecules → Complex macromolecules
- **Energy is CONSUMED** (Endergonic, ΔG > 0)
- Examples: Protein synthesis, DNA replication, Photosynthesis

### 💨 C. Aerobic Metabolism (With Oxygen)
- Uses **O₂** as the final electron acceptor
- Occurs in the **mitochondria** of eukaryotes
- High ATP yield: ~**30–32 ATP** per glucose

### 🚫 D. Anaerobic Metabolism (Without Oxygen)
- Does **NOT** use O₂
- Occurs in the **cytoplasm**
- Low ATP yield: **2 ATP** per glucose
- Examples: Lactic acid fermentation, Alcoholic fermentation

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic Foundation of Metabolism

Every metabolic reaction obeys the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** and is characterized by **Gibbs Free Energy (ΔG)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Theorem — The Energy Coupling Principle:**
> Since living systems are at *constant temperature* and *pressure*, the spontaneity of a reaction is governed solely by **ΔG** (Gibbs Free Energy change).

The two fundamental energy classes:

| Reaction Type | ΔG Sign | Energy Flow | Spontaneity | Biological Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Exergonic** (Catabolic) | ΔG < 0 | Released | Spontaneous | Power the cell |
| **Endergonic** (Anabolic) | ΔG > 0 | Required | Non-spontaneous | Build biomolecules |

## 2.2 The Universal Energy Currency: ATP

> [!NOTE]
> **Adenosine Triphosphate (ATP)** is the central energy currency of the cell. It is often called the *"molecular unit of currency"* for intracellular energy transfer.

The energy is stored in the **high-energy phosphoanhydride bonds** between its phosphate groups.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Energy Yields

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical bar `|` is **strictly forbidden** inside markdown table cells. Use `\vert` for absolute values or modulus in all tables below.

### Table 2.3.A: Master Energy Equation for Glucose Oxidation

| Parameter | Equation/Value | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Complete Combustion of Glucose | $C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \rightarrow 6CO_2 + 6H_2O$ | — | Aerobic respiration |
| Standard Free Energy Change | $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -2870$ | kJ/mol | Highly exergonic |
| ATP Hydrolysis Energy | $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -30.5$ | kJ/mol | Releases energy |
| Energy stored per ATP (in vivo) | $\approx -50$ to $-60$ | kJ/mol | Actual cellular conditions |

### Table 2.3.B: ATP Yield Comparison Across Metabolic Pathways

| Pathway | Location | O₂ Required? | ATP Yield (per glucose) | End Products |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Glycolysis** | Cytoplasm | No | 2 ATP (net) + 2 NADH | 2 Pyruvate |
| **Fermentation** (Lactic Acid) | Cytoplasm | No | 2 ATP (net) | 2 Lactate |
| **Fermentation** (Alcoholic) | Cytoplasm | No | 2 ATP (net) | 2 Ethanol + 2 CO₂ |
| **Krebs Cycle (TCA)** | Mitochondrial Matrix | Yes | 2 ATP (GTP) + 6 NADH + 2 FADH₂ | 4 CO₂ |
| **Oxidative Phosphorylation** | Inner Mitochondrial Membrane | Yes | ~26–28 ATP | H₂O |
| **Aerobic Total** | — | Yes | **30–32 ATP** | 6 CO₂ + 6 H₂O |
| **Anaerobic Total** | — | No | **2 ATP** | Lactate / Ethanol |

## 2.4 The Logic of Anabolic vs. Catabolic Pathways

### Step 1: Catabolic Logic (Energy Extraction)
- **Substrate-level phosphorylation:** Direct transfer of a phosphate group to ADP → ATP
- **Oxidative phosphorylation:** ATP generated via the Electron Transport Chain (ETC) coupled with chemiosmosis
- **Photophosphorylation:** Light energy drives ATP synthesis (only in photosynthetic cells)

### Step 2: Anabolic Logic (Energy Investment)
- Anabolic pathways consume the ATP produced by catabolism
- The cell maintains a strict **ATP : ADP ratio** (typically ~10:1)
- When ATP is depleted, catabolism is **upregulated**; when ATP is abundant, anabolism is favored

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Engineering (Bioprocess/Biotech Relevance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> 1. **Bioprocess Engineering** — Designing fermenters for *E. coli* (anaerobic) vs. yeast (aerobic) requires different oxygen delivery systems.
> 2. **Biofuel Production** — Anaerobic digestion of biomass produces biogas (methane).
> 3. **Tissue Engineering** — Understanding aerobic vs. anaerobic metabolism explains ischemic injury during heart attacks.
> 4. **Food Technology** — Lactic acid fermentation by *Lactobacillus* produces yogurt, cheese, sauerkraut.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📐 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total ATP Yield from Complete Aerobic Oxidation of Glucose

We will exhaustively derive the total ATP count starting from one molecule of glucose, following the KTU valuation standard.

### Step 1: Glycolysis (Cytoplasm) — Net Products

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Glucose} &\xrightarrow{\text{Glycolysis}} 2 \text{ Pyruvate} + 2 \text{ ATP} + 2 \text{ NADH} \\
\text{Where:} \quad \text{NADH} + H^+ &\rightarrow \text{yields 2.5 ATP (via ETC in eukaryotes)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation of logic:**
- Glycolysis invests **2 ATP** in the preparatory phase (Hexokinase, Phosphofructokinase steps).
- It produces **4 ATP** in the payoff phase (via substrate-level phosphorylation).
- Net ATP from glycolysis = $4 - 2 = 2$ ATP.
- It also produces 2 NADH in the cytoplasm, which must be shuttled into the mitochondrion.

### Step 2: Pyruvate Decarboxylation (Mitochondrial Matrix)

$$\begin{aligned}
2 \text{ Pyruvate} + 2 \text{ NAD}^+ + 2 \text{ CoA} &\rightarrow 2 \text{ Acetyl-CoA} + 2 \text{ CO}_2 + 2 \text{ NADH}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation of logic:**
- Each pyruvate loses one carbon as CO₂.
- This produces 2 NADH (one per pyruvate).
- Each NADH will yield **2.5 ATP** in the ETC.

### Step 3: Krebs Cycle / TCA Cycle (Mitochondrial Matrix) — Per Acetyl-CoA

$$\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ Acetyl-CoA} &\rightarrow 2 \text{ CO}_2 + 3 \text{ NADH} + 1 \text{ FADH}_2 + 1 \text{ GTP}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation of logic:**
- 1 GTP ≈ 1 ATP (substrate-level phosphorylation).
- FADH₂ enters the ETC at Complex II, yielding **1.5 ATP**.
- For 2 Acetyl-CoA (from 1 glucose), we multiply all by 2.

### Step 4: Summation of ATP from Oxidative Phosphorylation

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{ATP from NADH} &= (\text{Total NADH}) \times 2.5 \\
&= \left(2_{\text{glycolysis}} + 2_{\text{PDH}} + 6_{\text{TCA}}\right) \times 2.5 \\
&= 10 \times 2.5 = 25 \text{ ATP}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{ATP from FADH}_2 &= 2 \times 1.5 = 3 \text{ ATP}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{ATP from Substrate-Level Phosphorylation} &= 2_{\text{glycolysis}} + 2_{\text{TCA (GTP)}} = 4 \text{ ATP}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Final Total ATP Tally (Aerobic)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total ATP} &= \text{NADH ATP} + \text{FADH}_2 \text{ ATP} + \text{SLP ATP} \\
&= 25 + 3 + 4 \\
&= \boxed{32 \text{ ATP per glucose}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The exact number can range from **30 to 32 ATP** depending on the shuttle used to transport cytoplasmic NADH (malate-aspartate shuttle yields 2.5 ATP/NADH; glycerol-phosphate shuttle yields only 1.5 ATP/NADH).

## 3.2 Derivation: ATP Yield from Anaerobic Glycolysis (Lactic Acid Fermentation)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Glucose} + 2 \text{ NAD}^+ + 2 \text{ ADP} + 2 \text{ P}_i &\rightarrow 2 \text{ Pyruvate} + 2 \text{ NADH} + 2 \text{ ATP} + 2 \text{ H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
2 \text{ Pyruvate} + 2 \text{ NADH} &\xrightarrow{\text{Lactate Dehydrogenase}} 2 \text{ Lactate} + 2 \text{ NAD}^+
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Anaerobic ATP} &= 4_{\text{produced}} - 2_{\text{invested}} = \boxed{2 \text{ ATP per glucose}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation of logic:**
- The NADH produced is **reoxidized** to NAD⁺ during the reduction of pyruvate to lactate.
- This regenerates NAD⁺, allowing glycolysis to continue.
- **No net NADH** is left to enter the ETC, so the ATP yield is dramatically lower.

## 3.3 Comparative Efficiency Matrix

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Efficiency Ratio} &= \frac{\text{ATP}_{\text{anaerobic}}}{\text{ATP}_{\text{aerobic}}} = \frac{2}{32} = 0.0625 = 6.25\%
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Energy Capture (Aerobic)} &= \frac{32 \times 30.5 \text{ kJ}}{2870 \text{ kJ}} \times 100\% \approx 34\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Aerobic respiration is approximately **16 times more efficient** at extracting energy from glucose than anaerobic glycolysis. This is precisely why **mitochondria** (aerobic organelles) are considered the "powerhouses" of the cell.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Metabolism Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["FOOD INTAKE"] --> nodeB["DIGESTION"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["MONOMERS - Glucose Amino Acids Fatty Acids"]
    
    nodeC --> nodeD["CATABOLISM"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["ANABOLISM"]
    
    nodeD --> nodeF["ENERGY RELEASE"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["REDUCING EQUIVALENTS - NADH FADH2"]
    
    nodeF --> nodeH["ATP AND HEAT"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["ELECTRON TRANSPORT CHAIN"]
    nodeI --> nodeH
    
    nodeH --> nodeJ["ATP POOL"]
    nodeE --> nodeJ
    
    nodeJ --> nodeK["BIOSYNTHESIS - Proteins Lipids Nucleic Acids"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL["CELLULAR WORK - Transport Mechanical"]
    
    style nodeA fill:#FFD700
    style nodeH fill:#90EE90

```

## 4.2 Aerobic vs Anaerobic Pathway Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    nodeChoice -- "YES - AEROBIC" --> nodeMito["MITOCHONDRIA ENTRY"]

    nodeFerm --> nodeAlc["ETHANOL PLUS CO2 - yeast - 2 ATP"]
    
    style nodeGly fill:#FFE4B5
    style nodeATPhigh fill:#98FB98
    style nodeLac fill:#FFB6C1
```
