Drugs like **3-bromopyruvate** target **hexokinase-II** bound to mitochondria — a key target in oncological biotechnology. > - **Tissue Engineering & Bioprocess Control:** Dissolved O₂ levels in bioreactors determine whether cultures run **aerobic glycolysis (→ TCA)** or **anaerobic glycolysis (→ lactate)**. Engineers manipulate pO₂ to optimize product yield. > - **Sports Physiology / Metabolic Engineering:** The **Pasteur effect** (inhibition of glycolysis by O₂) and the **Crabtree effect** (suppression of oxidative phosphorylation by high glucose) are routinely exploited in yeast bioprocessing. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Reaction Mechanism > [!IMPORTANT] > **Convention Used:** `(2)` denotes that the reaction occurs **twice per single glucose molecule** because the 6-carbon sugar is split into two 3-carbon fragments at Step 4. ATP and NADH bookkeeping will follow this convention. --- ### 🧪 Step 1 — Phosphorylation of Glucose (Trapping Reaction) **Catalyst:** Hexokinase (Km ≈ 0.1 mM, ubiquitous) or Glucokinase (Km ≈ 10 mM, liver & pancreatic β-cells). Requires **Mg²⁺** as cofactor. $$\text{Glucose} + \text{ATP} \xrightarrow{\text{Hexokinase, Mg}^{2+}} \text{Glucose-6-Phosphate} + \text{ADP} + \text{H}^+$$ - **Logic:** The γ-phosphate of ATP is transferred to the C6 hydroxyl of glucose. Glucose-6-P carries a negative charge at physiological pH → **membrane-impermeable** → glucose is "trapped" inside the cell. - **Driving force:** ΔG = −33.5 kJ/mol (in vivo) — essentially **irreversible**. - **ATP count:** **−1 ATP** consumed. --- ### 🧪 Step 2 — Isomerization of Glucose-6-P to Fructose-6-P $$\text{Glucose-6-P} \xrightleftharpoons{\text{Phosphoglucose Isomerase}} \text{Fructose-6-P}$$ - **Logic:** An aldose (glucose-6-P, aldehyde at C1) is converted to a ketose (fructose-6-P, ketone at C2) via an **enediol intermediate**. The reaction is freely reversible (ΔG ≈ 0). - **ATP count:** **No change.** --- ### 🧪 Step 3 — Second Phosphorylation (The Committed Step) ⭐ **Catalyst:** **Phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1)** — the **master regulator of glycolysis**. $$\text{Fructose-6-P} + \text{ATP} \xrightarrow{\text{PFK-1, Mg}^{2+}} \text{Fructose-1,6-Bisphosphate} + \text{ADP} + \text{H}^+$$ - **Logic:** F6P is phosphorylated at C1. This commits the molecule to the glycolytic pathway (cannot enter gluconeogenesis, glycogen synthesis, or pentose phosphate pathway from this point onward). - **ATP count:** **−1 ATP** consumed. **Cumulative investment = −2 ATP.** --- ### 🧪 Step 4 — Cleavage of the 6-Carbon Sugar **Catalyst:** **Aldolase** (Class I in animals/yeast uses Lys-Schiff base; Class II in microbes uses Zn²⁺). $$\text{Fructose-1,6-Bisphosphate} \xrightleftharpoons{\text{Aldolase}} \text{DHAP} + \text{G3P}$$ - **Logic:** A retro-aldol cleavage splits the 6-carbon sugar into: - **Dihydroxyacetone phosphate (DHAP)** — a ketose - **Glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate (G3P)** — an aldose - Although DHAP and G3P are isomers, only G3P continues directly in the payoff phase. --- ### 🧪 Step 5 — Isomerization of DHAP to G3P **Catalyst:** **Triose Phosphate Isomerase (TPI)** — kinetically *perfect* (kcat/Km ≈ 2 × 10⁸ M⁻¹s⁻¹, near the diffusion limit). $$\text{DHAP} \xrightleftharpoons{\text{TPI}} \text{G3P}$$ - **Logic:** DHAP and G3P exist in equilibrium. TPI rapidly converts DHAP into G3P, ensuring that **both triose phosphates feed into the payoff phase**. Net effect: **1 glucose → 2 G3P**. - All subsequent steps (6–10) occur **twice per glucose**. --- ### 🧪 Step 6 — Oxidation & Phosphorylation of G3P (2× per glucose) ⭐ **Catalyst:** **Glyceraldehyde-3-Phosphate Dehydrogenase (GAPDH)**. $$\text{G3P} + \text{NAD}^+ + \text{P}_i \xrightarrow{\text{GAPDH}} \text{1,3-Bisphosphoglycerate} + \text{NADH} + \text{H}^+$$ - **Logic:** The aldehyde at C1 of G3P is oxidized to a carboxyl group (forming a high-energy **acyl-phosphate mixed anhydride bond at C1**). The electrons are transferred to NAD⁺ → **NADH**. Inorganic phosphate (Pᵢ) is the phosphate donor — *not ATP*. - **ATP count:** **+1 NADH (×2 = +2 NADH per glucose).** --- ### 🧪 Step 7 — Substrate-Level Phosphorylation (2× per glucose) ⭐ **Catalyst:** **Phosphoglycerate Kinase (PGK)**. $$\text{1,3-Bisphosphoglycerate} + \text{ADP} \xrightleftharpoons{\text{3-Phosphoglycerate} + \text{ATP}}$$ - **Logic:** The high-energy acyl-phosphate group at C1 is transferred directly to ADP → **ATP**. This is **substrate-level phosphorylation** (no proton gradient required). - **ATP count:** **+1 ATP (×2 = +2 ATP per glucose).** --- ### 🧪 Step 8 — Phosphate Group Migration **Catalyst:** **Phosphoglycerate Mutase (PGM)**. $$\text{3-Phosphoglycerate} \xrightleftharpoons{\text{2-Phosphoglycerate}}$$ - **Logic:** The phosphate migrates from C3 to C2 via a **2,3-bisphosphoglycerate (2,3-BPG) intermediate**. In erythrocytes, 2,3-BPG has a separate physiological role (regulating hemoglobin O₂ affinity). - **ATP count:** **No change.** --- ### 🧪 Step 9 — Dehydration (Creates a High-Energy Enol-Phosphate) **Catalyst:** **Enolase** (requires Mg²⁺). $$\text{2-Phosphoglycerate} \xrightleftharpoons{\text{Phosphoenolpyruvate (PEP)} + \text{H}_2\text{O}}$$ - **Logic:** Dehydration of 2-PG redistributes the molecular energy. Although the phosphate is still a low-energy ester bond, the resulting enol-phosphate is a **very high-energy bond** (ΔG°′ of hydrolysis ≈ −61.9 kJ/mol). - **ATP count:** **No change.** --- ### 🧪 Step 10 — Final Substrate-Level Phosphorylation (2× per glucose) ⭐ **Catalyst:** **Pyruvate Kinase (PK)** — requires K⁺ and Mg²⁺. $$\text{PEP} + \text{ADP} \xrightarrow{\text{Pyruvate Kinase}} \text{Pyruvate} + \text{ATP}$$ - **Logic:** The high-energy enol-phosphate is transferred to ADP, and PEP is converted to the keto-acid **pyruvate** (the *enolate tautomerizes to the more stable keto form*). This reaction is essentially irreversible in cells. - **ATP count:** **+1 ATP (×2 = +2 ATP per glucose).** --- ## 3.2 Net Energy Balance Derivation > [!NOTE] > **Step-by-step ATP/NADH accounting** (multiplied correctly for the dual triose stream): $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{Investment Phase (per glucose):} \\ \text{Step 1:} \quad & -1 \text{ ATP} \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad & -1 \text{ ATP} \\ \hline \textbf{Total Invested:} \quad & -2 \text{ ATP} \\[4pt] \textbf{Payoff Phase (per glucose = 2 triose streams):} \\ \text{Step 6:} \quad & +1 \text{ NADH} \times 2 = +2 \text{ NADH} \\ \text{Step 7:} \quad & +1 \text{ ATP} \times 2 = +2 \text{ ATP} \\ \text{Step 10:} \quad & +1 \text{ ATP} \times 2 = +2 \text{ ATP} \\ \hline \textbf{Total Payoff:} \quad & +4 \text{ ATP, } +2 \text{ NADH} \\[4pt] \textbf{Net Yield (Glycolysis only):} \\ & (+4 \text{ ATP}) + (-2 \text{ ATP}) = +2 \text{ ATP net} \\ & +2 \text{ NADH (cytosolic)} \end{aligned} $$ The complete balanced equation (verified): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 &+ 2\,\text{NAD}^+ + 2\,\text{ADP} + 2\,\text{P}_i \\ &\longrightarrow 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{COCOO}^- + 2\,\text{NADH} + 2\,\text{ATP} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\,\text{H}^+ \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Regulatory Enzymes (KTU Favourite) > [!TIP] > This is a **high-yield comparison matrix** that frequently appears in 14-mark KTU questions. | Feature | Hexokinase | PFK-1 | Pyruvate Kinase | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Step** | 1 | 3 | 10 | | **Substrate** | Glucose | F6P | PEP | | **Allosteric Activator** | — | AMP, F2,6BP, F6P | F1,6BP (feed-forward) | | **Allosteric Inhibitor** | Glucose-6-P | ATP, Citrate, H⁺ | ATP, Alanine, Long-chain FA | | **Hormonal Control** | Induced by Insulin (Glucokinase) | Induced by Insulin | Induced by Insulin | | **Covalent Modification** | — | — | Phosphorylation by PKA inactivates (liver) | | **Tissue Isoforms** | Hexokinase I–III (ubiquitous), IV (Glucokinase, liver/β-cells) | M (muscle), L (liver), C (ubiquitous) | M (muscle), L (liver), A (kidney), etc. | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Ten Steps of Glycolysis > [!IMPORTANT] > Use the diagram below for rapid mental visualization. The two phase boundaries are highlighted using subgraph clusters. ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode([Glucose Entry]) --> s1 s1["Step 1: Hexokinase
Glucose to G6P
ATP consumed"] --> s2 s2["Step 2: PGI
G6P to F6P"] --> s3 s3["Step 3: PFK-1
F6P to F1,6BP
ATP consumed
COMMITTED STEP"] --> s4 s4["Step 4: Aldolase
F1,6BP to DHAP plus G3P"] --> s5 s5["Step 5: TPI
DHAP to G3P"] --> s6 subgraph phase2["PHASE II - PAYOFF - Triose Stage"] s6["Step 6: GAPDH
G3P to 1,3 BPG
NADH produced"] --> s7 s7["Step 7: PGK
1,3 BPG to 3 PG
ATP produced"] --> s8 s8["Step 8: PGM
3 PG to 2 PG"] --> s9 s9["Step 9: Enolase
2 PG to PEP
Water released"] --> s10 s10["Step 10: Pyruvate Kinase
PEP to Pyruvate
ATP produced"] --> endNode end endNode([Pyruvate - 2 molecules]) inv1[/Investment: 2 ATP/] -.-> s1 inv2[/Investment: 2 ATP/] -.-> s3 payoffA[/Payoff per G3P: 2 ATP plus 1 NADH/] -.-> s6 payoffB[/Payoff per G3P: 2 ATP plus 1 NADH/] -.-> s7 payoffC[/Payoff per G3P: 2 ATP plus 1 NADH/] -.-> s10 classDef investStyle fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000 classDef payoffStyle fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#27ae60,color:#000 classDef irvStyle fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#f39c12,color:#000 class s1,s3 classDef investStyle class s6,s7,s10 classDef payoffStyle ``` > [!NOTE] > The red-tinted nodes represent **ATP-consuming** steps; green-tinted nodes represent **ATP/NADH-generating** steps. The **PFK-1 step** is the master control node. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Glycolytic Energy Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR inNode([Glucose Substrate]) --> prep[Preparatory Phase] prep --2 ATP spent--> split[Cleavage by Aldolase] split --> payoff[Payoff Phase runs twice] payoff --4 ATP generated--> poolA[ATP Pool] payoff --2 NADH generated--> poolB[NADH Pool] poolA --Net Gain--> calcNode poolB --To Electron Transport Chain ETC--> calcNode split --2 Pyruvate--> outNode([Pyruvate Output]) calcNode[Net Balance: 2 ATP plus 2 NADH per Glucose] --> outNode classDef phaseStyle fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000 class prep,payoff phaseStyle ``` --- ## 4.3 Tabular Map of Carbon Tracking (C-Atom Journey) | Step | Carbon Skeleton | #C | Comment | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---| | 0 | Glucose | 6 | Substrate | | 1 | G6P | 6 | Phosphate at C6 | | 2 | F6P | 6 | Ketose form | | 3 | F1,6BP | 6 | Phosphates at C1 and C6 | | 4 | DHAP + G3P | 3 + 3 | Aldolase cleavage | | 5 | 2 × G3P | 3 | DHAP converted | | 6 | 2 × 1,3-BPG | 3 | Oxidation + phosphorylation | | 7 | 2 × 3-PG | 3 | Substrate-level phosphorylation | | 8 | 2 × 2-PG | 3 | Phosphate migration | | 9 | 2 × PEP | 3 | Dehydration | | 10 | 2 × Pyruvate | 3 | Final substrate-level phosphorylation | > [!TIP] > **Track the carbons:** Notice that **C1, C2, C3 of glucose end up in pyruvate** and **C4, C5, C6 of glucose end up in the *other* pyruvate** (because of the aldolase cleavage plane). This is a **classic KTU viva question**. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting the "×2" multiplier:** Steps 6–10 occur **twice per glucose**. Students often write +4 ATP and stop — but they must show it is **+2 ATP × 2 occurrences**. > 2. **Confusing ΔG°′ (standard) with ΔG (in vivo):** The textbook values are **standard-state** (1 M concentrations). Actual cellular ΔG values are very different because reactant/product concentrations are far from standard. State the condition explicitly. > 3. **Wrong compartmentation:** Glycolysis is **cytosolic** — not mitochondrial. The Krebs cycle and oxidative phosphorylation are inside the mitochondrion. > 4. **Forgetting the role of Mg²⁺:** Every kinase in glycolysis requires **Mg²⁺** as an essential cofactor (it chelates the ATP phosphates). > 5. **Confusing Hexokinase vs Glucokinase:** They have different Km, Vmax, and tissue distributions — a frequent 3-mark short-answer question. > 6. **Neglecting fate of NADH:** Cytosolic NADH must be **shuttled** into the mitochondrion (glycerol-3-P shuttle *or* malate-aspartate shuttle) — different ATP yields. --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]** **Define glycolysis. Why is it referred to as the EMP pathway, and what is the net ATP yield under aerobic and anaerobic conditions?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** Glycolysis is a **universal, cytosolic, ten-step catabolic pathway** that converts one molecule of **glucose (C₆)** into **two molecules of pyruvate (C₃)** with a net yield of ATP and NADH. > > **Why EMP (1 mark):** The pathway was elucidated by **Embden, Meyerhof, and Parnas**, hence called the **EMP pathway**. It was the first complete metabolic pathway ever worked out. > > **Net ATP (1 mark):** > - **Aerobic:** Net **2 ATP** (substrate-level phosphorylation) + **2 NADH** ≈ 7–8 ATP total. > - **Anaerobic (fermentation):** Net **2 ATP only**, since NADH is reoxidized to NAD⁺ via lactate or ethanol fermentation (NADH is *not* routed to the ETC). --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]** **Differentiate between Hexokinase and Glucokinase. (Any three points)** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Hexokinase | Glucokinase | |:---|:---|:---| | **Km for glucose (1 mark)** | **Low (~0.1 mM)** — high affinity, saturated at normal blood [glucose] | **High (~10 mM)** — low affinity, only active after a meal | | **Tissue distribution (1 mark)** | Ubiquitous (all tissues) | **Liver and pancreatic β-cells only** | | **Vmax & Function (1 mark)** | Low Vmax; ensures basal glucose uptake for all cells | High Vmax; acts as a **glucose sensor** — phosphorylates excess dietary glucose for storage as glycogen | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks) > [!NOTE] > As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, every Module 14-mark question offers an **internal choice (A or B)**. Both versions are provided below for full preparation. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model QP]** **(a)** Describe in detail the **ten steps of glycolysis** with the names of all enzymes, the substrates and products at each step, and clearly identify the steps that consume or produce ATP and NADH. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Calculate the **net ATP gain from the complete aerobic oxidation of one glucose molecule** through glycolysis, pyruvate dehydrogenase, the Krebs cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation. Indicate the compartmentation of each stage. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **(Award 0.7 marks per step, summed to 7 marks)** | Step | Enzyme | Reaction | Energy Change | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | **Hexokinase** | Glucose + ATP → G6P + ADP | −1 ATP (1.4 marks) | | 2 | Phosphoglucose isomerase | G6P ⇌ F6P | — (0.7 marks) | | 3 | **Phosphofructokinase-1** | F6P + ATP → F1,6BP + ADP | −1 ATP; **committed step** (1.4 marks) | | 4 | Aldolase | F1,6BP ⇌ DHAP + G3P | — (0.7 marks) | | 5 | Triose phosphate isomerase | DHAP ⇌ G3P | — (0.7 marks) | | 6 | **G3P dehydrogenase** | G3P + NAD⁺ + Pᵢ → 1,3-BPG + NADH | +1 NADH (1.4 marks) | | 7 | **Phosphoglycerate kinase** | 1,3-BPG + ADP → 3-PG + ATP | +1 ATP (1.4 marks) | | 8 | Phosphoglycerate mutase | 3-PG ⇌ 2-PG | — (0.7 marks) | | 9 | Enolase | 2-PG ⇌ PEP + H₂O | — (0.7 marks) | | 10 | **Pyruvate kinase** | PEP + ADP → Pyruvate + ATP | +1 ATP (1.4 marks) | > **Key highlight (1 mark reserved):** Steps 1, 3, and 10 are the **irreversible, regulatory** steps of glycolysis in animal cells. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Compartmentation Table (3.5 marks):** | Stage | Compartment | ATP / NADH / FADH₂ Produced | ATP Yield | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | Glycolysis (substrate-level) | Cytosol | 2 ATP (net) | **2** | | Glycolysis (oxidative) | Cytosol → Mito (via shuttle) | 2 NADH | **5** (or 3 with glycerol-3-P shuttle) | | Pyruvate Dehydrogenase | Mitochondrial matrix | 2 NADH | **5** | | Krebs Cycle (substrate-level) | Mitochondrial matrix | 2 ATP (or GTP) | **2** | | Krebs Cycle (oxidative) | Mitochondrial matrix | 6 NADH + 2 FADH₂ | **17** | | Oxidative Phosphorylation | Inner mitochondrial membrane | — | — | **P/O ratios assumed:** NADH = 2.5 ATP, FADH₂ = 1.5 ATP (modern chemiosmotic consensus). **Final Net ATP (3.5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net ATP} &= 2_{\text{(glycolysis SLP)}} + 5_{\text{(glyc NADH)}} + 5_{\text{(PDH NADH)}} + 2_{\text{(Krebs GTP)}} + 17_{\text{(Krebs oxidative)}} \\ &= \mathbf{31 \text{ ATP per glucose}} \end{aligned} $$ > **Note (1 mark):** If the glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle is used, total is **30 ATP** (the cytosolic NADH transfers electrons to FAD → yields 1.5 ATP only). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model QP]** **(a)** Discuss the **three key regulatory enzymes of glycolysis**, their activators, inhibitors, and hormonal regulation. Mention the significance of **fructose-2,6-bisphosphate**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **two shuttle systems** for transferring cytosolic NADH into the mitochondrial matrix. Compare their ATP yields. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **The three regulatory enzymes are:** Hexokinase / Glucokinase (1 mark), PFK-1 (3 marks), Pyruvate Kinase (3 marks). **PFK-1 — The Master Regulator (3 marks detail):** - **Activators:** **AMP, Fructose-2,6-Bisphosphate (F2,6BP), F6P** (substrate feed-forward) - **Inhibitors:** **ATP, Citrate, H⁺ (low pH)** - **Significance of F2,6BP (1 mark):** F2,6BP is the **most potent allosteric activator** of PFK-1. It is synthesized from F6P by the bifunctional enzyme **PFK-2 / FBPase-2**. When [glucose] is high, **insulin** activates a phosphatase that converts the kinase to its active form, **raising [F2,6BP]**, which **stimulates glycolysis and inhibits gluconeogenesis** simultaneously. **Hexokinase vs Glucokinase Regulation (1 mark):** - Hexokinase: inhibited by its product **G6P** (feedback). - Glucokinase: not inhibited by G6P; regulated by **glucokinase regulatory protein (GKRP)** and by **translocation** between nucleus and cytosol. **Pyruvate Kinase Regulation (1 mark):** - **Liver (L-form):** Inhibited by **ATP, alanine, and phosphorylation by PKA** (during fasting/gluconeogenesis). - **Muscle (M-form):** Not hormonally regulated; activated by **F1,6BP (feed-forward)**. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **The Two NADH Shuttles (7 marks):** **(i) Glycerol-3-Phosphate Shuttle (3.5 marks):** - Predominant in **skeletal muscle and brain**. - Cytosolic NADH transfers electrons to **DHAP** → forms **glycerol-3-P** (catalyzed by cytosolic glycerol-3-P dehydrogenase). - Glycerol-3-P then donates electrons to **FAD** of the **mitochondrial glycerol-3-P dehydrogenase** on the outer face of the inner membrane → forms **FADH₂**. - FADH₂ feeds electrons into the ETC at **Complex II (succinate dehydrogenase)** level. - **ATP yield per cytosolic NADH = 1.5 ATP** (1.5 × 2 = 3 ATP for 2 NADH). **(ii) Malate-Aspartate Shuttle (3.5 marks):** - Predominant in **liver, heart, and kidney**. - Cytosolic NADH reduces **oxaloacetate** → **malate** (malate dehydrogenase). - Malate crosses the inner membrane via the **malate-α-ketoglutarate antiporter**. - In the matrix, malate is reoxidized to OAA, producing **mitochondrial NADH**. - OAA is converted to **aspartate** and exported. - **ATP yield per cytosolic NADH = 2.5 ATP** (2.5 × 2 = 5 ATP for 2 NADH). | Shuttle | Tissue | Electron Entry Point | ATP per NADH | |:---|:---|:---|:---:| | Glycerol-3-P | Skeletal muscle, brain | Complex II (FADH₂) | 1.5 | | Malate-Aspartate | Liver, heart, kidney | Complex I (NADH) | 2.5 | --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Final 30-Second Revision Checklist (Must Memorize):** - 🔑 **Glycolysis = EMP pathway**; first metabolic pathway elucidated; **cytosolic**; **anaerobic**; **10 steps**. - 🔑 **Net products per glucose = 2 Pyruvate + 2 ATP (net) + 2 NADH**. - 🔑 **Three irreversible steps:** Hexokinase (Step 1), **PFK-1 (Step 3, master regulator)**, Pyruvate Kinase (Step 10). - 🔑 **Two ATP invested** (Steps 1, 3); **four ATP produced** (Steps 7, 10, each × 2); **two NADH produced** (Step 6, × 2). - 🔑 **Largest negative ΔG in vivo:** Pyruvate kinase step (−23 kJ/mol). - 🔑 **Largest positive ΔG°′:** Aldolase (+23.9 kJ/mol), but pulled forward in cells. - 🔑 **Substrate-level phosphorylation steps:** **7 and 10** (PGK and PK). - 🔑 **Cofactors:** Mg²⁺ (all kinases); NAD⁺ (GAPDH); K⁺ (Pyruvate Kinase). - 🔑 **F2,6BP** is the most potent activator of PFK-1; insulin increases its level. - 🔑 **Warburg Effect:** Cancer cells favor aerobic glycolysis → lactate, even with O₂ present. - 🔑 **Total aerobic ATP from glucose = ~30–32 ATP** (KTU convention: use 30 or 31 with P/O = 2.5/1.5). - 🔑 **Carbon tracking:** C1–C3 of glucose end up in one pyruvate; C4–C6 in the other (via aldolase cleavage plane). - 🔑 **NADH shuttle choice** determines whether 2 cytosolic NADH yield 3 or 5 ATP. - 🔑 **Pasteur Effect:** Oxygen inhibits glycolysis (downregulates PFK-1). **Crabtree Effect:** High glucose suppresses oxidative phosphorylation (in some tumor cells and yeast).

topics.title: TCA Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT302 - BIOCHEMISTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** TCA Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Tricarboxylic Acid (TCA) Cycle**, also known as the **Krebs Cycle** or **Citric Acid Cycle**, is the central catabolic oxidative pathway of aerobic metabolism. It is a closed, amphibolic, eight-step enzymatic loop that occurs in the **mitochondrial matrix** of eukaryotic cells (and the cytoplasm of prokaryotes). Its primary biochemical purpose is to oxidize the **two-carbon acetyl group** of **acetyl-CoA** into **two molecules of CO₂**, while generating high-energy electron carriers (**3 NADH**, **1 FADH₂**) and **1 GTP (or ATP)** per turn.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCBBT302, Module 2):**
> The TCA cycle is the **final common oxidative pathway** for carbohydrates, fatty acids, and amino acids. KTU examiners expect you to know: (1) the sequence of 8 intermediates, (2) the enzymes, (3) the sites of CO₂ release, (4) the energy yield, and (5) regulatory enzymes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cellular Furnace"

Imagine a **factory furnace** (the mitochondrion) that burns fuel (acetyl-CoA) to generate electricity (ATP).

- **Acetyl-CoA** is the **coal/log** being shoveled in — it carries energy from carbohydrates (via glycolysis), fats (via β-oxidation), and proteins (via deamination).
- **Oxaloacetate (OAA)** is the **fire-brick platform** that catches the fuel. Without OAA, the fire cannot start.
- The **eight intermediate molecules** (Citrate, Isocitrate, α-Ketoglutarate, Succinyl-CoA, Succinate, Fumarate, Malate, OAA) are the **stages of combustion** — the carbon is progressively oxidized.
- **NADH and FADH₂** are the **sparks/flue gases carrying electrons** to the electron transport chain (ETC) to drive the ATP synthase turbine.
- **CO₂** is the **smoke/ash** — the fully oxidized carbon waste exhaled by animals.
- **GTP** is the **local heat** recovered during combustion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a "cycle"?** Because the final product, **oxaloacetate**, is regenerated and ready to combine with the next acetyl-CoA. The furnace never needs a fresh brick — it is **self-perpetuating**, as long as substrates and oxygen keep arriving.

## 1.3 The Master Equation

The overall stoichiometry of one turn of the TCA cycle is:

$$\text{Acetyl-CoA} + 3\,\text{NAD}^+ + \text{FAD} + \text{GDP} + \text{P}_i + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{NADH} + \text{FADH}_2 + \text{GTP} + \text{CoA} + 3\,\text{H}^+$$

The cycle is **exergonic**, with a $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} \approx -20$ to $-25$ kcal/mol (per turn), but the energy is **conserved almost entirely in the reduced coenzymes**, not released as heat.

## 1.4 Historical Note

The cycle was elucidated in **1937** by **Sir Hans Adolf Krebs**, a German-British biochemist who received the **1953 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine** for this work. He demonstrated that the pathway operates as a cyclic sequence rather than a linear chain, building on Albert Szent-Györgyi's earlier observations of OAA's catalytic role.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the cycle sequence (KTU favourite):**
> **"Can I Ask Some Friend For More Apples?"**
> **C**itrate → **I**socitrate → **α-K**etoglutarate → **S**uccinyl-CoA → **S**uccinate → **F**umarate → **M**alate → **O**xaloacetate (**A**cetyl-CoA joins back).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Why Eight Steps?

The cycle is engineered as an **eight-step, eight-enzyme** sequence to accomplish four engineering objectives:

1. **Anchoring the acetyl group** to a four-carbon carrier (OAA → Citrate, 6C).
2. **Isomerizing** the molecule to expose the hydroxyl group for oxidation (Citrate → Isocitrate).
3. **Performing two oxidative decarboxylations** (Isocitrate → α-KG, then α-KG → Succinyl-CoA), releasing the two carbons as CO₂.
4. **Reconstructing OAA** through a series of oxidoreductions that harvest additional energy (Succinyl-CoA → Succinate → Fumarate → Malate → OAA).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cycle never directly produces ATP. The **only high-energy phosphate** is **GTP** (equivalent to ATP via nucleoside diphosphate kinase). All "ATP equivalents" in textbooks are earned only when NADH/FADH₂ are re-oxidized in the **electron transport chain (ETC)**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The table below condenses every key constant, conversion, and energetic yield needed for KTU numericals and short derivations.

| **Parameter** | **Value / Symbol** | **Engineering / Biochemical Significance** |
|---|---|---|
| Number of carbons entering (Acetyl-CoA) | $2\,\text{C}$ | From pyruvate, β-oxidation, or amino acid catabolism |
| Number of carbons leaving (as CO₂) | $2\,\text{CO}_2$ | Released at **Step 3** and **Step 4** |
| NADH produced per turn | $3$ | At Steps 3, 4, 8 |
| FADH₂ produced per turn | $1$ | At Step 6 (only membrane-bound TCA enzyme) |
| GTP (≈ ATP) produced per turn | $1$ | At Step 5 (substrate-level phosphorylation) |
| H₂O consumed | $2$ | At Steps 2 and 7 (hydration steps) |
| ATP yield per NADH (via ETC) | $\approx 2.5$ | Modern P/O ratio |
| ATP yield per FADH₂ (via ETC) | $\approx 1.5$ | Enters at Complex II |
| ATP yield per GTP | $1$ | Converted via NDPK |
| **Net ATP per acetyl-CoA** | $\mathbf{3(2.5) + 1(1.5) + 1(1) = 10}$ | **Standard KTU value** |
| Free energy change per turn | $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} \approx -22$ kcal/mol | Strongly exergonic |
| Location | Mitochondrial matrix | Eukaryotes; cytoplasm in prokaryotes |
| Rate of flux at rest | $\sim 60{-}100$ nmol/min/g tissue | Depends on O₂ availability |
| O₂ dependency | Strictly aerobic | Anaerobes lack a complete cycle |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Clinical Utility

- **Mitochondrial disease diagnostics:** Deficiencies in TCA enzymes (e.g., SDH, fumarase) cause severe encephalomyopathies, detectable via $^{13}$C-labeled acetate breath tests.
- **Cancer metabolism (Warburg effect):** Tumor cells often exhibit suppressed TCA flux and rely on glutaminolysis to replenish α-KG and succinate.
- **Bioprocess engineering:** Industrial production of citric acid by *Aspergillus niger* exploits the TCA cycle for yields exceeding **1 kg/kg glucose**.
- **Toxicology:** Cyanide (CN⁻), rotenone, and antimycin A block the ETC, causing the TCA cycle to halt due to NADH accumulation (NAD⁺ shortage).
- **Food spoilage:** TCA intermediates in *Pseudomonas* and *Lactobacillus* drive off-flavors in meat and dairy.

## 2.4 Three Regulatory Bottlenecks

KTU questions on regulation almost always target these three **irreversible, exergonic** steps:

| **Enzyme** | **Step** | **Activators** | **Inhibitors** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Citrate synthase | Acetyl-CoA + OAA → Citrate | Acetyl-CoA, ATP, Ca²⁺ | ATP (high), NADH, citrate, succinyl-CoA |
| Isocitrate dehydrogenase (ICDH) | Isocitrate → α-KG | ADP, Ca²⁺, NAD⁺ | ATP, NADH |
| α-Ketoglutarate dehydrogenase (α-KGDH) | α-KG → Succinyl-CoA | Ca²⁺, CoA, NAD⁺ | NADH, succinyl-CoA |

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy-charge logic:** High [ATP] and [NADH] signal "enough energy" → cycle slows. High [Ca²⁺] (muscle contraction signal) and high [ADP] signal "need more ATP" → cycle accelerates.

## 2.5 The Amphibolic Nature — A Two-Way Street

The cycle is not purely catabolic. Intermediates are **continuously withdrawn** (anaplerosis) for biosynthesis:

- **α-Ketoglutarate** → glutamate (and onward to proline, arginine, GABA).
- **Succinyl-CoA** → porphyrin ring of heme.
- **OAA** → aspartate (and onward to pyrimidines, asparagine, lysine).
- **Citrate** → cytoplasmic acetyl-CoA for fatty acid and cholesterol synthesis.

These drains are replenished by **anaplerotic reactions** (e.g., pyruvate carboxylase: Pyruvate + CO₂ + ATP → OAA).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents the **exhaustive chemical walk-through** of all eight steps. Each step lists: substrate, product, enzyme, cofactors, energy carrier, and the rationale.

## Step 1 — Citrate Synthase (Condensation)

The first step is an **aldol-type Claisen condensation** joining the 2-carbon acetyl group to the 4-carbon oxaloacetate to form a 6-carbon citrate.

$$\text{Acetyl-CoA} + \text{Oxaloacetate} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Citrate} + \text{CoA-SH}$$

**Catalysis logic:**

1. The methyl group of acetyl-CoA is deprotonated to form an **enolate carbanion** (stabilized by the thioester).
2. The carbanion attacks the carbonyl carbon of OAA, forming a transient **citryl-CoA** intermediate.
3. Hydrolysis of the high-energy thioester bond (CoA) drives the reaction forward and releases CoA.
4. The product citrate is **prochiral**; the enzyme binds it in a specific orientation to set up stereospecific dehydration in Step 2.

**Energetics:** $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} \approx -7.7$ kcal/mol (highly exergonic; the only irreversible step in the first half).

> [!NOTE]
> Citrate synthase is a **dimer**, structurally classified as a Type I amidotransferase-like fold, and is **product-inhibited** by citrate.

## Step 2 — Aconitase (Isomerization)

Aconitase catalyzes the **reversible dehydration-rehydration** of citrate to isocitrate via the intermediate **cis-aconitate**. Despite the name, **aconitase does not use aconitate as a tightly-bound substrate**.

$$\text{Citrate} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{-H}_2\text{O}]{\text{Aconitase}} \textit{cis}\text{-Aconitate} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{+H}_2\text{O}]{\text{Aconitase}} \text{Isocitrate}$$

**Catalysis logic:**

1. The hydroxyl group on the central carbon of citrate cannot be oxidized directly because it is tertiary and buried.
2. Aconitase first **dehydrates** citrate to the planar *cis*-aconitate.
3. It then **rehydrates** from the opposite face, yielding isocitrate, whose hydroxyl is now on a **secondary carbon**, accessible to NAD⁺.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Aconitase contains a **Fe-S cluster (Fe₄S₄)** at its active site, which acts as a Lewis acid to coordinate the substrate's carboxylate groups — one of the few enzymes in metabolism that uses an inorganic cofactor for a non-redox reaction.

## Step 3 — Isocitrate Dehydrogenase (Oxidative Decarboxylation #1)

The first of two **irreversible decarboxylations**. Isocitrate (6C) is oxidized to a 5-carbon α-ketoglutarate.

$$\text{Isocitrate} + \text{NAD}^+ \longrightarrow \alpha\text{-Ketoglutarate} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{NADH}$$

**Catalysis logic:**

1. The C-2 hydroxyl of isocitrate is oxidized by NAD⁺, generating an unstable **oxalosuccinate** intermediate (a β-keto acid).
2. The β-carboxylate spontaneously decarboxylates, releasing **CO₂** from the carbon that originally belonged to OAA.
3. The resulting enamine tautomerizes to the keto form, **α-ketoglutarate**.

**Energy harvested:** $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} \approx -5$ kcal/mol; **1 NADH (≈ 2.5 ATP)** formed.

> [!TIP]
> Mitochondrial ICDH is **allosterically activated by ADP and Ca²⁺** and inhibited by ATP. Cytosolic NADP⁺-dependent ICDH supplies reducing power for fatty acid and cholesterol biosynthesis.

## Step 4 — α-Ketoglutarate Dehydrogenase Complex (Oxidative Decarboxylation #2)

A **multi-enzyme complex** mechanistically identical to the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex.

$$\alpha\text{-Ketoglutarate} + \text{CoA-SH} + \text{NAD}^+ \longrightarrow \text{Succinyl-CoA} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{NADH}$$

**Three enzymatic subunits:**

- **E1 (α-KGDH):** decarboxylates α-KG, forms a thiamine pyrophosphate (TPP)-stabilized carbanion on C-2.
- **E2 (dihydrolipoamide succinyltransferase):** transfers the succinyl group to CoA via a lipoamide arm, yielding **succinyl-CoA**.
- **E3 (dihydrolipoamide dehydrogenase):** reoxidizes the reduced lipoamide using **FAD → NAD⁺**, generating the second NADH.

**Energy harvested:** $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} \approx -8$ kcal/mol; **1 NADH (≈ 2.5 ATP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> Both ICDH and α-KGDH release a **CO₂ derived from the original oxaloacetate** carbons (not the incoming acetyl carbons) in the first turn. Only by the second turn do the acetyl-derived carbons begin to appear as CO₂ — a classic "fate of label" question in KTU exams.

## Step 5 — Succinyl-CoA Synthetase (Substrate-Level Phosphorylation)

$$\text{Succinyl-CoA} + \text{GDP} + \text{P}_i \xrightleftharpoons{\text{Succinate} + \text{GTP} + \text{CoA-SH}}$$

**Catalysis logic:**

1. The high-energy thioester bond of succinyl-CoA (~ -13 kcal/mol of free energy) is cleaved.
2. The released energy drives the **phosphorylation of GDP → GTP** via a phosphohistidine intermediate on the enzyme.
3. The reaction is **reversible**; in tissues that consume succinyl-CoA for heme synthesis, the cycle can run in reverse up to this point.

**Energy harvested:** $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} \approx -0.7$ kcal/mol (reversible); **1 GTP (≈ 1 ATP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> Mammalian tissues express two isoforms of succinyl-CoA synthetase: one generating **GTP** (liver, kidney) and one generating **ATP** (brain, cardiac muscle). Both reactions are biochemically interchangeable via **nucleoside diphosphate kinase**.

## Step 6 — Succinate Dehydrogenase (Oxidation, FADH₂)

Succinate is oxidized to fumarate. This is the **only TCA enzyme embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane** (Complex II of the ETC).

$$\text{Succinate} + \text{FAD} \longrightarrow \text{Fumarate} + \text{FADH}_2$$

**Catalysis logic:**

1. The two methylene hydrogens of succinate are removed in a **trans** configuration.
2. The enzyme uses **FAD covalently bound via a histidine** (8α-N(3)-histidyl-FAD) as the hydride acceptor; FAD is preferred over NAD⁺ because the resulting fumarate double bond does not provide sufficient driving force to reduce NAD⁺.
3. Electrons from FADH₂ are passed directly to **ubiquinone (Q)** in the ETC, bypassing Complex I.

**Energy harvested:** **1 FADH₂ (≈ 1.5 ATP)**.

> [!WARNING]
> Malonate, a structural analog of succinate, is a **competitive inhibitor** of succinate dehydrogenase — a classic poisoning mechanism taught in biochemistry (Krebs himself used it to dissect the cycle).

## Step 7 — Fumarase (Hydration)

$$\text{Fumarate} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightleftharpoons{\text{L-Malate}}$$

**Catalysis logic:**

1. Fumarase is **stereospecific**: water adds in a *trans* anti-addition across the double bond, producing only **L-malate**.
2. The active site contains a **Mg²⁺** ion that coordinates the fumarate carboxylate and a key lysine that protonates the double bond.
3. The reaction is freely reversible.

## Step 8 — Malate Dehydrogenase (Oxidation, OAA Regeneration)

$$\text{Malate} + \text{NAD}^+ \xrightleftharpoons{\text{Oxaloacetate} + \text{NADH} + \text{H}^+}$$

**Catalysis logic:**

1. The secondary hydroxyl of malate is oxidized to a ketone, regenerating **oxaloacetate**.
2. Although the equilibrium **favors malate**, the reaction is pulled forward by the rapid consumption of OAA in Step 1 (citrate synthase).
3. OAA is now ready to accept another acetyl-CoA, completing the cycle.

**Energy harvested:** $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} \approx +7$ kcal/mol (endergonic in isolation, but driven by Step 1). **1 NADH (≈ 2.5 ATP)**.

## 3.1 Consolidated Energy Tally (per acetyl-CoA)

| **Step** | **Reduced Coenzyme / GTP** | **ATP Equivalent** |
|---|---|---|
| 3 (ICDH) | 1 NADH | $2.5$ |
| 4 (α-KGDH) | 1 NADH | $2.5$ |
| 5 (SCS) | 1 GTP | $1.0$ |
| 6 (SDH) | 1 FADH₂ | $1.5$ |
| 8 (MDH) | 1 NADH | $2.5$ |
| **Total** | **3 NADH + 1 FADH₂ + 1 GTP** | **$\mathbf{10}$** |

## 3.2 Verification via Free-Energy Sum

Summing the standard free energies of the reduced coenzymes and GTP bond energies:

$$\Delta G_{\text{harvested}} = 3(-52.6) + (-43.4) + (-7.3) \approx -211 \text{ kcal/mol}$$

The remaining $\sim 7$ kcal/mol of the cycle's net $\Delta G$ is dissipated as entropy, accounting for the slight negative drift that keeps the cycle running.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — TCA Cycle Flow

The diagram below maps the **eight sequential transformations**, the coenzymes produced, and the sites of CO₂ release. It uses purely alphanumeric node IDs and clean uppercase labels as per protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["ACETYL COA 2C ENTERS"]
    A1["STEP 1 CITRATE SYNTHASE OAA 4C PLUS ACETYL COA YIELDS CITRATE 6C PLUS COA"]
    A2["STEP 2 ACONITASE CITRATE CONVERTS TO ISOCITRATE VIA CIS ACONITATE"]
    A3["STEP 3 ICDH ISOCITRATE TO ALPHA KG 5C RELEASES CO2 PRODUCES NADH"]
    A4["STEP 4 ALPHA KGDH ALPHA KG TO SUCCINYL COA RELEASES CO2 PRODUCES NADH"]
    A5["STEP 5 SUCCINYL COA SYNTHETASE SUCCINYL COA TO SUCCINATE PRODUCES GTP"]
    A6["STEP 6 SDH COMPLEX II SUCCINATE TO FUMARATE PRODUCES FADH2"]
    A7["STEP 7 FUMARASE FUMARATE PLUS H2O TO L MALATE"]
    A8["STEP 8 MALATE DEHYDROGENASE MALATE TO OAA PRODUCES NADH"]
    A9["OAA REGENERATED 4C READY FOR NEXT TURN"]

    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A1
```

## 4.2 Energy Carrier Distribution Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["STEP 3 ICDH"] -- NADH --> POOL["REDUCED COENZYME POOL 3 NADH PLUS 1 FADH2"]
    P2["STEP 4 ALPHA KGDH"] -- NADH --> POOL
    P3["STEP 6 SDH"] -- FADH2 --> POOL
    P4["STEP 5 SCS"] -- GTP --> ATP["ATP EQUIVALENT 10 ATP PER ACETYL COA"]
    POOL --> ETC["ELECTRON TRANSPORT CHAIN COMPLEXES I AND II"]
    ETC --> OXPHOS["OXIDATIVE PHOSPHORYLATION"]
    OXPHOS --> ATP
```

## 4.3 Regulation Network (Allosteric Control)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ENZ1["ENZYME CITRATE SYNTHASE"]
    ENZ2["ENZYME ISOCITRATE DEHYDROGENASE"]
    ENZ3["ENZYME ALPHA KGDH"]

    ACT1["ACTIVATORS CA2 PLUS ADP PLUS ACETYL COA"] --> ENZ1
    ACT2["ACTIVATORS CA2 PLUS ADP"] --> ENZ2
    ACT3["ACTIVATORS CA2 PLUS COA"] --> ENZ3

    INH1["INHIBITORS ATP PLUS NADH PLUS CITRATE"] --> ENZ1
    INH2["INHIBITORS ATP PLUS NADH"] --> ENZ2
    INH3["INHIBITORS NADH PLUS SUCCINYL COA"] --> ENZ3

    ENZ1 --> R1["SLOW CYCLE WHEN ENERGY HIGH"]
    ENZ2 --> R1
    ENZ3 --> R1
    R1 --> M1["MITOCHONDRIAL ATP HOMEOSTASIS"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual reading tip:** The Mermaid architecture is a **Block-Level Functional Map** of interactions (per protocol Section 4 Diagram Fallback). It is deliberately schematic — KTU examiners reward labelled flow charts in answer scripts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]
**Define the TCA cycle. State its location, the entry substrate, and the overall products formed per single turn.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Tricarboxylic Acid (TCA) cycle**, also called the **Krebs cycle** or **citric acid cycle**, is a series of eight enzyme-catalysed reactions that occur in the **mitochondrial matrix** of eukaryotes. Its entry substrate is the **2-carbon acetyl group of acetyl-CoA**, derived from pyruvate, fatty acid β-oxidation, or amino acid catabolism (1 mark).

In one turn, the cycle oxidises one acetyl-CoA to yield **2 CO₂, 3 NADH, 1 FADH₂, and 1 GTP (≈ 1 ATP)**, while regenerating oxaloacetate (1 mark).

The overall equation is:

$$\text{Acetyl-CoA} + 3\,\text{NAD}^+ + \text{FAD} + \text{GDP} + \text{P}_i + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + \text{CoA} + 3\,\text{NADH} + \text{FADH}_2 + \text{GTP} + 3\,\text{H}^+$$

(1 mark for stoichiometry).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]
**List any six enzymes of the TCA cycle and name the step they catalyse.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — any six correct pairs @ 0.5 each):**

| **Enzyme** | **Step Catalysed** |
|---|---|
| Citrate synthase | Acetyl-CoA + OAA → Citrate (Step 1) |
| Aconitase | Citrate → Isocitrate (Step 2) |
| Isocitrate dehydrogenase | Isocitrate → α-Ketoglutarate (Step 3) |
| α-Ketoglutarate dehydrogenase | α-Ketoglutarate → Succinyl-CoA (Step 4) |
| Succinyl-CoA synthetase | Succinyl-CoA → Succinate (Step 5) |
| Succinate dehydrogenase | Succinate → Fumarate (Step 6) |
| Fumarase | Fumarate → Malate (Step 7) |
| Malate dehydrogenase | Malate → Oxaloacetate (Step 8) |

(0.5 × 6 = 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Past Pattern]

#### (a) Describe in detail the eight sequential reactions of the TCA cycle. Name each intermediate, enzyme, and the products formed at every step. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Citrate Synthase (1 mark):**
Acetyl-CoA condenses with oxaloacetate (OAA) to form **citrate**, releasing CoA-SH. The reaction is driven by thioester hydrolysis and is highly exergonic.

$$\text{Acetyl-CoA} + \text{OAA} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Citrate} + \text{CoA-SH}$$

**Step 2 — Aconitase (0.5 mark):**
Citrate is reversibly isomerised to **isocitrate** via the intermediate *cis*-aconitate. Aconitase contains an Fe-S cluster.

**Step 3 — Isocitrate Dehydrogenase (1 mark):**
Isocitrate is oxidatively decarboxylated to **α-ketoglutarate**, releasing the **first CO₂** and producing **1 NADH**.

**Step 4 — α-Ketoglutarate Dehydrogenase Complex (1 mark):**
α-Ketoglutarate is oxidatively decarboxylated to **succinyl-CoA**, releasing the **second CO₂** and producing **1 NADH**. Uses five cofactors: TPP, lipoamide, CoA, FAD, NAD⁺.

**Step 5 — Succinyl-CoA Synthetase (1 mark):**
Succinyl-CoA is converted to **succinate**, with **substrate-level phosphorylation** of GDP → **GTP**.

**Step 6 — Succinate Dehydrogenase (1 mark):**
Succinate is oxidised to **fumarate**, producing **1 FADH₂**. The only membrane-bound TCA enzyme (Complex II). Inhibited by malonate.

**Step 7 — Fumarase (0.5 mark):**
Fumarate is hydrated to **L-malate** in a stereospecific anti-addition.

**Step 8 — Malate Dehydrogenase (1 mark):**
Malate is oxidised to **oxaloacetate**, producing **1 NADH** and completing the cycle.

> [!TIP]
> Use the mnemonic **"CIA IPS FAM"** for the 8 enzymes in order.

#### (b) Calculate the total ATP yield from the complete oxidation of one molecule of glucose through glycolysis, pyruvate dehydrogenase, and the TCA cycle. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

| **Stage** | **Reduced Coenzymes / GTP** | **ATP Yield** |
|---|---|---|
| Glycolysis (substrate-level ATP) | 2 ATP | 2 (1) |
| Glycolysis (cytosolic NADH, glycerol-phosphate shuttle) | 2 NADH | 3 (1) |
| Pyruvate dehydrogenase (2 pyruvate) | 2 NADH | 5 (1) |
| TCA cycle (per glucose, 2 turns): 6 NADH | 6 NADH | 15 (1) |
| TCA cycle: 2 FADH₂ | 2 FADH₂ | 3 (1) |
| TCA cycle: 2 GTP | 2 GTP | 2 (1) |
| **Total per glucose** | — | **30 ATP** |

**Conversion factors used:**

$$1\,\text{NADH} \rightarrow 2.5\,\text{ATP}, \quad 1\,\text{FADH}_2 \rightarrow 1.5\,\text{ATP}, \quad 1\,\text{GTP} = 1\,\text{ATP}$$

$$\text{Total} = 2 + 3 + 5 + 15 + 3 + 2 = 30\,\text{ATP per glucose}$$

(0.5 mark for final summation)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** If you use the **malate-aspartate shuttle** (heart/liver), cytosolic NADH yields **2.5 ATP** each, giving a **total of 32 ATP/glucose**. Always state the shuttle assumed. The glycerol-phosphate shuttle (skeletal muscle, brain) gives 30 ATP. KTU's "modern" value of **30–32 ATP** is acceptable, but specify the shuttle.

---

### QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Past Pattern]

#### (a) Explain the regulation of the TCA cycle, highlighting the three key regulatory enzymes, their activators, and inhibitors. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The TCA cycle is regulated at **three exergonic, irreversible steps** (1 mark):

**1. Citrate Synthase (Step 1) (2 marks):**
- **Activators:** Acetyl-CoA (substrate availability), Ca²⁺, ATP (at low concentrations, it primes the enzyme).
- **Inhibitors:** High [ATP], [NADH], [citrate], [succinyl-CoA] (product inhibition).
- Logic: When downstream energy is plentiful, the enzyme is turned off to prevent wasteful oxidation.

**2. Isocitrate Dehydrogenase (Step 3) (2 marks):**
- **Activators:** **ADP** (signals low energy), **Ca²⁺** (muscle contraction signal), NAD⁺.
- **Inhibitors:** **ATP**, **NADH** (high energy charge).
- Logic: ICDH is the **primary control point** in many tissues. Calcium-mediated activation links ATP demand (muscle contraction) directly to oxidative flux.

**3. α-Ketoglutarate Dehydrogenase (Step 4) (2 marks):**
- **Activators:** Ca²⁺, CoA, NAD⁺.
- **Inhibitors:** NADH, succinyl-CoA.
- Logic: Mechanism analogous to pyruvate dehydrogenase; the **NADH/NAD⁺ ratio** is the strongest signal. Arsenite inhibits by attacking the lipoamide cofactor.

**Overall Control Theme:** The cycle responds to the cellular **energy charge** ([ATP]/[ADP]) and **redox state** ([NADH]/[NAD⁺]). **Calcium** is the master "feed-forward" activator, signalling increased ATP demand in muscle.

#### (b) Discuss the amphibolic nature of the TCA cycle, providing two examples each of its anabolic and catabolic roles, and explain anaplerosis. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The term **amphibolic** (from Greek *amphi* = "both") was coined by **B. D. Davis (1961)** to describe the cycle's dual role in catabolism and anabolism (1 mark).

**Catabolic roles — energy harvesting (1.5 marks):**
1. Complete oxidation of acetyl-CoA to 2 CO₂.
2. Generation of 3 NADH, 1 FADH₂, 1 GTP per turn for ATP synthesis.

**Anabolic roles — precursor supply (2.5 marks):**

| **Intermediate Withdrawn** | **Biosynthetic Product** |
|---|---|
| α-Ketoglutarate | Glutamate → Proline, Arginine, GABA |
| Succinyl-CoA | Porphyrin (heme) ring |
| OAA | Aspartate → Asparagine, Pyrimidines, Lysine |
| Citrate (exported to cytosol) | Acetyl-CoA → Fatty acids, Cholesterol, Steroid hormones |
| Malate (exported) | NADPH via malic enzyme for biosynthesis |

**Anaplerosis (2 marks):**
Anaplerotic reactions replenish TCA intermediates drained for biosynthesis. The most important is:

$$\text{Pyruvate} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{ATP} \xrightarrow{\text{Pyruvate carboxylase (biotin)}} \text{Oxaloacetate} + \text{ADP} + \text{P}_i$$

Other anaplerotic enzymes include **PEP carboxykinase** (PEP → OAA), **transaminases** (glutamate → α-KG; aspartate → OAA), and the **glyoxylate shunt** (in plants and microbes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Anaplerosis must balance **cataplerosis** (withdrawal of intermediates). A cell that loses α-KG faster than it can replenish it develops a **cataplerotic crisis**, observed clinically in glutaric aciduria and several organic acidemias.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting CO₂ origin:** In the first turn, the two CO₂'s come from the OAA carbons, NOT the incoming acetyl carbons. Examiners specifically test this "label-tracing" point.
> 2. **Misidentifying FAD vs NAD⁺ in Step 6:** Succinate → fumarate uses FAD (not NAD⁺) because the oxidation potential is too low.
> 3. **Confusing GTP and ATP:** The TCA cycle makes **GTP**, not ATP directly, at Step 5.
> 4. **Omitting water stoichiometry:** Two H₂O are consumed (Steps 2 and 7) — frequently missed.
> 5. **Skipping the anaplerotic role:** Pure "energy" answers lose marks; always mention the amphibolic duality.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Cycle name:** Tricarboxylic Acid Cycle = Krebs Cycle = Citric Acid Cycle.
- ✅ **Location:** Mitochondrial matrix (eukaryotes), cytoplasm (prokaryotes).
- ✅ **Discoverer:** Sir Hans Krebs, 1937; Nobel Prize 1953.
- ✅ **Entry molecule:** Acetyl-CoA (2C) + Oxaloacetate (4C).
- ✅ **Total intermediates (8):** Citrate, Isocitrate, α-KG, Succinyl-CoA, Succinate, Fumarate, Malate, OAA.
- ✅ **Mnemonic sequence:** **"CIA IPS FAM"** — C **I**A (Isocitrate, Aconitase alternate) → I P S → F A M.
- ✅ **Irreversible steps:** 1, 3, 4 (the three regulatory sites).
- ✅ **CO₂ release:** Steps 3 and 4 (oxidative decarboxylations).
- ✅ **NADH release:** Steps 3, 4, 8 (total = 3).
- ✅ **FADH₂ release:** Step 6 only (total = 1).
- ✅ **GTP release:** Step 5 only (substrate-level phosphorylation).
- ✅ **H₂O consumption:** Steps 2 (Aconitase) and 7 (Fumarase); total 2 H₂O per turn.
- ✅ **Energy yield per acetyl-CoA:** $3(2.5) + 1(1.5) + 1(1) = 10$ ATP.
- ✅ **Energy yield per glucose:** **30 ATP** (glycerol-phosphate shuttle) or **32 ATP** (malate-aspartate shuttle).
- ✅ **Membrane-bound enzyme:** Succinate dehydrogenase (Complex II).
- ✅ **Fe-S cluster:** Aconitase and Succinate dehydrogenase.
- ✅ **Coenzymes used:** NAD⁺, FAD, TPP, lipoamide, CoA, biotin (anaplerotic).
- ✅ **Regulatory enzymes:** Citrate synthase, ICDH, α-KGDH.
- ✅ **Master activators:** Ca²⁺ (muscle signal), ADP.
- ✅ **Master inhibitors:** High [ATP], high [NADH].
- ✅ **Amphibolic intermediates:** α-KG, OAA, Succinyl-CoA, Citrate, Malate.
- ✅ **Anaplerotic enzyme:** **Pyruvate carboxylase** (biotin-dependent) replenishes OAA.
- ✅ **Glyoxylate shunt:** Variant of TCA in plants/bacteria; bypasss the two CO₂ steps to net-synthesize carbohydrates from fatty acids (absent in animals).
- ✅ **Fate of label (first turn):** Acetyl carbons of acetyl-CoA are NOT the ones released as CO₂ in the first turn — the OAA carbons are. Label appears as CO₂ only from the second turn onward.
- ✅ **Clinical correlations:** Arsenic poisoning (α-KGDH), cyanide/CO poisoning (ETC, indirect), thiamine deficiency (beri-beri, affects α-KGDH), SDH mutations (paragangliomas), fumarase deficiency (encephalomyopathy).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gluconeogenesis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT302 - BIOCHEMISTRY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gluconeogenesis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gluconeogenesis** (Greek: *glykys* = sweet, *neo* = new, *genesis* = creation) is the **metabolic pathway that results in the generation of glucose from certain non-carbohydrate carbon substrates.** It is an **anabolic, endergonic, cytosolic and mitochondrial process** that occurs primarily in the **liver (≈ 90%)** and to a lesser extent in the **renal cortex of the kidneys (≈ 10%)**, particularly during prolonged fasting, starvation, and intense exercise.

The non-carbohydrate precursors (also called **gluconeogenic substrates**) include:
- **Lactate** (produced anaerobically in skeletal muscle and RBCs)
- **Glycerol** (released from adipose tissue lipolysis)
- **Glucogenic amino acids** (e.g., alanine from muscle, glutamate, aspartate)
- **TCA cycle intermediates** (citrate, succinate, fumarate, malate, α-ketoglutarate)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Gluconeogenesis is *not* a simple reversal of glycolysis. The three highly exergonic, physiologically irreversible steps of glycolysis (catalyzed by **hexokinase, phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1), and pyruvate kinase**) must be circumvented by **four dedicated bypass reactions** that are unique to gluconeogenesis.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a city that runs entirely on solar power during the day, but needs electricity at night when the sun is gone. The city cannot simply "rewind" the daytime energy; it must build a **completely separate set of generators** that pull stored fuel (coal, gas, batteries) to keep the lights on.

**The human body works the same way:**
- **Glycolysis** is the "daytime" path — it breaks glucose down to make ATP fast.
- **During fasting or heavy exercise**, the brain, RBCs, and renal medulla **still desperately need glucose** (they cannot oxidize fatty acids effectively).
- The body cannot simply run glycolysis in reverse because three of its steps release too much energy (they are like a waterfall that can only flow **downhill**).
- So, evolution produced **four specific "anti-waterfall" enzymes** that let the cell climb *up* the metabolic waterfall, using stored lactate, glycerol, and amino acids as raw materials.
- The cost? **Energy.** The cell must spend **6 high-energy phosphate bonds (4 ATP + 2 GTP) per glucose molecule** built, plus 2 NADH from the mitochondria.

> [!TIP]
> **Easy Mnemonic — "Pathway is PGFF"** (the four bypass enzymes): **P**yruvate carboxylase, **G** (PEPC**K** — GTP-using), **F**ructose-1,6-bisphosphatase, **G**lucose-6-phosphatase. Some students remember it as **"Please Get Food Quickly"** — **P**EPCK, **G**lucokinase-like step, **F**BPase, **G**6Pase.

---

### 1.3 Physiological Significance & Key Metrics

| Parameter | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| Primary site | **Liver (hepatocytes)** — **90%** of total flux |
| Secondary site | **Renal cortex** — **10%** of total flux (up-regulated in prolonged starvation) |
| Subcellular location | Mitochondria + Cytosol + Endoplasmic Reticulum (ER) |
| Total energy cost | **6 high-energy phosphates per glucose** (4 ATP + 2 GTP) + **2 NADH** |
| Daily glucose output (fasting) | **~ 6 – 10 g** from kidneys, **~ 150 g** from liver |
| Hormonal activator | **Glucagon** (and cortisol, epinephrine) |
| Hormonal inhibitor | **Insulin** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Students often confuse **gluconeogenesis** with **glycogenolysis**. They are *not* the same. **Glycogenolysis = breaking down stored glycogen** to glucose-6-P. **Gluconeogenesis = synthesizing *new* glucose** from non-carbohydrate precursors. Only the *final step* (G6Pase) is shared.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core "Why" — Why Gluconeogenesis Cannot Be a Simple Glycolysis Reversal

Glycolysis contains **three physiologically irreversible steps** (each with a large negative ΔG under cellular conditions). The cell cannot reverse them because the energetic barrier is too steep.

| Step # | Glycolysis (Forward) | ΔG°′ (kJ/mol) | Why it cannot be reversed |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Glucose → Glucose-6-P (Hexokinase) | −16.7 | Phosphoryl-transfer is essentially unidirectional |
| 3 | Fructose-6-P → Fructose-1,6-BP (PFK-1) | −14.2 | Largest regulatory step of glycolysis |
| 10 | PEP → Pyruvate (Pyruvate Kinase) | −23.9 | Substrate-level phosphorylation releases too much energy |

> **Engineering Analogy:** Think of these steps as **check-valves in a water pipe**. Water can only flow forward. To pump water backward, you need a *separate, powered pump* at each check-valve. The four gluconeogenic bypass enzymes are those powered pumps.

---

### 2.2 The Four Bypass Reactions (The "Heart" of Gluconeogenesis)

#### Bypass 1: Pyruvate → Phosphoenolpyruvate (Two Enzyme Steps)

This is the most energy-intensive bypass and uses two mitochondrial / cytosolic enzymes.

**Step A — Mitochondrial Matrix:**
$$\text{Pyruvate} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{ATP} \xrightarrow{\text{Pyruvate Carboxylase (PC)}} \text{Oxaloacetate} + \text{ADP} + \text{P}_i$$

- **Cofactor required:** Biotin (vitamin B7) — acts as a mobile CO₂ carrier.
- **Allosteric activator:** Acetyl-CoA (mandatory activator; signals "fuel is abundant").
- **Mnemonic:** **"PC needs Acetyl-CoA to wake up."**

**Step B — Mitochondrial Matrix and/or Cytosol:**
$$\text{Oxaloacetate} + \text{GTP} \xrightarrow{\text{PEPCK}} \text{PEP} + \text{GDP} + \text{CO}_2$$

- The CO₂ fixed in Step A is **released back** in Step B (this is why the pathway is energetically expensive — the cell effectively "spends" ATP to activate and then release CO₂).
- PEPCK exists in two isoforms: **mitochondrial (mPEPCK)** and **cytosolic (cPEPCK)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Oxaloacetate exported to the cytosol?** Because PEP must be formed in the cytosol for the rest of gluconeogenesis to proceed. The oxaloacetate cannot cross the inner mitochondrial membrane directly, so it is reduced to **malate** by mitochondrial malate dehydrogenase, exported via the **malate-aspartate shuttle**, and re-oxidized to oxaloacetate in the cytosol.

#### Bypass 2: Fructose-1,6-Bisphosphate → Fructose-6-Phosphate

$$\text{Fructose-1,6-BP} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Fructose-1,6-Bisphosphatase (FBPase-1)}} \text{Fructose-6-P} + \text{P}_i$$

- This is a simple **hydrolytic (exergonic) dephosphorylation**.
- Allosterically **inhibited by AMP and Fructose-2,6-BP** (the most potent inhibitor).
- Allosterically **activated by ATP and citrate** (signals "energy is high, build sugar").

#### Bypass 3: Glucose-6-Phosphate → Glucose

$$\text{Glucose-6-P} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{Glucose-6-Phosphatase (G6Pase)}} \text{Glucose} + \text{P}_i$$

- **G6Pase is anchored in the ER membrane**, with its active site facing the lumen.
- This is the *only* enzyme that can produce **free glucose** for export into the blood.
- Tissues lacking G6Pase (muscle, brain) **cannot release free glucose** into circulation.

---

### 2.3 Complete Stoichiometric Summary

**Overall Balanced Equation of Gluconeogenesis (starting from 2 Pyruvate):**

$$2\,\text{Pyruvate} + 4\,\text{ATP} + 2\,\text{GTP} + 2\,\text{NADH} + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Glucose} + 4\,\text{ADP} + 2\,\text{GDP} + 6\,\text{P}_i + 2\,\text{NAD}^+ + 2\,\text{H}^+$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ΔG°′ for the overall reaction ≈ +47 kJ/mol** (highly endergonic), confirming gluconeogenesis requires continuous ATP/GTP input.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Reaction / Concept | Equation / Key Value | Cofactor / Energy | Regulation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pyruvate → Oxaloacetate | Pyr + CO₂ + ATP → OAA + ADP + Pi | Biotin, ATP | **Activated by Acetyl-CoA** |
| 2 | Oxaloacetate → PEP | OAA + GTP → PEP + GDP + CO₂ | GTP | Induced by glucagon/cortisol |
| 3 | F-1,6-BP → F-6-P | Hydrolysis (Pi released) | H₂O | **Inhibited by F-2,6-BP & AMP** |
| 4 | G-6-P → Glucose | Hydrolysis (Pi released) | H₂O | Induced during fasting |
| **5** | **Total ATP/GTP per glucose** | **4 ATP + 2 GTP + 2 NADH** | 6 high-energy bonds | — |
| 6 | Reversal of Glycolysis ΔG°′ | **+47 kJ/mol** | Endergonic | — |
| 7 | Site of gluconeogenesis | Liver (90 %) + Kidney (10 %) | — | Glucagon ↑ in fasting |

> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Understanding gluconeogenesis is foundational for designing **parenteral nutrition formulations** in clinical medicine, **managing Type-2 diabetes** (where excessive hepatic gluconeogenesis causes fasting hyperglycemia), and **bioprocess engineering** of microbial systems for bioethanol and recombinant protein production.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Mapping of Gluconeogenesis (from 2 Pyruvate)

The pathway is **not simply "glycolysis in reverse"**. The reaction sequence is:

| Step | Substrate | Product | Enzyme | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1a | Pyruvate | Oxaloacetate | **Pyruvate Carboxylase** | Mitochondrial; uses ATP + CO₂ |
| 1b | Oxaloacetate | Malate | Mitochondrial MDH | Shuttle to cytosol |
| 1c | Malate | Oxaloacetate | Cytosolic MDH | Uses NAD⁺ → NADH |
| 1d | Oxaloacetate | **PEP** | **PEPCK** | Uses GTP; releases CO₂ |
| 2 | PEP | 2-Phosphoglycerate | Enolase (reversible) | Releases H₂O |
| 3 | 2-Phosphoglycerate | 3-Phosphoglycerate | PGM (reversible) | — |
| 4 | 3-Phosphoglycerate | 1,3-Bisphosphoglycerate | **Phosphoglycerate Kinase** | Uses ATP; makes NADH-equivalent (substrate-level) |
| 5 | 1,3-BPG | Glyceraldehyde-3-P | **G3P Dehydrogenase** | Uses NADH + Pi |
| 6 | G3P | 1,3-BPG (×2) | Aldolase + TPI | Triose-P isomerase step |
| 7 | Fructose-1,6-BP | **Fructose-6-P** | **Fructose-1,6-BPase** | Hydrolytic; **second bypass** |
| 8 | Fructose-6-P | Glucose-6-P | PGI (reversible) | — |
| 9 | **Glucose-6-P** | **Glucose** | **Glucose-6-Phosphatase** | **Third bypass**; ER-luminal |

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Energy Accounting per Glucose Molecule

Let us derive the **total ATP/GTP cost** mathematically.

**Step 1 (Pyruvate → Oxaloacetate):** occurs **twice per glucose** (since 2 pyruvates are needed for 1 glucose).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_{\text{Step 1}} &= 2 \times 1\,\text{ATP} = 2\,\text{ATP} \\
\text{(because one ATP is consumed per pyruvate carboxylase reaction)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (Oxaloacetate → PEP):** occurs **twice per glucose**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_{\text{Step 2}} &= 2 \times 1\,\text{GTP} = 2\,\text{GTP} \\
\text{(because one GTP is consumed per PEPCK reaction)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (3-Phosphoglycerate → 1,3-BPG):** occurs **twice per glucose** in the cytosol.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_{\text{Step 3}} &= 2 \times 1\,\text{ATP} = 2\,\text{ATP} \\
\text{(one ATP equivalent used to make the high-energy 1,3-BPG intermediate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**NADH consumption in Step 5 (G3P Dehydrogenase):** occurs **twice per glucose**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_{\text{Step 5}} &= 2 \times 1\,\text{NADH} = 2\,\text{NADH} \\
\text{(NADH is consumed in the cytosol; if shuttled from mitochondria via malate, 1 NADH = 3 ATP equiv.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cost} &= \text{ATP} + \text{GTP} + \text{NADH} \\
&= (2 + 2)\,\text{ATP} + 2\,\text{GTP} + 2\,\text{NADH} \\
&= \mathbf{4\,ATP + 2\,GTP + 2\,NADH} \\
&= \mathbf{6\,high-energy\,phosphate\,bonds\,per\,glucose}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment on the equation:** Note that although we use 2 NADH, these are produced in the cytosol by the malate dehydrogenase reaction. The *net* new NADH requirement is 2. In some textbooks the cost is stated as "4 ATP + 2 GTP + 2 NADH" where 1 NADH is considered equivalent to 3 ATP via the electron transport chain.

---

### 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Glycolysis vs. Gluconeogenesis

| Parameter | Glycolysis | Gluconeogenesis |
|---|---|---|
| **Overall direction** | Glucose → 2 Pyruvate | 2 Pyruvate → Glucose |
| **ΔG°′ (kJ/mol)** | −85 (exergonic) | **+47** (endergonic) |
| **ATP produced/consumed** | 2 ATP produced (net) | **6 high-energy bonds consumed** |
| **Rate-limiting step** | PFK-1 (step 3) | **FBPase-1 / PEPCK / G6Pase triad** |
| **Key regulatory molecule** | Fructose-2,6-BP (activates) | **Fructose-2,6-BP (inhibits FBPase-1)** |
| **Hormonal activation** | Insulin | **Glucagon, Cortisol, Epinephrine** |
| **Tissue location** | All tissues | **Liver (90 %) + Kidney (10 %)** |
| **Compartments** | Cytosol only | Mitochondria + Cytosol + ER |
| **Subcellular shuttle required** | No | **Yes — Malate-Aspartate Shuttle** |

---

### 3.4 Regulation: Allosteric and Hormonal Crossover

| Enzyme | Allosteric Activator | Allosteric Inhibitor | Hormonal Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pyruvate Carboxylase | **Acetyl-CoA** | ADP | Induced by glucagon (long-term) |
| Fructose-1,6-Bisphosphatase | ATP, Citrate | **AMP, Fructose-2,6-BP** | Glucagon ↑ transcription |
| Glucose-6-Phosphatase | — | — | Glucagon induces expression |
| PEPCK | — | — | **Strongly induced by glucagon & cortisol**; **suppressed by insulin** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fructose-2,6-Bisphosphate (F-2,6-BP)** is the master **reciprocal regulator**: it **activates PFK-1 (glycolysis)** and **inhibits FBPase-1 (gluconeogenesis)**, ensuring the two pathways never run simultaneously in a futile cycle. Its level is controlled by the bifunctional enzyme **PFK-2 / FBPase-2**, which is hormonally regulated by the **glucagon/insulin ratio via PKA phosphorylation**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Overall Gluconeogenesis Pathway (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PyrA["Pyruvate Mitochondria"] --> PC1["Pyruvate Carboxylase Biotin ATP required"]
    PC1 --> OAA1["Oxaloacetate Mitochondrial"]
    OAA1 --> MDHm["Mitochondrial Malate Dehydrogenase NADH consumed"]
    MDHm --> MAL["Malate Exported via Malate Aspartate Shuttle"]
    MAL --> MDHc["Cytosolic Malate Dehydrogenase NADH produced"]
    MDHc --> OAA2["Oxaloacetate Cytosolic"]
    OAA2 --> PEPCK1["PEPCK GTP consumed CO2 released"]
    PEPCK1 --> PEPnode["Phosphoenolpyruvate PEP Cytosolic"]
    PEPnode --> ENO["Enolase"]
    ENO --> PG2["2 Phosphoglycerate"]
    PG2 --> PGM["Phosphoglycerate Mutase"]
    PGM --> PG3["3 Phosphoglycerate"]
    PG3 --> PGK["Phosphoglycerate Kinase ATP consumed"]
    PGK --> BPG["1 3 Bisphosphoglycerate"]
    BPG --> G3PDH["G3P Dehydrogenase NADH consumed Pi released"]
    G3PDH --> G3Pnode["Glyceraldehyde 3 Phosphate"]
    G3Pnode --> ALDO["Aldolase"]
    ALDO --> FBP["Fructose 1 6 Bisphosphate"]
    FBP --> FBPase1["Fructose 1 6 Bisphosphatase Hydrolysis Bypass 2"]
    FBPase1 --> F6P["Fructose 6 Phosphate"]
    F6P --> PGI["Phosphoglucose Isomerase"]
    PGI --> G6P["Glucose 6 Phosphate"]
    G6P --> G6Pase1["Glucose 6 Phosphatase ER membrane Bypass 3"]
    G6Pase1 --> GLUC["Free Glucose Released to Blood"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Four Bypass Reactions Only (High-Yield Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Glycolysis["Glycolysis Irreversible Steps Cannot Reverse"]
        G1["Glucose → G6P Hexokinase"]
        G2["F6P → F1 6BP PFK1"]
        G3["PEP → Pyruvate Pyruvate Kinase"]
    end
    subgraph GNG["Gluconeogenesis Four Bypass Pumps"]
        B1["Bypass 1A Pyruvate Carboxylase Pyruvate → OAA"]
        B2["Bypass 1B PEPCK OAA → PEP"]
        B3["Bypass 2 FBPase1 F1 6BP → F6P"]
        B4["Bypass 3 G6Pase G6P → Glucose"]
    end
    G1 -.blocked.-> B4
    G2 -.blocked.-> B3
    G3 -.blocked.-> B1
    B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Regulation Crossover (Substrate Cycling)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GLU["Glucagon binds GPCR → PKA activated"] --> PKA["Phosphorylates PFK2 FBPase2"]
    PKA --> F26down["Fructose 2 6 BP DECREASES"]
    F26down --> GNGup["FBPase1 released from inhibition → GNG UP"]
    F26down --> Glycdown["PFK1 less active → Glycolysis DOWN"]
    INS["Insulin receptor → PKB/Akt"] --> F26up["Fructose 2 6 BP INCREASES"]
    F26up --> Glycup["PFK1 active → Glycolysis UP"]
    F26up --> GNGdown["FBPase1 inhibited → GNG DOWN"]
```

### 4.4 Block Architecture: The Cori Cycle (Lactate-Glucose Recycling)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MUSCLE["Skeletal Muscle Anaerobic"] --> LAC["Lactate Produced"]
    LAC --> BLOOD["Bloodstream to Liver"]
    BLOOD --> LIV1["Liver Hepatocyte"]
    LIV1 --> LIV2["Pyruvate via Lactate Dehydrogenase"]
    LIV2 --> GNGnode["Gluconeogenesis Energy 6 Pi bonds"]
    GNGnode --> GLUCnode["Free Glucose"]
    GLUCnode --> BLOOD2["Released to Bloodstream"]
    BLOOD2 --> MUSCLE
    BLOOD2 --> BRAIN["Brain and RBCs obligate glucose consumers"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define gluconeogenesis. Name any three glucogenic precursors and the organ in which it predominantly occurs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Gluconeogenesis is the **metabolic pathway for the synthesis of glucose from non-carbohydrate precursors**. It is essential during **fasting, prolonged starvation, and strenuous exercise** to maintain blood glucose homeostasis for the brain, RBCs, and renal medulla.

> The three glucogenic precursors are:
> 1. **Lactate** (from anaerobic glycolysis in muscle and RBCs)
> 2. **Glycerol** (from triglyceride hydrolysis in adipose tissue)
> 3. **Glucogenic amino acids** (especially alanine from muscle protein catabolism)

> It occurs predominantly in the **liver (hepatocytes)** and to a lesser extent (~10%) in the **kidney (renal cortex)**.

> *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for precursors, 1 mark for site.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is gluconeogenesis not a simple reversal of glycolysis? Name the four key enzymes unique to gluconeogenesis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Glycolysis contains **three physiologically irreversible, highly exergonic steps** catalyzed by hexokinase, phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1), and pyruvate kinase. The cell cannot thermodynamically reverse these reactions.

> Therefore, four distinct enzymes (the "bypass enzymes") catalyze alternate reactions to circumvent these irreversible steps:

| # | Bypass Enzyme | Reaction |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pyruvate Carboxylase** | Pyruvate → Oxaloacetate |
| 2 | **PEP Carboxykinase (PEPCK)** | Oxaloacetate → PEP |
| 3 | **Fructose-1,6-Bisphosphatase** | F-1,6-BP → F-6-P |
| 4 | **Glucose-6-Phosphatase** | G-6-P → Glucose |

> *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for explaining the 3 irreversible steps, 1 mark for naming the first two bypass enzymes, 1 mark for naming the last two.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU ESE 2024):** Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) Describe in detail the four bypass reactions of gluconeogenesis. Highlight the energetics and cofactors involved. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Bypass 1: Pyruvate → Oxaloacetate (Mitochondria)**
- **Enzyme:** Pyruvate Carboxylase (PC)
- **Reaction:** Pyruvate + CO₂ + ATP → Oxaloacetate + ADP + Pᵢ
- **Cofactor:** **Biotin** (vitamin B7) — covalently attached to a lysine residue; acts as a mobile carboxyl carrier.
- **Allosteric regulation:** Strongly **activated by Acetyl-CoA**; inhibited by ADP.
- **Significance:** Acetyl-CoA acts as a signal that fatty acid oxidation is active and oxaloacetate is needed for gluconeogenesis.

**Bypass 2: Oxaloacetate → PEP (Mitochondria / Cytosol)**
- **Enzyme:** PEP Carboxykinase (PEPCK)
- **Reaction:** Oxaloacetate + GTP → PEP + GDP + CO₂
- **The CO₂ added in Bypass 1 is released in Bypass 2** — making the overall process energetically expensive (4 ATP + 2 GTP per glucose).
- **Isoforms:** Mitochondrial (mPEPCK) and Cytosolic (cPEPCK).
- **Hormonal control:** **Induced by glucagon and cortisol**; repressed by insulin.

**Bypass 3: Fructose-1,6-BP → Fructose-6-P (Cytosol)**
- **Enzyme:** Fructose-1,6-Bisphosphatase (FBPase-1)
- **Reaction:** F-1,6-BP + H₂O → F-6-P + Pᵢ (hydrolytic cleavage)
- **Allosteric regulators:** Activated by **ATP and citrate**; inhibited by **AMP and Fructose-2,6-BP** (the master reciprocal regulator).

**Bypass 4: Glucose-6-P → Free Glucose (Endoplasmic Reticulum)**
- **Enzyme:** Glucose-6-Phosphatase (G6Pase)
- **Reaction:** G-6-P + H₂O → Glucose + Pᵢ
- **Location:** Active site faces the **lumen of the smooth ER**.
- **Tissue specificity:** Present in **liver and kidney cortex only** — muscle and brain lack this enzyme, which is why they cannot release free glucose into the blood.

> *(Total 7 marks: ~1.5 marks per bypass enzyme with cofactor and regulation.)*

---

**(b) Explain the energy balance and overall stoichiometry of gluconeogenesis starting from 2 pyruvate molecules. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The net reaction for the synthesis of one glucose molecule from 2 pyruvate is:

$$2\,\text{Pyruvate} + 4\,\text{ATP} + 2\,\text{GTP} + 2\,\text{NADH} + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Glucose} + 4\,\text{ADP} + 2\,\text{GDP} + 6\,\text{P}_i + 2\,\text{NAD}^+ + 2\,\text{H}^+$$

**Energetic cost breakdown:**

| Step | Energy Used |
|---|---|
| Pyruvate carboxylase (×2) | 2 ATP |
| PEPCK (×2) | 2 GTP |
| Phosphoglycerate kinase (×2) | 2 ATP |
| G3P Dehydrogenase (×2) | 2 NADH |
| **Total** | **4 ATP + 2 GTP + 2 NADH = 6 high-energy bonds + 2 NADH** |

**Note on NADH origin:**
- The 2 NADH used in the cytosolic G3P dehydrogenase step are produced by the cytosolic malate dehydrogenase, which oxidizes the malate shuttled out of the mitochondria.
- If 1 NADH is taken as 3 ATP equivalent, the *bioenergetic* cost ≈ **6 + 6 = 12 ATP equivalents**, but in canonical biochemistry count, it is recorded as **4 ATP + 2 GTP + 2 NADH**.

**Comparison with glycolysis:** Glycolysis yields a net of 2 ATP per glucose, so gluconeogenesis consumes **3× the energy** of the ATP produced by glycolysis for the same molecule.

> *(7 marks: 2 marks equation, 2 marks step-wise cost, 2 marks NADH explanation, 1 mark comparison.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a) With a neat labelled diagram, describe the Cori cycle and explain its biological significance. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The Cori Cycle (Glucose-Lactate Cycle):**

It is a **cooperative metabolic cycle** between **skeletal muscle** and the **liver** that recycles lactate produced by anaerobic glycolysis back into glucose.

**Step 1:** During intense exercise, skeletal muscle performs anaerobic glycolysis, producing **pyruvate → lactate** via lactate dehydrogenase (LDH). This regenerates NAD⁺ to keep glycolysis running.

**Step 2:** Lactate diffuses into the **bloodstream** and is transported to the **liver**.

**Step 3:** In hepatocytes, lactate is converted back to **pyruvate** by liver LDH.

**Step 4:** Pyruvate enters **gluconeogenesis** (consuming 4 ATP + 2 GTP + 2 NADH per glucose).

**Step 5:** Newly synthesized **glucose** is released into the bloodstream.

**Step 6:** Glucose returns to the **muscle** to fuel continued contraction, and also supplies the **brain and RBCs** (which are obligate glucose consumers).

**Biological Significance:**
- Prevents **lactic acidosis** by clearing lactate from blood.
- Maintains **blood glucose homeostasis** during exercise.
- Allows muscle to function anaerobically while still "borrowing" glucose from the liver.
- The ATP cost is borne by the **liver**, which has oxidative capacity to spare it.

> *(7 marks: 4 marks diagram + steps, 2 marks biochemical conversions, 1 mark significance.)*

---

**(b) Discuss the hormonal and allosteric regulation of key gluconeogenic enzymes. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Enzyme | Allosteric Activator | Allosteric Inhibitor | Hormonal Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pyruvate Carboxylase | **Acetyl-CoA** | ADP | Induced by glucagon |
| FBPase-1 | ATP, Citrate | **F-2,6-BP, AMP** | Glucagon induces; insulin represses |
| G6Pase | — | — | Glucagon induces |
| PEPCK | — | — | **Strongly induced by glucagon + cortisol; repressed by insulin** |

**The master regulator — Fructose-2,6-Bisphosphate (F-2,6-BP):**
- F-2,6-BP is the most potent allosteric activator of **PFK-1** (glycolysis) and inhibitor of **FBPase-1** (gluconeogenesis).
- Synthesized/degraded by the bifunctional enzyme **PFK-2 / FBPase-2**.
- In the **fed state (high insulin)**: dephosphorylated → PFK-2 active → F-2,6-BP ↑ → **glycolysis ↑, gluconeogenesis ↓**.
- In the **fasting state (high glucagon)**: PKA phosphorylates it → FBPase-2 active → F-2,6-BP ↓ → **gluconeogenesis ↑, glycolysis ↓**.

**Hormonal cascade:**
- **Glucagon / Epinephrine** → cAMP ↑ → PKA activated → phosphorylation cascade → transcription of PEPCK, FBPase-1, G6Pase genes ↑.
- **Insulin** → PI3K / Akt pathway → FoxO1 phosphorylation (excluded from nucleus) → transcription of gluconeogenic genes ↓.
- **Cortisol** → glucocorticoid response elements → long-term induction of PEPCK and G6Pase.

> *(7 marks: 3 marks table, 2 marks F-2,6-BP, 2 marks hormonal cascades.)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make — and where marks are lost:**
> 1. **Confusing PEPCK with Pyruvate Carboxylase.** PEPCK uses **GTP** (not ATP) and is cytosolic/mitochondrial — remember **"PEPCK-GTP"**.
> 2. **Forgetting the NADH cost.** Many students write only "6 ATP per glucose" — the **2 NADH** is also consumed and must be mentioned.
> 3. **Mixing up G6Pase with Hexokinase.** Hexokinase **phosphorylates** glucose; G6Pase **dephosphorylates** G6P — opposite reactions, different compartments.
> 4. **Saying "gluconeogenesis occurs in muscle."** WRONG. G6Pase is **absent in muscle** — muscle cannot release free glucose.
> 5. **Writing "2 ATP per glucose for gluconeogenesis."** The cost is **4 ATP + 2 GTP** (6 high-energy phosphates), not 2.
> 6. **Forgetting the malate-aspartate shuttle** when explaining oxaloacetate transport from mitochondria to cytosol.
> 7. **Writing the reverse ΔG°′ value** (−47 instead of +47 kJ/mol) — gluconeogenesis is **endergonic**, not exergonic.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Gluconeogenesis = synthesis of new glucose from non-carbohydrate precursors.**
- ✅ **Site:** Liver (90%) + Kidney cortex (10%) — NOT muscle or brain.
- ✅ **Compartments:** Mitochondria → Cytosol → Smooth ER (G6Pase in ER lumen).
- ✅ **Four bypass enzymes:** **Pyruvate Carboxylase, PEPCK, FBPase-1, G6Pase** — collectively the "PGFF" or "Pathway is PGFF."
- ✅ **Cofactor for Pyruvate Carboxylase:** **Biotin (vitamin B7).**
- ✅ **Pyruvate Carboxylase activator:** **Acetyl-CoA** (the molecular signal from β-oxidation).
- ✅ **Energy cost:** **4 ATP + 2 GTP + 2 NADH = 6 high-energy bonds per glucose.**
- ✅ **ΔG°′ of overall reaction:** **+47 kJ/mol** (endergonic).
- ✅ **Hormonal regulation:** **Glucagon, cortisol, epinephrine** induce; **insulin** represses (especially PEPCK and G6Pase).
- ✅ **Master reciprocal regulator:** **Fructose-2,6-Bisphosphate** — activates PFK-1 (glycolysis), inhibits FBPase-1 (gluconeogenesis).
- ✅ **Cori cycle:** Muscle lactate → liver glucose → muscle (recycles carbon and clears lactate).
- ✅ **Glucose-Alanine cycle:** Muscle alanine (from transamination of pyruvate) → liver glucose (parallel to Cori).
- ✅ **Fructose-1,6-Bisphosphatase is the rate-limiting gluconeogenic step** (in most texts) — tightly controlled.
- ✅ **Fasting state:** Glucagon dominates → cAMP ↑ → PKA active → PEPCK & FBPase-1 transcription ↑ → GNG up.
- ✅ **Clinical relevance:** Type 2 diabetes involves **excessive hepatic gluconeogenesis** → fasting hyperglycemia; drugs like **metformin** suppress GNG.
- ✅ **Mnemonic for precursors:** **"LGG"** — **L**actate, **G**lycerol, **G**lucogenic amino acids.
- ✅ **Acetyl-CoA cannot be converted to glucose** in animals (the pyruvate dehydrogenase reaction is irreversible, and TCA cycle loses 2 carbons as CO₂).
- ✅ **Substrate cycle (futile cycle):** Glycolysis and gluconeogenesis must not run simultaneously — F-2,6-BP ensures this reciprocal regulation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HMP Pathway
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT302 - BIOCHEMISTRY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** HMP Pathway ## 1.
