# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Crassulacean Acid Metabolism (CAM). Mention two key enzymes involved.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** CAM is a photosynthetic adaptation in which plants fix CO₂ at night into a 4-carbon acid (malate) via PEP carboxylase, store it in vacuoles, and release the CO₂ internally during the day to drive the Calvin–Benson cycle. This temporal separation minimizes water loss through closed daytime stomata.

**Enzymes (1 mark, name any two):**
1. **PEP carboxylase (PEPC)** — nocturnal CO₂ fixation.
2. **NADP-malic enzyme (NADP-ME)** — daytime decarboxylation.

*(Alternative valid answers: MDH, PPDK, Rubisco, carbonic anhydrase.)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is the Water Use Efficiency (WUE) of CAM plants higher than C3 plants?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

CAM plants open their stomata only during the **cool, humid night**, when the **vapor pressure deficit (VPD)** between leaf and atmosphere is low. This drastically reduces **transpirational water loss**. In contrast, C3 plants keep stomata open during the hot daytime, losing large amounts of water. Since CO₂ fixation per unit water transpired is the definition of WUE, CAM plants achieve a WUE that is **3 to 6 times greater** than that of C3 plants, making them ecologically dominant in arid zones.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **four temporal phases** of the CAM pathway with emphasis on the enzymes and metabolites involved.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare the **energy costs** of CAM and C3 photosynthesis. Why does CAM have a higher ATP requirement?

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

| Phase | Time | Stomata | Key Enzyme | Key Metabolite |
|-------|------|---------|-----------|----------------|
| I — Night fixation | Sunset–Sunrise | Open | PEPC, MDH | Malate (vacuolar) |
| II — Dawn transition | Early morning | Closing | PEPC + Rubisco | Both pathways |
| III — Day decarboxylation | Midday | Closed | NADP-ME, Rubisco, PPDK | Pyruvate, 3-PGA |
| IV — Dusk transition | Late afternoon | Reopening | Rubisco | Sugars |

**Phase I:** At night, CO₂ is hydrated to HCO₃⁻ and fixed by **PEP carboxylase** into oxaloacetate, which is reduced to **malate** by **malate dehydrogenase**. Malate accumulates in the vacuole to concentrations of up to **200 mM**, causing leaf acidity to peak at dawn.

**Phase II:** As light intensity increases, stomata begin to close. Both PEPC and Rubisco operate, marking the transition.

**Phase III:** With stomata closed, vacuolar malate is released and decarboxylated by **NADP-malic enzyme**, releasing CO₂ for the **Calvin cycle** (Rubisco). Pyruvate is converted back to PEP by **PPDK** at the cost of ATP. **(3 marks)**

**Phase IV:** Toward evening, vacuolar malate is depleted. External CO₂ is again fixed directly by Rubisco as stomata reopen.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**C3 plant (per CO₂ fixed):**

$$ \text{3 ATP} + \text{2 NADPH} \rightarrow 1 \text{ CO}_2 \rightarrow \tfrac{1}{6} \text{ glucose} $$

**CAM plant (per CO₂ fixed):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Calvin cycle:} & \; 3 \text{ ATP} + 2 \text{ NADPH} \\
\text{PEP regeneration via PPDK:} & \; + 2 \text{ ATP (one ATP consumed as 2~P bonds since ATP} \rightarrow \text{AMP)} \\
\hline
\text{Total:} & \; 5 \text{ ATP} + 2 \text{ NADPH per CO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reason for higher ATP demand (4 marks):**
1. **PEP regeneration:** Each night, malate is decarboxylated to pyruvate, which must be converted back to PEP via PPDK. This consumes an extra ATP equivalent (since ATP → AMP = 2 high-energy phosphate bonds).
2. **Vacuolar transport:** Active transport of malate into and out of the vacuole requires **ATP-driven tonoplast H⁺-pumps** that establish the **proton gradient** used by malate antiporters.
3. **No spatial optimization:** Unlike C4, CAM cannot concentrate CO₂ spatially, so it pays both an **energy** and a **time** cost.

**Conclusion:** The ATP penalty is the **evolutionary trade-off** CAM plants pay for survival in water-limited habitats — higher energy cost is acceptable because water, not carbon, is the limiting resource.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **biochemical role of PEP carboxylase (PEPC)** in CAM plants. How is its activity regulated between day and night?

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **ecological and agricultural significance** of CAM plants with suitable examples.

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Biochemical Role (3 marks):**
PEPC catalyzes the **irreversible β-carboxylation** of PEP using HCO₃⁻ as the inorganic carbon substrate, yielding oxaloacetate and inorganic phosphate:

$$ \text{PEP} + \text{HCO}_3^- \xrightarrow{\text{PEPC, Mg}^{2+}} \text{Oxaloacetate} + \text{Pi} $$

In CAM plants, PEPC operates at night when stomata are open, capturing atmospheric CO₂ into a 4-carbon acid (malate) that can be safely stored without losing carbon to photorespiration.

**Day–Night Regulation (4 marks):**

| Regulator | Effect on PEPC |
|-----------|----------------|
| **Malate (high)** | Allosteric **inhibition** (competitive vs. PEP) |
| **Glucose-6-phosphate** | Allosteric **activation** |
| **Phosphorylation by PEPC-kinase** | Activates enzyme, **desensitizes to malate inhibition** |
| **Daytime dephosphorylation by PP2A** | Re-sensitizes to malate; enzyme suppressed |

At night, PEPC-kinase phosphorylates PEPC, allowing it to function even as malate levels rise. During the day, PEPC is dephosphorylated and becomes highly malate-sensitive — combined with declining cytoplasmic malate and increasing glucose-6-P, the enzyme is switched off, preventing futile cycling.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Ecological Significance (4 marks):**
1. **Arid-zone dominance:** CAM plants like *Opuntia ficus-indica* and *Agave americana* dominate deserts, where C3 plants cannot survive due to high transpiration rates.
2. **Epiphytic niche:** In tropical canopies, orchids and bromeliads use CAM to cope with intermittent water supply.
3. **Atmospheric CO₂ capture:** Nocturnal CO₂ uptake contributes to global carbon cycling in arid biomes (estimated ~**10–15%** of global terrestrial carbon fixation in arid regions).
4. **Climate resilience:** CAM species are predicted to expand under **climate-change-driven aridification**.

**Agricultural Significance (3 marks):**
1. ***Agave tequilana*** — source of tequila; produces **~15 tonnes/ha** biomass with minimal water input.
2. ***Ananas comosus*** (pineapple) — major tropical crop, exhibits **CAM cycle** improving drought tolerance.
3. ***Opuntia*** (prickly pear) — used as **livestock fodder** in drought-prone regions (Mexico, North Africa).
4. **Biofuel potential:** CAM crops are being engineered for **second-generation bioethanol** production on marginal lands without competing with food crops.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing PEPC and Rubisco substrates:** Rubisco uses **CO₂** directly (not HCO₃⁻); PEPC uses **HCO₃⁻** as substrate.
> 2. **Wrong product of decarboxylation:** Many students write "OAA + CO₂" instead of **pyruvate + CO₂** for the NADP-ME reaction.
> 3. **Energy cost error:** Forgetting that **PPDK converts ATP → AMP**, which is energetically equivalent to consuming **2 ATP**, not 1.
> 4. **Mixing C4 and CAM:** Writing "mesophyll–bundle sheath separation" for CAM is a **fatal conceptual error**. CAM operates in a **single cell type**.
> 5. **No mention of PEPC phosphorylation regulation:** Day-night regulation of PEPC is a **favorite 3-mark sub-question** in KTU exams.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — CAM Pathway:**
> - ⏰ **Temporal** separation (not spatial like C4).
> - 🌙 **Phase I (Night):** PEPC + MDH → Malate stored in vacuole.
> - ☀️ **Phase III (Day):** NADP-ME → CO₂ + Pyruvate; PPDK regenerates PEP.
> - 💧 **WUE:** 3–6× higher than C3 plants.
> - ⚡ **ATP cost:** 5 ATP + 2 NADPH per CO₂ (vs. 3 ATP + 2 NADPH for C3).
> - 🧪 **PEPC regulation:** Phosphorylation by PEPC-kinase at night; malate inhibition during day.
> - 🏜️ **Examples:** *Kalanchoe*, *Opuntia*, *Agave*, *Pineapple*, *Orchids*.
> - 📊 **Titratable acidity** peaks at dawn — diagnostic marker for CAM.
> - 🔬 **PPDK converts ATP → AMP + PPi** (extra ATP cost of 2 high-energy bonds).
> - 🌵 **Ecological role:** Survival in **arid**, **epiphytic**, and **drought-stressed** habitats.
